Understanding the class file format is essential because **everything the JVM knows about your program originates from the information stored in the `.class` file**.

pages.title: Phase 4: Class File Structure (.class Format)
pages.text:
# Java 21 JVM Masterclass

# **Module 1 ‚Äì JVM Fundamentals**

# **Phase 4 ‚Äì Class File Structure (.class Format)**

> **Goal of this phase**
>
> Understand what is stored inside a `.class` file, how the JVM reads it, and why every Java application depends on its structure.

This is one of the most important JVM internals topics. Every JVM implementation (HotSpot, OpenJ9, GraalVM, etc.) starts by reading the `.class` file format defined by the Java Virtual Machine Specification.

---

# Learning Objectives

After this phase, you'll understand:

* What a `.class` file is
* How `javac` creates it
* Internal structure of a `.class` file
* Constant Pool
* Fields and Methods
* Attributes
* Bytecode Instructions
* Why `0xCAFEBABE` exists
* How the JVM parses a class file

---

# 1. What is a Class File?

When we compile:

```java
public class Calculator {

    public int add(int a, int b){
        return a+b;
    }

}
```

using

```bash
javac Calculator.java
```

we get

```
Calculator.class
```

This `.class` file **does not contain machine code**.

It contains

* Bytecode
* Metadata
* Constants
* Field definitions
* Method definitions
* Class information

---

# 2. High-Level View

```
Calculator.java

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

      javac

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Calculator.class

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

      JVM
```

The JVM never reads `.java`.

It always reads `.class`.

---

# 3. Binary Structure of a Class File

A class file has a fixed format.

```
+--------------------------+
| Magic Number             |
+--------------------------+
| Version                  |
+--------------------------+
| Constant Pool            |
+--------------------------+
| Access Flags             |
+--------------------------+
| This Class               |
+--------------------------+
| Super Class              |
+--------------------------+
| Interfaces               |
+--------------------------+
| Fields                   |
+--------------------------+
| Methods                  |
+--------------------------+
| Attributes               |
+--------------------------+
```

Every Java class follows this layout.

---

# 4. Magic Number

Every valid Java class starts with

```
0xCAFEBABE
```

Example:

```
CA FE BA BE
```

Purpose:

The JVM immediately recognizes

> "This is a valid Java class file."

If the magic number is wrong

```
java.lang.ClassFormatError
```

is thrown.

---

## Why `CAFEBABE`?

James Gosling's team chose it as a memorable hexadecimal signature. It has **no technical meaning**, but it helps the JVM quickly recognize valid class files.

---

# 5. Version Information

Immediately after the magic number

```
Minor Version

Major Version
```

Example

Java 21

```
Major Version = 65
```

Common versions

| Java    | Major Version |
| ------- | ------------- |
| Java 8  | 52            |
| Java 11 | 55            |
| Java 17 | 61            |
| Java 21 | 65            |

If a Java 17 JVM tries to load a Java 21 class

```
UnsupportedClassVersionError
```

because it doesn't understand the newer class file format.

---

# 6. Constant Pool

The Constant Pool is the **heart of the class file**.

Think of it as a table containing everything the class references.

Example

```java
public class Calculator{

    public static void main(String[] args){

        System.out.println("Hello");
    }

}
```

Constant Pool contains entries like

```
Calculator

Object

System

PrintStream

println

Hello

java/lang/System

java/io/PrintStream
```

Instead of duplicating these values throughout the file, the JVM stores them once and references them by index.

---

## Why is it called a Pool?

Imagine:

```
String

‚Üì

Hello

Hello

Hello

Hello
```

Instead of storing `"Hello"` four times,

```
Constant Pool

‚Üì

#18 = Hello
```

The bytecode simply refers to entry `#18`.

This reduces class file size and improves efficiency.

---

# 7. Access Flags

These define the class modifiers.

Example

```java
public final class Employee
```

The Access Flags indicate:

```
PUBLIC

FINAL
```

Other examples:

* ABSTRACT
* INTERFACE
* ENUM
* RECORD
* ANNOTATION

The JVM uses these flags to enforce language rules.

---

# 8. This Class

Stores the current class name.

Example

```
Employee.class
```

contains

```
Employee
```

---

# 9. Super Class

Every Java class (except `Object`) stores its parent.

Example

```java
class Employee extends Person
```

```
Super Class

‚Üì

Person
```

If no superclass is declared,

```
java.lang.Object
```

is used automatically.

---

# 10. Interfaces

Suppose

```java
class Employee implements Serializable, Comparable<Employee>
```

The class file contains references to

```
Serializable

Comparable
```

---

# 11. Fields

Example

```java
private int age;

private String name;
```

Field information stored includes:

* Name
* Type descriptor
* Access modifiers
* Attributes

The JVM uses this information to create the object's layout later.

---

# 12. Methods

Example

```java
public int add(int a,int b){
    return a+b;
}
```

The class file stores:

* Method name
* Parameters
* Return type
* Bytecode
* Exception table
* Attributes

The actual implementation is stored as JVM bytecode instructions.

---

# 13. Bytecode Example

Java

```java
return a+b;
```

Bytecode

```
iload_1

iload_2

iadd

ireturn
```

Meaning:

```
Load variable 1

‚Üì

Load variable 2

‚Üì

Add integers

‚Üì

Return result
```

The JVM interpreter and JIT compiler understand these instructions.

---

# 14. Attributes

Attributes provide additional metadata.

Examples:

```
SourceFile

LineNumberTable

StackMapTable

Code

Exceptions

LocalVariableTable

BootstrapMethods

Record
```

These support debugging, verification, reflection, lambdas, records, and other JVM features.

---

# 15. Complete Class File Layout

```
Calculator.class

‚Üì

Magic Number

‚Üì

Version

‚Üì

Constant Pool

‚Üì

Access Flags

‚Üì

This Class

‚Üì

Super Class

‚Üì

Interfaces

‚Üì

Fields

‚Üì

Methods

‚Üì

Attributes
```

The Class Loader reads this structure sequentially.

---

# 16. How the JVM Reads a Class File

```
.class File

‚Üì

Read Magic Number

‚Üì

Read Version

‚Üì

Read Constant Pool

‚Üì

Read Class Information

‚Üì

Read Fields

‚Üì

Read Methods

‚Üì

Read Attributes

‚Üì

Create Internal Class Representation

‚Üì

Verification

‚Üì

Linking

‚Üì

Initialization
```

Only after these steps can the class be used.

---

# 17. Production Example

Suppose Spring Boot starts.

Thousands of class files are loaded.

For each class

```
.class

‚Üì

Read Constant Pool

‚Üì

Read Methods

‚Üì

Read Fields

‚Üì

Verification

‚Üì

Loaded
```

Large applications can load tens of thousands of classes during startup.

---

# 18. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå `.class` contains machine code.

**Reality:**
It contains JVM bytecode.

---

### ‚ùå Constant Pool stores only strings.

**Reality:**
It stores:

* Strings
* Numbers
* Class references
* Method references
* Field references
* Interface references
* Dynamic constants
* Bootstrap method references

---

### ‚ùå Bytecode is text.

**Reality:**
A `.class` file is a **binary** file. Tools such as `javap` present a human-readable disassembly.

---

### ‚ùå Class files are OS-specific.

**Reality:**
Class files are platform-independent. The JVM for each platform translates the bytecode into native instructions.

---

# 19. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What is a `.class` file?
2. Why does Java generate bytecode?
3. What is the Magic Number?
4. What is the Constant Pool?
5. Why is the Constant Pool needed?

---

## Intermediate

1. Explain the structure of a class file.
2. What is stored in the Methods section?
3. Why are Access Flags required?
4. What happens if the Magic Number is invalid?
5. How does the JVM read a class file?

---

## Architect-Level

**Question:**

A production deployment fails with:

```
UnsupportedClassVersionError
```

What does it mean?

A strong answer:

* The application was compiled with a newer Java version than the runtime JVM supports.
* Compare the class file's major version with the JVM version.
* Recompile with an appropriate `--release` target or upgrade the runtime JVM.
* Ensure build and deployment environments use compatible Java versions.

---

# Phase Summary

You should now understand:

* A `.class` file is a binary file containing JVM bytecode and metadata.
* Every class file starts with the `0xCAFEBABE` magic number.
* The Constant Pool stores reusable constants and symbolic references.
* The class file describes fields, methods, interfaces, and attributes.
* The JVM parses the class file in a defined order before linking and initialization.
* Platform independence comes from the standardized class file format, not from machine code.

---

# What's Next?

## **Module 1 ‚Äì Phase 5: Class Loading Mechanism**

This is one of the most important JVM topics.

We'll cover:

* What happens when the JVM loads a class
* Loading, Linking, and Initialization
* Lazy Class Loading
* When classes are loaded
* Dynamic loading
* Static blocks
* `Class.forName()`
* Spring Boot class loading
* Production examples
* Interview questions from Basic to Architect

This phase connects the **class file** we've just studied to the **runtime behavior** of the JVM.

pages.title: Phase 5: Class Loading Mechanism
pages.text:
# Java 21 JVM Masterclass

# **Module 1 ‚Äì JVM Fundamentals**

# **Phase 5 ‚Äì Class Loading Mechanism**

> **Goal of this phase**
>
> Understand **how a `.class` file becomes a live Java class inside the JVM**.

This is one of the **most important JVM topics**. Every Java application‚Äîfrom a simple `HelloWorld` to a large Spring Boot microservice‚Äîdepends on the Class Loading Mechanism.

---

# Learning Objectives

After this phase, you'll understand:

* What is Class Loading?
* Why Class Loading is needed
* The complete Class Loading Lifecycle
* Loading, Linking, Initialization
* Lazy Class Loading
* Dynamic Class Loading
* Active vs Passive Class Loading
* Production examples
* Common interview questions

---

# 1. What is Class Loading?

Suppose you have:

```java
public class Employee {
    private String name;
}
```

After compilation:

```text
Employee.java

‚Üì

javac

‚Üì

Employee.class
```

Question:

**Is `Employee.class` immediately usable by the JVM?**

**No.**

It is just a binary file stored on disk.

The JVM must:

* Read the file
* Validate it
* Create internal data structures
* Allocate metadata
* Make it available for execution

This entire process is called **Class Loading**.

---

# 2. Why Do We Need Class Loading?

Think of a library.

Books are stored on shelves.

```
Library

‚Üì

Book Shelf

‚Üì

Java Programming Book
```

You don't carry every book with you.

Instead,

You fetch it only when needed.

The JVM follows the same idea.

```
Disk

‚Üì

Employee.class

‚Üì

JVM loads only when needed
```

This saves:

* Memory
* Startup time
* CPU

---

# 3. Complete Class Loading Lifecycle

The JVM Specification defines three major stages.

```text
                Employee.class

                     ‚îÇ

                     ‚ñº

                Loading

                     ‚îÇ

                     ‚ñº

                 Linking

                     ‚îÇ

                     ‚ñº

              Initialization

                     ‚îÇ

                     ‚ñº

           Employee Class Ready
```

These three stages contain several internal steps.

---

# 4. Detailed Lifecycle

```text
Employee.class

‚Üì

Loading

‚Üì

Verification

‚Üì

Preparation

‚Üì

Resolution

‚Üì

Initialization

‚Üì

Execution
```

Notice:

Linking consists of:

* Verification
* Preparation
* Resolution

We'll study each in dedicated phases.

---

# 5. Step 1 ‚Äì Loading

The Class Loader performs:

```
Employee.class

‚Üì

Read Binary File

‚Üì

Create Class Object

‚Üì

Store Metadata
```

At this point,

The JVM knows:

* Class name
* Methods
* Fields
* Constant Pool
* Interfaces
* Superclass

The class is **loaded**, but **not initialized**.

---

# 6. What Happens During Loading?

Suppose:

```java
class Employee {
}
```

The JVM creates an internal representation.

```
Employee.class

‚Üì

ClassLoader

‚Üì

java.lang.Class<Employee>

‚Üì

Metaspace
```

This `Class<?>` object is what reflection APIs operate on.

Example:

```java
Class<?> clazz = Employee.class;
```

---

# 7. Step 2 ‚Äì Linking

Linking has three parts.

```
Verification

‚Üì

Preparation

‚Üì

Resolution
```

We'll cover these in depth in the next phases.

---

# 8. Step 3 ‚Äì Initialization

Only during initialization does the JVM execute:

* Static field initializers
* Static blocks

Example:

```java
class Employee {

    static {
        System.out.println("Employee Loaded");
    }

}
```

Output:

```
Employee Loaded
```

This happens **once per class loader**, when the class is initialized.

---

# 9. Active vs Passive Class Loading

This is a favorite interview topic.

## Active Loading

The JVM initializes the class.

Example:

```java
Employee emp = new Employee();
```

or

```java
Employee.test();
```

or

```java
Class.forName("Employee");
```

Initialization occurs.

---

## Passive Loading

Only class metadata is used.

Example:

```java
Employee.class
```

or

```java
Employee[] array = new Employee[10];
```

The class may be loaded, but it is **not necessarily initialized**.

---

# 10. Example

```java
class Test {

    static {
        System.out.println("Loaded");
    }

}
```

Example 1

```java
Test.class;
```

Output:

```
(no output)
```

Example 2

```java
new Test();
```

Output:

```
Loaded
```

Why?

`new` actively initializes the class.

---

# 11. Lazy Class Loading

Question:

Suppose your application has

```
5,000 classes
```

Should JVM load all 5,000 at startup?

No.

Instead,

```
Class A

Used

‚Üì

Load

Class B

Never Used

‚Üì

Not Loaded
```

This is called

**Lazy Class Loading**.

Benefits:

* Faster startup
* Lower memory usage
* Better scalability

---

# 12. Dynamic Class Loading

Classes can be loaded at runtime.

Example:

```java
Class.forName("com.example.Employee");
```

This enables:

* JDBC drivers
* Plugin architectures
* Spring Framework
* Hibernate
* Reflection
* Dependency Injection

Without dynamic loading, many modern Java frameworks would not be possible.

---

# 13. Production Example ‚Äì Spring Boot

Spring Boot starts.

```
Application Starts

‚Üì

Component Scan

‚Üì

Controller Classes

‚Üì

Service Classes

‚Üì

Repository Classes

‚Üì

Configuration Classes
```

Only the required classes are loaded and initialized.

Later, if an optional feature is never used, its classes may never be loaded.

---

# 14. Loading Example

```java
public class Main {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        Employee emp = new Employee();

    }

}
```

Execution Flow

```text
Main.class

‚Üì

Load Main

‚Üì

Initialize Main

‚Üì

main()

‚Üì

Need Employee?

‚Üì

Load Employee

‚Üì

Verify

‚Üì

Prepare

‚Üì

Resolve

‚Üì

Initialize

‚Üì

Create Object
```

Notice that `Employee` is loaded only when first needed.

---

# 15. Where is Class Information Stored?

During loading,

The JVM stores metadata in

```
Metaspace
```

Example:

```
Employee

‚Üì

Methods

‚Üì

Fields

‚Üì

Constant Pool

‚Üì

Annotations

‚Üì

Stored in Metaspace
```

The object instances themselves are **not** stored there; they live on the heap.

---

# 16. Complete Flow

```text
Employee.class

‚Üì

Class Loader

‚Üì

Loading

‚Üì

Verification

‚Üì

Preparation

‚Üì

Resolution

‚Üì

Initialization

‚Üì

Class Ready

‚Üì

new Employee()

‚Üì

Heap Object Created
```

Class metadata and object creation are separate concerns.

---

# 17. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå Compilation loads classes.

**Reality:**

Compilation (`javac`) creates `.class` files.

Loading happens later, when the JVM runs the application.

---

### ‚ùå Every class loads at startup.

**Reality:**

Most classes are loaded lazily.

---

### ‚ùå Loading and initialization are the same.

**Reality:**

Loading reads the class into the JVM.

Initialization executes static initialization logic.

---

### ‚ùå Creating an array initializes the element type.

```java
Employee[] employees = new Employee[10];
```

**Reality:**

Creating an array of a type does not initialize that class.

---

# 18. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What is Class Loading?
2. Why is Class Loading required?
3. What are the three phases of Class Loading?
4. What is Lazy Class Loading?
5. What is Dynamic Class Loading?

---

## Intermediate

1. Explain Loading, Linking, and Initialization.
2. Difference between Loading and Initialization.
3. What triggers Class Initialization?
4. What is `Class.forName()` used for?
5. Why does Spring Boot use Dynamic Class Loading?

---

## Architect-Level

### Question

Your Spring Boot application startup increased from

```
10 seconds

‚Üì

65 seconds
```

How would you investigate?

A structured answer:

1. Measure startup with Spring Boot startup metrics or Java Flight Recorder.
2. Check whether excessive class loading is occurring (large dependency graph, reflection-heavy libraries).
3. Look for unnecessary component scanning or auto-configuration.
4. Evaluate lazy initialization where appropriate.
5. Consider Class Data Sharing (CDS) to reduce class loading overhead.
6. Review classpath size and dependency changes.

This shows that startup performance depends on both **JVM class loading** and **framework behavior**.

---

# Phase Summary

You should now understand:

* A `.class` file must be loaded before it can be used.
* Class Loading consists of **Loading ‚Üí Linking ‚Üí Initialization**.
* Linking includes **Verification**, **Preparation**, and **Resolution**.
* Initialization executes static field initializers and static blocks.
* The JVM loads classes lazily, improving startup time and memory usage.
* Dynamic Class Loading enables frameworks such as Spring and Hibernate.
* Class metadata is stored in **Metaspace**, while object instances are allocated on the **heap**.

---

# What's Next?

## **Module 1 ‚Äì Phase 6: Class Loader Hierarchy**

We'll dive into:

* Bootstrap ClassLoader
* Platform ClassLoader
* Application ClassLoader
* Custom ClassLoaders
* How the JVM decides which loader should load a class
* Class loader isolation
* Spring Boot and class loaders
* OSGi and plugin architectures
* Production debugging
* Interview questions from Basic to Architect

Understanding the hierarchy is essential before learning the **Parent Delegation Model**, which is one of the core design principles of Java class loading.

pages.title: Phase 6: Class Loader Hierarchy
pages.text:
# Java 21 JVM Masterclass

# **Module 1 ‚Äì JVM Fundamentals**

# **Phase 6 ‚Äì Class Loader Hierarchy**

> **Goal of this phase**
>
> Understand **who loads a class** inside the JVM, why there are multiple class loaders, and how the JVM organizes them into a hierarchy.

This is a **very common interview topic** because it explains how the JVM separates JDK classes from application classes and enables frameworks, containers, and plugin architectures.

---

# Learning Objectives

After this phase, you'll understand:

* What is a ClassLoader?
* Why multiple ClassLoaders exist
* Bootstrap ClassLoader
* Platform ClassLoader
* Application ClassLoader
* Custom ClassLoaders
* Class Loader Hierarchy
* Real production examples
* Interview questions

---

# 1. Why Do We Need Multiple Class Loaders?

Suppose your application uses:

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import org.springframework.context.ApplicationContext;
import com.company.service.EmployeeService;
```

Question:

**Should one ClassLoader load all these classes?**

No.

They belong to different sources:

* JDK classes
* Third-party libraries
* Application classes

The JVM separates them for **security**, **modularity**, and **maintainability**.

---

# 2. Class Loader Hierarchy

The JVM uses a parent-child hierarchy.

```text
                 Bootstrap ClassLoader
                        ‚îÇ
                        ‚ñº
                Platform ClassLoader
                        ‚îÇ
                        ‚ñº
              Application ClassLoader
                        ‚îÇ
                        ‚ñº
              Custom ClassLoader(s)
```

Important:

This is a **logical hierarchy**, not Java inheritance. The Bootstrap ClassLoader is implemented by the JVM itself (native code), while the others are Java objects.

---

# 3. Bootstrap ClassLoader

## Purpose

Loads **core Java classes**.

Examples:

```java
java.lang.String

java.lang.Object

java.lang.Integer

java.util.ArrayList

java.util.HashMap
```

These classes are required before almost anything else can run.

---

## Where does it load from?

In modern Java (9+), it loads core classes from the runtime image created by the module system (rather than the old `rt.jar` used before Java 9).

Conceptually:

```text
JDK Runtime Image (java.base and other core modules)

‚Üì

Bootstrap ClassLoader
```

---

## Why is it special?

It is:

* Implemented by the JVM
* Not a normal Java class
* The root of the class loading hierarchy

---

# 4. Platform ClassLoader

Introduced with Java 9.

Purpose:

Loads platform modules that are **not part of the core bootstrap set**.

Examples include APIs from standard modules outside the minimal core runtime.

```text
java.sql

java.xml

java.management
```

Think of it as loading additional JDK functionality.

---

# 5. Application ClassLoader

Also called:

**System ClassLoader**

Purpose:

Loads classes from your application's classpath or module path.

Examples:

```text
Employee.class

OrderService.class

AnalyticsApplication.class
```

Spring Boot application classes are typically loaded by the Application ClassLoader (or a custom loader provided by Spring Boot's executable JAR launcher).

---

# 6. Custom ClassLoader

Applications can create their own loaders.

Example:

```java
public class PluginClassLoader extends ClassLoader {
}
```

Used by:

* Plugin systems
* IDEs
* Application servers
* OSGi
* Bytecode generation libraries
* Hot deployment mechanisms

---

# 7. Complete Hierarchy

```text
                Bootstrap

        Loads:

        java.lang.*

        java.util.*

        java.io.*

                ‚îÇ

                ‚ñº

             Platform

        Loads:

        java.sql

        java.xml

        java.management

                ‚îÇ

                ‚ñº

           Application

        Loads:

        Spring Boot

        Business Classes

        Controllers

        Services

                ‚îÇ

                ‚ñº

        Custom Loaders

        Plugins

        Dynamic Modules
```

---

# 8. Example

Suppose:

```java
String name = "Java";

Employee emp = new Employee();
```

Who loads these?

```text
String

‚Üì

Bootstrap
```

```text
Employee

‚Üì

Application
```

Different classes, different loaders.

---

# 9. How to Find the ClassLoader

Example:

```java
System.out.println(String.class.getClassLoader());
```

Output:

```text
null
```

Why?

Bootstrap ClassLoader is represented as `null` in this API because it is implemented by the JVM rather than as a Java object.

Now:

```java
System.out.println(Employee.class.getClassLoader());
```

Output:

```text
jdk.internal.loader.ClassLoaders$AppClassLoader
```

---

# 10. Another Example

```java
System.out.println(java.sql.Driver.class.getClassLoader());
```

Typical output:

```text
jdk.internal.loader.ClassLoaders$PlatformClassLoader
```

This shows different parts of the JDK are loaded by different loaders.

---

# 11. Why Is This Hierarchy Needed?

Imagine:

```text
Application

‚Üì

Fake String.class
```

If the Application ClassLoader loaded `java.lang.String` before the Bootstrap ClassLoader:

Chaos.

Your application could replace core JDK classes.

The hierarchy helps prevent this by ensuring trusted core classes are loaded first.

---

# 12. Production Example ‚Äì Spring Boot

Your application:

```text
analytics-service.jar

‚Üì

Application ClassLoader
```

JDK classes:

```text
java.util

‚Üì

Bootstrap
```

Spring Framework:

```text
spring-context.jar

‚Üì

Application ClassLoader
```

The JVM uses different loaders depending on where the classes come from.

---

# 13. Application Server Example

Suppose Tomcat hosts:

```text
Application A

Application B
```

Each application can have its own ClassLoader.

```text
               Bootstrap

                    ‚îÇ

                    ‚ñº

              Platform

                    ‚îÇ

                    ‚ñº

              Common Loader

                /         \

               ‚ñº           ‚ñº

        App A Loader   App B Loader
```

Benefits:

* Isolation
* Independent deployments
* Different library versions
* No interference between applications

---

# 14. Plugin Example

IntelliJ IDEA:

```text
IDE

‚Üì

Plugin Loader

‚Üì

Plugin Classes
```

Each plugin can be loaded and unloaded independently.

This is possible because of custom ClassLoaders.

---

# 15. Spring Boot Fat JAR

A Spring Boot executable JAR contains:

```text
BOOT-INF/classes

BOOT-INF/lib
```

Spring Boot uses a specialized launcher and custom class loading mechanism so that classes and nested JARs can be loaded directly from the executable archive.

You normally don't notice this because it happens transparently.

---

# 16. Production Problems

Common ClassLoader issues:

### Memory Leak

```text
Application Undeployed

‚Üì

ClassLoader Still Referenced

‚Üì

Metaspace Cannot Be Freed
```

This is a classic application server issue.

---

### Duplicate Classes

Two different ClassLoaders load the same class.

```text
Employee

‚Üì

Loader A

Employee

‚Üì

Loader B
```

Although the bytecode is identical, the JVM treats them as **different types** because both the **class name and the defining ClassLoader** identify a class.

This can lead to:

```text
ClassCastException
```

---

# 17. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå There is only one ClassLoader.

Reality:

There are multiple ClassLoaders organized in a hierarchy.

---

### ‚ùå Bootstrap is a Java class.

Reality:

Bootstrap ClassLoader is implemented by the JVM itself.

---

### ‚ùå Every class uses the same loader.

Reality:

Different classes are loaded by different loaders depending on their origin.

---

### ‚ùå Same class name means same class.

Reality:

The JVM identifies a class by:

```text
(ClassLoader, Fully Qualified Class Name)
```

Both must match.

---

# 18. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What is a ClassLoader?
2. Why do we need multiple ClassLoaders?
3. Name the three built-in ClassLoaders.
4. Which ClassLoader loads `String`?
5. Which ClassLoader loads your application classes?

---

## Intermediate

1. Explain the ClassLoader hierarchy.
2. Why does `String.class.getClassLoader()` return `null`?
3. What is a Custom ClassLoader?
4. Why are application servers designed with multiple ClassLoaders?
5. How does Spring Boot load classes from a fat JAR?

---

## Architect-Level

### Question

Your application throws:

```text
java.lang.ClassCastException:
com.company.Employee
cannot be cast to
com.company.Employee
```

How is this possible?

A strong answer:

* The class was loaded by **two different ClassLoaders**.
* Although the class name is identical, the JVM treats `(ClassLoader, ClassName)` as the unique identity.
* Investigate application server isolation, plugin frameworks, duplicate libraries, or custom ClassLoaders.

This is a classic senior-level JVM troubleshooting question.

---

# Phase Summary

You should now understand:

* The JVM uses multiple ClassLoaders instead of a single global loader.
* The built-in hierarchy consists of:

  * **Bootstrap ClassLoader** ‚Äì core Java runtime classes
  * **Platform ClassLoader** ‚Äì platform modules
  * **Application ClassLoader** ‚Äì application and most third-party libraries
* Custom ClassLoaders enable plugins, application servers, and dynamic loading.
* A class's identity is determined by **both its fully qualified name and the ClassLoader that defined it**.
* ClassLoader isolation is fundamental to frameworks like Tomcat, OSGi, and Spring Boot.

---

# What's Next?

## **Module 1 ‚Äì Phase 7: Parent Delegation Model**

This is arguably the **most important concept** in Java class loading.

We'll answer questions like:

* Why can't your application replace `java.lang.String`?
* How does the JVM decide which ClassLoader should load a class?
* What is the parent-first delegation algorithm?
* When do frameworks intentionally break parent delegation?
* How does Tomcat isolate web applications?
* Why do custom ClassLoaders exist if delegation already works?

Understanding the Parent Delegation Model is essential before moving on to linking, verification, and advanced class loading scenarios.

pages.title: Phase 7: Parent Delegation Model
pages.text:
# Java 21 JVM Masterclass

# **Module 1 ‚Äì JVM Fundamentals**

# **Phase 7 ‚Äì Parent Delegation Model**

> **Goal of this phase**
>
> Understand the **Parent Delegation Model**, one of the most fundamental design principles of the JVM. This model ensures **security, consistency, and class uniqueness** by controlling how classes are loaded.

This topic is asked in almost every **Senior Java, Staff Engineer, and Architect** interview because it explains why Java is secure and why class loading behaves the way it does.

---

# Learning Objectives

After this phase, you'll understand:

* What is Parent Delegation?
* Why it exists
* The complete delegation algorithm
* Security benefits
* How class loading requests travel
* When delegation is intentionally broken
* Tomcat and Spring Boot examples
* Common interview questions

---

# 1. The Problem Without Parent Delegation

Suppose your application contains:

```text
com/company/String.class
```

Or even worse:

```text
java/lang/String.class
```

Question:

**Which `String` should the JVM use?**

* JDK's `java.lang.String`
* Your fake `java.lang.String`

If the JVM loaded the application's version first, anyone could replace core Java classes.

Example:

```java
public class String {
    public String() {
        System.out.println("Hacked!");
    }
}
```

This would completely break Java's security model.

---

# 2. The Solution

The JVM follows a simple rule:

> **A ClassLoader always asks its parent to load a class first.**

Only if the parent cannot load it does the child try.

This is called the **Parent Delegation Model**.

---

# 3. Delegation Hierarchy

```text
              Bootstrap
                   ‚ñ≤
                   ‚îÇ
             Platform
                   ‚ñ≤
                   ‚îÇ
          Application
                   ‚ñ≤
                   ‚îÇ
          Custom ClassLoader
```

Notice:

Requests travel **up first**, not down.

---

# 4. Loading Algorithm

Suppose the Application ClassLoader receives:

```text
java.lang.String
```

The algorithm is:

```text
Application Loader

‚Üì

Ask Parent

‚Üì

Platform Loader

‚Üì

Ask Parent

‚Üì

Bootstrap Loader

‚Üì

Found String

‚Üì

Return Class
```

The Application ClassLoader never attempts to load `String` itself.

---

# 5. Visual Flow

```text
Application Loader

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Need java.lang.String

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Platform Loader

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Bootstrap Loader

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Loads String

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Returns Class
```

---

# 6. Another Example

Now:

```java
Employee emp = new Employee();
```

Application Loader receives:

```text
Employee.class
```

Algorithm:

```text
Application

‚Üì

Ask Platform

‚Üì

Not Found

‚Üì

Ask Bootstrap

‚Üì

Not Found

‚Üì

Application Loads Employee
```

Because the parent loaders don't know about your business classes, the Application ClassLoader loads them.

---

# 7. Parent Delegation Algorithm

Simplified pseudo-code:

```java
Class<?> loadClass(String name) {

    if (alreadyLoaded(name))
        return loadedClass;

    try {
        return parent.loadClass(name);
    } catch (ClassNotFoundException e) {
        return findClass(name);
    }

}
```

Key points:

* Check if already loaded.
* Ask the parent.
* If the parent can't find it, load it locally.

This avoids duplicate loading and preserves trust in core classes.

---

# 8. Why Parent Delegation?

## Security

Imagine:

```text
Fake Object.class
```

Without delegation:

```text
Application Loader

‚Üì

Loads Fake Object
```

The JVM would become unsafe.

With delegation:

```text
Application

‚Üì

Bootstrap

‚Üì

Loads Original Object
```

Core JDK classes always come from trusted sources.

---

## Avoid Duplicate Loading

Suppose:

```text
Employee.class
```

is referenced from 100 places.

Without checks:

```text
Employee

‚Üì

Loaded 100 Times
```

With delegation and loader caching:

```text
Employee

‚Üì

Loaded Once
```

---

## Consistency

Every component sees the same definition of core classes.

```text
java.lang.String

‚Üì

One Trusted Copy
```

---

# 9. Example

```java
System.out.println(String.class.getClassLoader());
```

Output:

```text
null
```

Because Bootstrap loads `String`.

Now:

```java
System.out.println(Employee.class.getClassLoader());
```

Output:

```text
AppClassLoader
```

The delegation process already ensured Bootstrap and Platform couldn't load it.

---

# 10. Can Parent Delegation Be Broken?

Yes.

Some frameworks intentionally load classes themselves first.

This is called **Child-First Loading**.

Examples:

* Tomcat (for web application isolation in certain scenarios)
* OSGi
* Plugin systems
* Some application servers

---

# 11. Why Break Delegation?

Suppose:

Server provides:

```text
commons-logging 1.0
```

Application requires:

```text
commons-logging 2.0
```

If parent always wins:

```text
Wrong Version Loaded
```

Instead:

```text
Application Loader

‚Üì

Loads Local Version First
```

This allows different applications to use different library versions.

---

# 12. Tomcat Example

Tomcat hosts:

```text
App A

App B
```

Both have different versions of Jackson.

```text
App A

jackson-2.15
```

```text
App B

jackson-2.18
```

Each application has its own WebApp ClassLoader.

```text
             Bootstrap

                  ‚îÇ

                  ‚ñº

            Common Loader

             /         \

            ‚ñº           ‚ñº

      WebApp A     WebApp B
```

Each application can load its own libraries without interfering with the other.

---

# 13. Spring Boot Example

Spring Boot executable JAR:

```text
BOOT-INF/classes

BOOT-INF/lib
```

The Spring Boot launcher uses a custom ClassLoader to load classes and nested JARs directly from the executable archive.

Even then, it still follows delegation for core JDK classes.

---

# 14. What Happens if Delegation Didn't Exist?

Imagine:

```text
100 Libraries

‚Üì

Each Has Object.class

‚Üì

Chaos
```

Different parts of the application could end up using incompatible definitions of core classes.

The Parent Delegation Model prevents this.

---

# 15. Production Example

Your telecom analytics application uses:

* Spring Boot
* Kafka
* PostgreSQL Driver
* Jackson
* Micrometer

When the JVM needs:

```text
java.lang.String
```

It is always loaded by Bootstrap.

When it needs:

```text
org.springframework.context.ApplicationContext
```

It is loaded by the Application ClassLoader (or Spring Boot's launcher class loader).

When it needs:

```text
com.company.analytics.AnalyticsApplication
```

It is loaded by the Application ClassLoader.

The delegation chain ensures each request reaches the appropriate loader.

---

# 16. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå Parent Delegation means the parent loads every class.

**Reality:**

The parent gets the first chance. If it can't find the class, the child loads it.

---

### ‚ùå The Application ClassLoader never loads classes.

**Reality:**

It loads almost all application and third-party library classes.

---

### ‚ùå Parent Delegation cannot be bypassed.

**Reality:**

Custom ClassLoaders can implement child-first behavior where appropriate.

---

### ‚ùå Bootstrap is a Java object.

**Reality:**

Bootstrap ClassLoader is implemented by the JVM itself and is not represented as a normal Java object.

---

# 17. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What is the Parent Delegation Model?
2. Why does Java use Parent Delegation?
3. Which ClassLoader loads `java.lang.String`?
4. What happens if a parent cannot load a class?
5. What is the benefit of delegation?

---

## Intermediate

1. Explain the Parent Delegation algorithm.
2. Why can't an application replace `java.lang.Object`?
3. How does the JVM prevent duplicate class loading?
4. What is child-first loading?
5. Why do plugin systems often use custom ClassLoaders?

---

## Architect-Level

### Question

Your application contains:

```text
java/lang/String.class
```

Will it replace the JDK `String`?

**Expected answer:**

No.

The Application ClassLoader delegates to its parent.

The Bootstrap ClassLoader finds and loads the trusted `java.lang.String` first.

The application's fake class is ignored for that fully qualified name.

---

### Question

Two web applications deployed on the same Tomcat server use different Jackson versions.

How is this possible?

**Expected answer:**

* Tomcat gives each web application its own ClassLoader.
* Each ClassLoader loads its application's libraries independently.
* ClassLoader isolation allows different versions of the same library to coexist.

---

# Phase Summary

You should now understand:

* The **Parent Delegation Model** is the JVM's default class loading strategy.
* Class loading requests travel **from child to parent**, giving trusted loaders the first opportunity.
* This model provides:

  * Security
  * Consistency
  * Prevention of duplicate class loading
* The Application ClassLoader loads classes only when parent loaders cannot.
* Some frameworks intentionally use **child-first loading** for isolation or versioning.
* ClassLoader delegation is one of the foundations of Java's security and modular runtime.

---

# Module 1 Progress

‚úÖ Phase 1 ‚Äì Introduction to JVM & Java Architecture
‚úÖ Phase 2 ‚Äì JVM Architecture Deep Dive
‚úÖ Phase 3 ‚Äì Java Compilation Process (.java ‚Üí CPU)
‚úÖ Phase 4 ‚Äì Class File Structure (.class Format)
‚úÖ Phase 5 ‚Äì Class Loading Mechanism
‚úÖ Phase 6 ‚Äì Class Loader Hierarchy
‚úÖ **Phase 7 ‚Äì Parent Delegation Model**

At this point, you have a solid understanding of how Java source code becomes loaded classes inside the JVM.

---

# What's Next?

## **Module 1 ‚Äì Phase 8: Linking Process (Verification, Preparation, Resolution)**

This phase dives into what happens **after a class is loaded but before it can execute**.

We'll explore:

* Verification (bytecode safety)
* Preparation (allocation of static fields)
* Resolution (symbolic to direct references)
* Constant Pool resolution
* Lazy vs eager resolution
* Real JVM examples
* Production troubleshooting
* Architect-level interview questions

This is where the JVM transforms a loaded class into one that is ready for initialization and execution.

pages.title: Comments
pages.text: # This is completed as part of Garbage collectors Book

pages.title: Java Flight Recorder
pages.text:
Java Flight Recorder (JFR) is one of the most important JVM profiling and diagnostics tools for production applications. It has **very low overhead (typically <1%)**, making it suitable even for production systems.

Since you're preparing for **Senior/Staff/Architect Java interviews**, JFR is an essential topic because companies like Oracle, Amazon, Netflix, and many enterprise product companies use it to diagnose performance issues.

---

# What is Java Flight Recorder (JFR)?

Java Flight Recorder continuously records JVM events with minimal overhead.

It records:

* CPU usage
* Memory allocations
* Garbage Collection
* Thread activity
* Lock contention
* File I/O
* Socket I/O
* Exceptions
* Method profiling
* Native Memory
* JVM internals

Think of it as a **black box recorder for the JVM**, similar to an airplane flight recorder.

---

# Architecture

```
                 Application
                      ‚îÇ
                      ‚ñº
                JVM Runtime
                      ‚îÇ
      ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
      ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº               ‚ñº                ‚ñº
 Thread Events   GC Events      Memory Events
      ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº               ‚ñº                ‚ñº
          Java Flight Recorder
                     ‚îÇ
              records events
                     ‚îÇ
                     ‚ñº
            recording.jfr
                     ‚îÇ
                     ‚ñº
      Java Mission Control (JMC)
```

---

# What can JFR detect?

Imagine your Spring Boot application is slow.

JFR can answer:

* Why CPU is 100%?
* Why GC pauses are high?
* Which method allocates most memory?
* Which thread is blocked?
* Which synchronized block causes contention?
* Why latency suddenly increased?
* Which SQL query is slow?
* Which HTTP request is slow?

---

# Check if JFR is available

```bash
java -XX:+FlightRecorder -version
```

Modern Java (11+) has JFR built in.

---

# Start JFR at JVM Startup

Example:

```bash
java \
-XX:StartFlightRecording=filename=app.jfr,duration=5m \
-jar app.jar
```

After 5 minutes

```
app.jfr
```

is generated.

---

# Continuous Recording

```bash
java \
-XX:StartFlightRecording=filename=app.jfr,maxsize=500M,maxage=1d \
-jar app.jar
```

Meaning

```
Keep last one day
OR

Keep maximum 500MB
```

Very useful for production.

---

# Start JFR on Running Process

Find PID

Linux

```bash
jps
```

Example

```
12345 MyApplication
```

Start recording

```bash
jcmd 12345 JFR.start
```

Output

```
Started recording 1
```

---

# Give Recording Name

```bash
jcmd 12345 JFR.start \
name=ProductionRecording \
duration=5m \
filename=production.jfr
```

---

# Check Recording

```bash
jcmd 12345 JFR.check
```

Output

```
Recording 1:

Name: ProductionRecording

Running

Duration:
5m
```

---

# Stop Recording

```bash
jcmd 12345 JFR.stop name=ProductionRecording
```

File gets saved.

---

# Dump Current Recording

Useful for long-running applications.

```bash
jcmd 12345 JFR.dump \
name=ProductionRecording \
filename=current.jfr
```

No need to stop JVM.

---

# View Recording

Best tool:

Java Mission Control (JMC)

Open

```
current.jfr
```

You'll see

* Overview
* Threads
* GC
* Memory
* CPU
* Method Profiling
* Exceptions
* Locks
* I/O
* Heap Statistics

---

# Example 1: High CPU

Application is consuming 100% CPU.

JFR shows

```
CPU Samples

parseOrders()

42%

generateReport()

33%

serializeJson()

15%
```

Immediately identifies the hotspot.

---

# Example 2: Memory Leak

JFR

```
Object Allocation

HashMap

6GB

ArrayList

2GB

String

1.8GB
```

You immediately know where memory is being allocated.

---

# Example 3: Lock Contention

JFR

```
Lock Instances

OrderService

Blocked Time

34 seconds

Thread Count

240
```

You immediately know

```
synchronized(OrderService)
```

is the bottleneck.

---

# Example 4: Excessive Garbage Collection

JFR

```
Young GC

4000

Old GC

35

Pause

4 sec
```

Then investigate

* Heap too small?
* Too many object allocations?
* Incorrect GC tuning?

---

# Common `jcmd` Commands

| Command                                | Purpose                |
| -------------------------------------- | ---------------------- |
| `jcmd <pid> JFR.start`                 | Start recording        |
| `jcmd <pid> JFR.check`                 | Check recordings       |
| `jcmd <pid> JFR.dump filename=app.jfr` | Save current recording |
| `jcmd <pid> JFR.stop`                  | Stop recording         |

---

# Useful Recording Options

```
duration=10m

filename=app.jfr

maxage=2d

maxsize=1G

settings=profile

settings=default
```

Example

```bash
jcmd 12345 JFR.start \
settings=profile \
duration=10m \
filename=profile.jfr
```

---

# Default vs Profile Settings

## Default

* Low overhead
* Production-safe
* Continuous monitoring

## Profile

* More detailed events
* Higher sampling rate
* Better for performance investigations
* Slightly higher overhead

---

# JFR Events

Some commonly recorded events include:

| Category   | Events                             |
| ---------- | ---------------------------------- |
| GC         | Young GC, Old GC, Heap Summary     |
| Memory     | Object Allocation, TLAB Allocation |
| Threads    | Thread Start, Thread End, Sleep    |
| Locks      | Monitor Enter, Lock Contention     |
| CPU        | Execution Samples                  |
| Exceptions | Thrown Exceptions                  |
| File I/O   | Read, Write                        |
| Socket     | Read, Write                        |
| JVM        | Class Loading, Compilation         |
| Native     | Native Memory                      |

---

# Spring Boot Example

Run with JFR enabled:

```bash
java \
-XX:StartFlightRecording=filename=springboot.jfr,duration=10m \
-jar my-service.jar
```

Or start recording on a running service:

```bash
jps
```

```
22115 MyService
```

```bash
jcmd 22115 JFR.start \
settings=profile \
duration=10m \
filename=springboot.jfr
```

After reproducing the issue:

```bash
jcmd 22115 JFR.stop
```

Open `springboot.jfr` in JMC and inspect CPU, memory, GC, threads, lock contention, and I/O.

---

# Typical Production Workflow

```
Production issue
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Find JVM PID
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Start JFR (profile or default)
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Reproduce or wait for issue
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Dump/Stop recording
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Open .jfr in Java Mission Control
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Analyze:
  ‚Ä¢ CPU hotspots
  ‚Ä¢ Memory allocations
  ‚Ä¢ GC pauses
  ‚Ä¢ Lock contention
  ‚Ä¢ Thread states
  ‚Ä¢ Exceptions
  ‚Ä¢ File/Socket I/O
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Identify root cause and optimize
```

## Interview Questions

**Q1. Why use JFR instead of a traditional profiler?**
JFR has very low overhead, can safely run in production, records a wide range of JVM and application events, and integrates with Java Mission Control for detailed analysis.

**Q2. What's the difference between JFR and a heap dump?**

| JFR                                             | Heap Dump                              |
| ----------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Time-series recording of runtime events         | Snapshot of heap at one point in time  |
| Shows CPU, GC, threads, locks, I/O, allocations | Shows objects and references in memory |
| Excellent for performance analysis              | Excellent for memory leak analysis     |

**Q3. What's the difference between JFR and GC logs?**

| JFR                                                   | GC Logs                           |
| ----------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Comprehensive JVM telemetry                           | Garbage collection events only    |
| Includes CPU, threads, locks, allocations, exceptions | Focused on GC behavior and pauses |
| Best for end-to-end performance diagnosis             | Best for GC tuning                |

For Java 21 and modern JVM interview preparation, it's valuable to study **JFR together with Java Mission Control (JMC), `jcmd`, GC logs, and Async Profiler**, since they complement each other when diagnosing production performance issues.

./jmc

generalNotes.title: JIT-compiled native machine code
generalNotes.text:
Just-In-Time (JIT) compiled native machine code is the raw, binary instruction set generated on the fly while a program runs, allowing managed applications to execute directly on host CPU hardware with near-zero software translation overhead. [1, 2]  
By merging the portability of interpreted languages with the speed of statically compiled ones, a JIT engine produces execution-ready code tuned dynamically to your specific processor architecture. [1, 3]  
‚öôÔ∏è How It Is Generated (The Lifecycle) 
Instead of translating an entire codebase before execution (Ahead-Of-Time compilation), the machine code generation occurs continuously via a highly coordinated runtime loop: 

1. Bytecode Feeding: The runtime environment loads intermediate instructions (e.g., Java Bytecode or .NET Common Intermediate Language). 
2. The Cold Path (Interpretation): An interpreter translates and executes these bytecodes line-by-line. This guarantees near-instant application startup times. 
3. Profiling & Hotspot Detection: A background profiler tracks execution frequency. Loops or functions run thousands of times are flagged as "hotspots". 
4. On-the-Fly Compilation: The JIT compiler processes the hotspot, translating its intermediate instructions into architecture-specific binary machine code (like x86-64 or ARM64). 
5. Memory Allocation: The raw bytes are written directly to a designated region in memory (the Code Cache). 
6. Execution Switch: The virtual machine swaps the interpreter references out, pointing the CPU's instruction pointer straight to the freshly allocated block of native instructions. [10, 13, 14, 15]  

🛡Ô∏è The Low-Level Security Trap 
Computers enforce a security standard known as W^X (Write XOR Execute). Memory can either be writable (to accept new data) or executable (to run instructions), but never both simultaneously. [16, 17, 18, 19, 20]  
To safely bypass this when deploying JIT-compiled native code, engines utilize precise low-level memory APIs: 

‚Ä¢ Memory Allocation: The engine requests an anonymous memory map using system utilities like  (on POSIX systems) or  (on Windows). 
‚Ä¢ The Permission Flip: The system initially maps the buffer with Write permissions (). Once the JIT engine finishes writing the raw binary opcodes, it changes permissions to Execute () before calling the code. [11, 21]  

‚ö° Why It Can Outperform Static Compilation 
Statically compiled binaries (like those built from C++) cannot adapt once distributed. JIT-compiled native code has a profound structural edge because it is optimized using real-time production data: 

‚Ä¢ Targeted Architecture Optimizations: A pre-built binary must target a lowest-common-denominator processor to maximize compatibility. A JIT engine identifies the exact host CPU at runtime, enabling specialized vector extensions (like AVX-512) automatically. 
‚Ä¢ Dynamic Inlining: The JIT engine monitors code execution paths. If a method is called inside a hot loop, it physically replaces the function call instruction with the actual body of the function, entirely eliminating call overhead. 
‚Ä¢ Speculative Optimization & Deoptimization: The JIT engine makes bold performance assumptions based on current behavior (e.g., assuming a variable will never be ). If that assumption holds true, the machine code runs optimally. If conditions change, the JIT cleanly discards the optimized code cache and seamlessly falls back to the interpreter loop. [1, 31, 32, 33, 34]  

🏢 Real-World Engines in Production 
Most modern consumer and enterprise digital experiences rely on JIT-generated machine code to power their execution layers: [35]  

| Runtime Environment | Primary Language | JIT Compiler Components  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Java Virtual Machine (JVM) | Java, Kotlin, Scala | C1 (Client / Baseline); &; C2 (Server / Optimizing)  |
| .NET Common Language Runtime (CLR) | C#, F#, VB.NET | RyuJIT (The default .NET optimizing compiler)  |
| V8 Engine | JavaScript, WebAssembly | Sparkplug (Baseline) &; Turbofan (Optimizing Tier)  |

If you are exploring a specific project or ran into an optimization bottleneck, tell me: 

‚Ä¢ What programming language or runtime environment are you working with? 
‚Ä¢ Are you looking to optimize performance, or are you interested in how low-level memory execution works? 

I can tailor deeper implementation rules or code snippets for your exact scenario. 

AI responses may include mistakes.

pages.title: Intro
pages.text:
Excellent choice. For **Staff Engineer / Architect interviews**, **Garbage Collection (GC)** is one of the highest-value topics. It is common for interviewers to spend **45‚Äì90 minutes** discussing JVM internals, memory management, and GC tuning.

This topic is too large for a single lesson. To cover it properly, we'll treat it as a complete course.

# Garbage Collectors Deep Dive (Java 21)

## Why this matters

Modern Java applications are expected to:

* Handle millions of requests
* Process TBs of data
* Run in Kubernetes
* Meet strict latency SLAs
* Scale horizontally

All of these depend heavily on **GC behavior**.

A poor GC choice can easily increase latency from milliseconds to seconds.

---

# Complete Learning Roadmap

I recommend covering this in **25 phases**, starting from JVM fundamentals and ending with production tuning.

| Phase | Topic                                                 | Difficulty |
| ----- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ---------- |
| 1     | JVM Memory Architecture                               | ‚≠ê          |
| 2     | Object Creation & Memory Allocation                   | ‚≠ê          |
| 3     | Object Lifecycle                                      | ‚≠ê          |
| 4     | Reachability Analysis                                 | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         |
| 5     | Mark-Sweep-Compact Algorithm                          | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         |
| 6     | Generational GC Theory                                | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         |
| 7     | Serial Garbage Collector                              | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         |
| 8     | Parallel Garbage Collector                            | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         |
| 9     | CMS Garbage Collector (Historical)                    | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê        |
| 10    | G1 Garbage Collector                                  | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 11    | ZGC                                                   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| 12    | Shenandoah GC                                         | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| 13    | Epsilon GC                                            | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê        |
| 14    | Garbage Collectors Comparison                         | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 15    | GC Logging & Analysis                                 | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 16    | JVM Heap Tuning                                       | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 17    | Young GC vs Full GC                                   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê        |
| 18    | GC in Containers & Kubernetes                         | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 19    | Large Heap Management (100GB+)                        | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| 20    | Memory Leaks                                          | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 21    | OutOfMemoryError Deep Dive                            | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 22    | JVM Monitoring Tools (JFR, VisualVM, MAT, jcmd, jmap) | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê       |
| 23    | Real Production Case Studies                          | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| 24    | GC Interview Questions (100+)                         | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| 25    | Architect Decision Matrix                             | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |

---

# How Deep Will We Go?

This won't be a superficial overview.

For each GC, we'll cover:

* History and motivation
* Internal algorithms
* Data structures
* Heap layout
* Thread model
* Pause behavior
* Advantages and disadvantages
* Performance characteristics
* JVM options
* Tuning strategies
* Production use cases
* Kubernetes considerations
* Architect decision-making
* Common interview questions

We'll also include:

* Memory diagrams
* GC execution flow diagrams
* Real production examples
* JVM flags
* Sample GC logs
* Log analysis
* Performance tuning exercises

---

# Suggested Learning Order

We'll build knowledge incrementally.

```
JVM Memory
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Object Allocation
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Object Lifecycle
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Reachability
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
GC Algorithms
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Generational GC
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Individual Collectors
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Tuning
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Production Debugging
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Architect Decisions
```

Each phase depends on the previous one.

---

# Expected Outcome

By the end of this course, you should be able to answer questions like:

* Why does Java need Garbage Collection?
* How is an object allocated?
* What is a TLAB?
* Why are most objects allocated in Eden?
* What happens during a Minor GC?
* Why does a Full GC pause the JVM?
* Why was CMS deprecated?
* How does G1 divide the heap into regions?
* How does ZGC relocate objects without long pauses?
* What is a colored pointer?
* How does Shenandoah use Brooks pointers?
* When should you choose G1 vs ZGC?
* How do you analyze GC logs?
* How do you tune GC for Kubernetes?
* How do you diagnose memory leaks?
* How do you optimize a 500 GB heap?

These are exactly the kinds of questions asked in senior JVM and architecture interviews.

---

# Interview Relevance

Different roles emphasize different areas:

### Senior Java Developer

* JVM memory model
* Heap vs Stack
* Young vs Old Generation
* Minor vs Major GC
* G1 basics

### Staff Engineer

* GC tuning
* Throughput vs latency
* Container-aware JVM
* Production troubleshooting
* GC log analysis

### Architect

* Choosing the right collector
* Large-heap strategies
* Kubernetes deployment implications
* Cloud cost optimization
* Designing low-latency systems
* Capacity planning

---

# What Makes This Course Different?

We'll go beyond "what" and focus on "why".

For example, instead of simply stating:

> G1 divides the heap into regions.

We'll answer:

* Why regions?
* Why 1‚Äì32 MB region sizes?
* How are regions selected?
* How does G1 predict pause times?
* Why did Oracle replace CMS with G1?
* Why can ZGC achieve sub-10 ms pauses on multi-terabyte heaps?

Understanding these design decisions is what differentiates an architect from someone who has memorized features.

---

# Recommended Pace

Because of the depth, I recommend treating each phase like a chapter:

* **Phases 1‚Äì6:** Core theory
* **Phases 7‚Äì13:** Individual collectors
* **Phases 14‚Äì19:** Comparison and tuning
* **Phases 20‚Äì25:** Production troubleshooting and interview mastery

---

## Next Step

We'll begin with **Phase 1 ‚Äì JVM Memory Architecture**, where we'll build the foundation for everything else.

That phase will cover:

* JVM runtime memory areas
* Heap vs Stack vs Metaspace
* Program Counter Register
* Native Method Stack
* Thread-local vs shared memory
* Object allocation flow
* Memory diagrams
* Common misconceptions
* Interview questions
* Architect-level perspective

Everything about Garbage Collection builds on that foundation, so it's the ideal place to start.

pages.title: JVM Memory Architecture
pages.text:
Excellent. This is the **foundation of the entire JVM**. Every Garbage Collector (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah, Parallel GC) ultimately manages memory in the JVM runtime. If you don't understand the JVM memory architecture, the rest of the GC topics won't make sense.

For **Staff Engineer/Architect interviews** at companies like Oracle, Microsoft, Amazon, Walmart, Adobe, or product companies, this topic is almost always explored before discussing specific garbage collectors.

---

# Garbage Collectors Deep Dive

# Phase 1 ‚Äì JVM Memory Architecture

---

# Learning Objectives

After completing this phase, you should understand:

* Why JVM memory is divided into multiple areas
* JVM Runtime Data Areas
* Heap
* Stack
* Metaspace
* Code Cache
* Program Counter (PC Register)
* Native Method Stack
* Thread-local vs Shared memory
* Object allocation flow
* JVM startup memory layout
* Interview questions

---

# 1. Why Doesn't Java Use One Big Memory Area?

Imagine a Java application:

```java
public class OrderService {

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        OrderService service = new OrderService();
        service.process();
    }

    void process() {
        Customer customer = new Customer();
    }
}
```

The JVM needs to store different kinds of data:

* Local variables
* Method calls
* Objects
* Class metadata
* Bytecode
* Native library information
* JIT compiled code

Putting everything into one giant memory block would make:

* Memory management inefficient
* Garbage collection slower
* Concurrency harder
* Performance unpredictable

Instead, the JVM divides memory into specialized runtime areas.

---

# 2. JVM Runtime Memory Layout

```text
                    JVM PROCESS
‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
‚îÇ                                                      ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                Runtime Data Areas                    ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                                                      ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ Shared by All Threads ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ                                                 ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ  Heap                                           ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ  Metaspace                                      ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ  Code Cache                                     ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                                                      ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ One Copy Per Thread ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ                                                 ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ  Java Stack                                     ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ  Program Counter Register                       ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îÇ  Native Method Stack                            ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                                                      ‚îÇ
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
```

This is the most important JVM diagram to remember.

---

# 3. Shared Memory

Shared by every thread.

```
Heap

Metaspace

Code Cache
```

Example

```
Thread A

‚Üì

Customer Object

‚Üë

Thread B
```

Both threads can access the same heap object (subject to synchronization).

---

# 4. Thread-Local Memory

Every thread gets its own copy.

```
Thread A

    Stack

    PC Register

    Native Stack
```

```
Thread B

    Stack

    PC Register

    Native Stack
```

No other thread can directly access another thread's Java stack.

This isolation is fundamental to Java's concurrency model.

---

# 5. Heap

The Heap stores:

* Objects
* Arrays
* Instance fields

Example

```java
Customer customer =
        new Customer();
```

Memory

```text
Stack

customer
   ‚îÇ
   ‚ñº
Heap

Customer Object
```

The variable lives on the stack.

The object lives on the heap.

---

# 6. Stack

Every method call creates a new **stack frame**.

Example

```java
main()

‚Üì

service()

‚Üì

save()

‚Üì

publish()
```

Each method owns:

* Local variables
* Parameters
* Operand stack
* Return information

When a method returns:

Its stack frame disappears automatically.

No Garbage Collection is involved.

---

# 7. Stack Example

```java
void calculate() {

    int x = 10;

    int y = 20;

    int z = x + y;
}
```

Memory

```text
Stack Frame

x = 10

y = 20

z = 30
```

When `calculate()` finishes, the entire frame is popped from the stack.

---

# 8. Heap + Stack Together

```java
Employee emp =
        new Employee();
```

Memory

```text
Stack

emp
 ‚îÇ
 ‚ñº

Heap

Employee Object
```

The stack contains only the **reference**.

The actual object resides on the heap.

---

# 9. Multiple Threads

```
Thread A

Stack A

‚Üì

Employee
```

```
Thread B

Stack B

‚Üì

Order
```

Both stacks can point to the same heap object.

```text
Heap

Employee
```

The heap is shared.

The stacks are isolated.

---

# 10. Metaspace

Before Java 8:

```
PermGen
```

After Java 8:

```
Metaspace
```

Stores:

* Class metadata
* Method metadata
* Constant pool
* Field information
* Annotations

**Important:** Metaspace is allocated from **native memory**, not the Java heap.

---

# 11. What Is Stored in Metaspace?

Suppose

```java
class Customer
```

The following metadata is stored in Metaspace:

```
Class Name

Fields

Methods

Interfaces

Annotations

Bytecode metadata
```

The `Customer` instances themselves are stored in the Heap.

---

# 12. Code Cache

When the JVM's Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler compiles frequently executed ("hot") methods into native machine code, that machine code is stored in the **Code Cache**.

Flow:

```
Bytecode

‚Üì

JIT Compiler

‚Üì

Native Machine Code

‚Üì

Code Cache
```

This avoids interpreting the same bytecode repeatedly.

---

# 13. Program Counter (PC Register)

Every thread has its own Program Counter.

It stores:

```
Current Instruction
```

Example

```java
a();

b();

c();
```

The PC Register tells the JVM which bytecode instruction should execute next.

---

# 14. Native Method Stack

Used when Java calls native code through technologies like JNI.

Example

```java
System.loadLibrary(...);
```

or platform-specific native methods.

Java Stack

‚Üì

JNI

‚Üì

Native Method Stack

‚Üì

C/C++

---

# 15. Object Allocation Flow

Consider:

```java
Customer customer =
        new Customer();
```

Execution:

```
Thread

‚Üì

Stack Frame Created

‚Üì

new

‚Üì

Heap Allocation

‚Üì

Constructor Runs

‚Üì

Reference Stored in Stack
```

This is the lifecycle of a newly created object from the perspective of memory.

---

# 16. JVM Startup

When the JVM starts:

```
JVM

‚Üì

ClassLoader

‚Üì

Load Classes

‚Üì

Metaspace

‚Üì

main()

‚Üì

Stack

‚Üì

Heap
```

The JVM initializes runtime structures before your application code begins.

---

# 17. Memory Ownership

| Memory Area  | Owner             |
| ------------ | ----------------- |
| Heap         | Entire JVM        |
| Metaspace    | Entire JVM        |
| Code Cache   | Entire JVM        |
| Stack        | Individual Thread |
| PC Register  | Individual Thread |
| Native Stack | Individual Thread |

---

# 18. Which Memory Areas Are Managed by GC?

| Area         | Garbage Collector?                                                                                                      |
| ------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Heap         | ‚úÖ Yes                                                                                                                   |
| Stack        | ‚ùå No                                                                                                                    |
| PC Register  | ‚ùå No                                                                                                                    |
| Native Stack | ‚ùå No                                                                                                                    |
| Code Cache   | ‚ùå No (managed separately by the JVM)                                                                                    |
| Metaspace    | ‚öÝÔ∏è Class metadata can be reclaimed when class loaders become unreachable; it is not managed like ordinary heap objects. |

A key interview point:

> Garbage Collectors primarily reclaim **heap objects**. Metaspace cleanup is tied to **class unloading**, which is a separate mechanism.

---

# 19. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå "Objects are stored on the stack."

No.

Objects are allocated on the heap (with some JVM optimizations such as escape analysis potentially eliminating allocations).

---

### ‚ùå "GC cleans the stack."

No.

The stack is automatically unwound when methods return.

---

### ‚ùå "Metaspace is inside the Heap."

No.

Metaspace resides in native memory.

---

### ‚ùå "Every thread has its own heap."

No.

The heap is shared across all threads.

---

# 20. Real Production Example

Imagine a Spring Boot microservice handling thousands of requests.

```
HTTP Request

‚Üì

Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Java Stack

‚Üì

Controller

‚Üì

Service

‚Üì

Repository

‚Üì

Heap Objects

‚Üì

Database
```

For each request:

* A thread (platform or virtual) gets its own stack.
* Business objects are created in the heap.
* Framework metadata is stored in Metaspace.
* Frequently executed methods are JIT-compiled into the Code Cache.

Understanding which memory area is responsible for which kind of data is essential when diagnosing memory leaks or performance issues.

---

# 21. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What are the JVM Runtime Data Areas?
2. Difference between Heap and Stack?
3. What is Metaspace?
4. What is the Program Counter Register?

---

## Intermediate

5. Why is the heap shared?
6. Why does each thread need its own stack?
7. What is stored in the Code Cache?
8. What is the Native Method Stack?

---

## Advanced

9. Why was PermGen replaced with Metaspace?
10. Why isn't the stack garbage collected?
11. Can Metaspace run out of memory?
12. How do Virtual Threads affect JVM memory usage?
13. How does JIT interact with the Code Cache?

---

# Architect Perspective

Imagine your microservice runs:

* 5 million requests/day
* Java 21
* Virtual Threads
* G1 or ZGC
* Kubernetes

Memory layout:

```
Kubernetes Pod
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
      JVM Process
        ‚îÇ
 ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
 ‚îÇ Shared                      ‚îÇ
 ‚îÇ  ‚Ä¢ Heap                     ‚îÇ
 ‚îÇ  ‚Ä¢ Metaspace                ‚îÇ
 ‚îÇ  ‚Ä¢ Code Cache               ‚îÇ
 ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
        ‚îÇ
   Thousands of Virtual Threads
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Each Virtual Thread:
  ‚Ä¢ Java Stack (lightweight)
  ‚Ä¢ Program Counter
```

As an architect, you should think about:

* Heap sizing (`-Xms`, `-Xmx`)
* Metaspace sizing (`-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`)
* Code Cache sizing (`-XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize`)
* Container memory limits
* Thread counts
* GC strategy

These decisions directly affect latency, throughput, and cloud costs.

---

# Key Takeaways

* JVM memory is divided into specialized runtime areas for performance and isolation.
* **Heap** stores objects and is the primary target of garbage collection.
* **Stacks** are thread-local and automatically managed through method calls and returns.
* **Metaspace** stores class metadata in native memory.
* **Code Cache** stores JIT-compiled machine code.
* Understanding the JVM memory architecture is the foundation for learning object allocation, GC algorithms, and performance tuning.

-------------------------------------------------------

Here are concise interview-ready answers.

## Basic

### 1. What are the JVM Runtime Data Areas?

* **Heap** ‚Äì Stores objects and arrays.
* **Stack** ‚Äì Stores method frames, local variables, and call stack.
* **Metaspace** ‚Äì Stores class metadata.
* **Program Counter (PC) Register** ‚Äì Tracks the current instruction being executed.
* **Native Method Stack** ‚Äì Supports execution of native (JNI) methods.
* **Code Cache** ‚Äì Stores JIT-compiled native machine code.

---

### 2. Difference between Heap and Stack?

| Heap                  | Stack                                    |
| --------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Stores objects        | Stores method frames and local variables |
| Shared by all threads | One stack per thread                     |
| Garbage collected     | Automatically freed when method returns  |
| Larger memory         | Smaller memory                           |
| Slower access         | Very fast access                         |

---

### 3. What is Metaspace?

Metaspace is the JVM memory area that stores **class metadata**, method information, runtime constant pools, and bytecode-related data. Unlike PermGen, it uses **native memory**.

---

### 4. What is the Program Counter (PC) Register?

The PC Register is a **thread-private** memory area that stores the address of the current JVM instruction being executed.

---

# Intermediate

### 5. Why is the heap shared?

Because objects are often accessed by multiple threads. A shared heap allows threads to communicate by referencing the same objects.

---

### 6. Why does each thread need its own stack?

Each thread has its own execution flow. Separate stacks isolate local variables, method calls, and return addresses, making execution thread-safe.

---

### 7. What is stored in the Code Cache?

The Code Cache stores **JIT-compiled native machine code**, allowing the JVM to execute frequently used methods much faster than interpreting bytecode.

---

### 8. What is the Native Method Stack?

The Native Method Stack supports execution of methods written in native languages (such as C/C++) that are invoked through **JNI (Java Native Interface)**.

---

# Advanced

### 9. Why was PermGen replaced with Metaspace?

PermGen had a fixed size, leading to frequent `OutOfMemoryError`. Metaspace uses native memory and grows dynamically (up to a configurable limit), reducing memory issues.

---

### 10. Why isn't the stack garbage collected?

Stack memory follows **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)**. Method frames are automatically removed when methods return, so garbage collection is unnecessary.

---

### 11. Can Metaspace run out of memory?

Yes. If many classes are loaded (or class loaders leak) and native memory is exhausted or `MaxMetaspaceSize` is reached, the JVM throws `OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace`.

---

### 12. How do Virtual Threads affect JVM memory usage?

Virtual Threads require **much smaller stacks** than platform threads because their stacks grow and shrink dynamically. This allows millions of concurrent threads with significantly lower memory usage.

---

### 13. How does JIT interact with the Code Cache?

The **JIT compiler** identifies frequently executed ("hot") methods, compiles their bytecode into native machine code, and stores the compiled code in the **Code Cache** for faster execution on subsequent calls.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 2 ‚Äì Object Creation & Memory Allocation)

This is where the real JVM internals begin. We'll explore:

* What actually happens when the JVM executes `new`
* Eden Space
* TLAB (Thread Local Allocation Buffer)
* Fast-path vs slow-path allocation
* Escape Analysis
* Stack allocation optimizations
* Object headers (Mark Word & Klass Pointer)
* Memory alignment
* Allocation failures
* Real allocation flow inside HotSpot JVM

This phase explains **how an object travels from source code to memory**, which is fundamental to understanding how garbage collectors operate.

pages.title: Object Creation & Memory Allocation
pages.text:
Excellent. This is where we move from **JVM architecture** to **HotSpot JVM internals**.

This is one of the **most important topics** for Staff Engineer and Architect interviews because it explains **what actually happens when Java executes `new`**.

Many developers think:

```java
Customer customer = new Customer();
```

is just "create an object."

In reality, the JVM performs **15‚Äì20 internal steps** before that object is ready.

---

# Garbage Collectors Deep Dive

# Phase 2 ‚Äì Object Creation & Memory Allocation

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* What happens internally when `new` is executed
* Class loading check
* Eden Space
* TLAB (Thread Local Allocation Buffer)
* Fast-path allocation
* Slow-path allocation
* Object Header
* Mark Word
* Klass Pointer
* Memory Alignment
* Constructor execution
* Escape Analysis
* Stack Allocation
* Scalar Replacement
* Interview questions

---

# 1. The Journey Begins

Suppose we write:

```java
Customer customer = new Customer();
```

This simple statement triggers many JVM operations.

Conceptually:

```
Source Code
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Bytecode
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Class Loaded?
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Memory Allocated
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Object Header Initialized
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Fields Initialized
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Constructor Executes
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Reference Returned
```

---

# 2. Bytecode

The compiler generates bytecode similar to:

```text
new Customer
dup
invokespecial <init>
astore_1
```

Meaning:

```
Allocate memory

‚Üì

Duplicate reference

‚Üì

Run constructor

‚Üì

Store reference
```

Interview Tip:

The **`new` bytecode does not itself execute the constructor**. Constructor invocation is a separate bytecode instruction (`invokespecial`).

---

# 3. Step 1 ‚Äì Is the Class Loaded?

Before allocation, JVM checks:

```
Customer class loaded?

YES

‚Üì

Allocate Object

NO

‚Üì

Class Loader

‚Üì

Verification

‚Üì

Preparation

‚Üì

Resolution

‚Üì

Initialization

‚Üì

Allocate Object
```

If the class isn't loaded, the JVM performs the class loading lifecycle first.

---

# 4. Step 2 ‚Äì Where Is Memory Allocated?

Almost every new object starts in:

```
Heap

‚Üì

Young Generation

‚Üì

Eden Space
```

Not directly in the Old Generation (except in some special cases).

---

# 5. Why Eden?

Java applications create enormous numbers of temporary objects.

Example:

```java
String s = "A" + value;
```

Temporary objects are created.

Most die quickly.

Research behind the JVM showed:

> **Most objects die young.**

This is called the **Weak Generational Hypothesis**.

That observation drives the design of generational garbage collectors.

---

# 6. Heap Layout

Conceptually:

```
Heap

‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
‚îÇ Young Generation             ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                              ‚îÇ
‚îÇ  Eden                        ‚îÇ
‚îÇ  Survivor 0                  ‚îÇ
‚îÇ  Survivor 1                  ‚îÇ
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò

‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
‚îÇ Old Generation               ‚îÇ
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
```

Every allocation begins in Eden (unless optimized away or subject to special allocation rules).

---

# 7. Why Not Allocate Directly in Old Generation?

Because most objects are short-lived.

Example:

```java
calculate()
```

creates:

```
Customer

Address

Response

DTO

Temporary List
```

All disappear in milliseconds.

Putting them into Old Generation would increase Full GC frequency.

---

# 8. Thread Local Allocation Buffer (TLAB)

This is one of the most important JVM optimizations.

Without TLAB:

```
Thread A

‚Üì

Heap

‚Üë

Thread B

‚Üì

Heap

‚Üë

Thread C
```

Every allocation requires coordination.

That would become a bottleneck.

---

# 9. TLAB Solution

Each thread receives a small private allocation buffer inside Eden.

```
Eden

‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
‚îÇ                            ‚îÇ
‚îÇ TLAB A                     ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                            ‚îÇ
‚îÇ TLAB B                     ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                            ‚îÇ
‚îÇ TLAB C                     ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                            ‚îÇ
```
