* Memory fragmentation
* Floating garbage
* Concurrent Mode Failure
* Higher CPU usage
* More complex implementation
* Difficult tuning
* Removed from modern JDKs

---

# 20. Why Was CMS Removed?

Main reasons:

### Fragmentation

```
Used

Free

Used

Free

Used
```

No compaction.

---

### Concurrent Mode Failure

Unexpected long pauses.

---

### Complexity

Many corner cases.

Difficult maintenance.

---

### Better Alternative

```
G1 GC
```

G1 provided:

* Region-based heap
* Compaction
* Predictable pauses
* Better scalability

CMS became unnecessary.

---

# 21. Serial vs Parallel vs CMS

| Feature       | Serial       | Parallel              | CMS                   |
| ------------- | ------------ | --------------------- | --------------------- |
| STW           | Yes          | Yes                   | Mostly No             |
| Concurrent    | No           | No                    | Yes                   |
| Young GC      | Copying      | Parallel Copying      | ParNew (historically) |
| Old GC        | Mark-Compact | Parallel Mark-Compact | Mark-Sweep            |
| Fragmentation | No           | No                    | Yes                   |
| Throughput    | Low          | High                  | Medium                |
| Latency       | High         | Medium                | Low                   |

---

# 22. CMS vs G1

| Feature            | CMS     | G1                      |
| ------------------ | ------- | ----------------------- |
| Concurrent Mark    | Yes     | Yes                     |
| Concurrent Sweep   | Yes     | Region-based evacuation |
| Compaction         | No      | Yes                     |
| Fragmentation      | Yes     | No (normal operation)   |
| Predictable Pauses | Limited | Better                  |
| Java 21            | Removed | Default                 |

---

# 23. Real Production Example

Imagine a telecom alarm management system.

Requirements:

* Alarm latency < 100 ms
* Thousands of events per second

With Parallel GC:

```
Pause

800 ms

‚Üì

Missed SLA
```

CMS:

```
Pause

25 ms

‚Üì

Continue

‚Üì

Concurrent Cleanup
```

This was why CMS became popular before G1.

---

# 24. Why G1 Replaced CMS

CMS:

```
Mark

‚Üì

Sweep

‚Üì

Fragmentation
```

G1:

```
Mark

‚Üì

Copy Live Objects

‚Üì

Compact

‚Üì

No Fragmentation
```

G1 also performs much of its work concurrently while maintaining better memory organization.

---

# 25. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå "CMS has no Stop-The-World."

Incorrect.

CMS pauses during:

* Initial Mark
* Remark

---

### ‚ùå "CMS compacts memory."

No.

It uses **Mark-Sweep**, not Mark-Compact.

---

### ‚ùå "Concurrent means free."

Concurrent work consumes CPU resources.

The application and GC compete for CPU time.

---

### ‚ùå "CMS is available in Java 21."

No.

CMS was removed starting with **JDK 14**.

---

# 26. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What is CMS?
2. Why was CMS introduced?
3. What does CMS optimize?

---

## Intermediate

4. Explain the four CMS phases.
5. What is Concurrent Mark?
6. Why does CMS reduce pause time?
7. What is Floating Garbage?

---

## Advanced

8. Why does CMS suffer fragmentation?
9. What is Concurrent Mode Failure?
10. Why was CMS removed?
11. Compare CMS and G1.
12. Why doesn't CMS compact memory?
13. Explain CMS pause points.

---

# Architect Perspective

Imagine your Spring Boot application serves:

* 50,000 REST requests/minute
* 500 concurrent users
* 16 GB heap

Users care about:

```
Fast Response Time
```

not:

```
Maximum Batch Throughput
```

CMS improved latency by moving most GC work into concurrent phases.

However, over time:

```
Old Generation

‚Üì

Fragmentation

‚Üì

Allocation Failure

‚Üì

Concurrent Mode Failure

‚Üì

Long Full GC Pause
```

This unpredictability made CMS less suitable for modern cloud-native applications, leading to its replacement by G1.

---

# Summary Diagram

```
            Old Generation

Initial Mark (STW)
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Concurrent Mark
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Remark (STW)
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Concurrent Sweep
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Application Continues
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* CMS was the first widely used **low-pause collector** in HotSpot.
* Most of its work runs **concurrently** with the application.
* It uses **Mark-Sweep**, so it suffers from **memory fragmentation**.
* **Floating Garbage** and **Concurrent Mode Failure** were major drawbacks.
* CMS was removed because **G1 GC** delivered lower pause times with **compaction** and more predictable behavior.
* Understanding CMS is essential because many modern GC concepts evolved from it.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 10 ‚Äì G1 Garbage Collector (Java 21 Default))

This is the **most important collector** for Java 21 interviews.

We'll cover:

* Why G1 was created
* Region-based heap architecture
* Humongous objects
* Remembered Sets
* SATB (Snapshot-At-The-Beginning)
* Mixed GC
* Concurrent marking
* Evacuation pauses
* Pause-time prediction
* G1 tuning
* Reading G1 GC logs
* Real production tuning for Spring Boot microservices

This is the collector you should know in the greatest depth for Java 21 and modern backend interviews.

pages.title: G1 (Garbage First) Garbage Collector
pages.text:
Excellent. This is the **most important Garbage Collector for Java 21 interviews**.

> **If you learn only one GC in depth, learn G1 GC.**

Why?

* ‚úÖ **Default GC in Java 21**
* ‚úÖ Used by most Spring Boot microservices
* ‚úÖ Preferred for Kubernetes deployments
* ‚úÖ Frequently asked in Staff Engineer and Architect interviews
* ‚úÖ Foundation for understanding ZGC and Shenandoah

For interview purposes, this topic deserves the most attention.

---

# Garbage Collectors Deep Dive

# Phase 10 ‚Äì G1 (Garbage First) Garbage Collector

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* Why G1 was created
* Region-based heap architecture
* Young, Old, and Humongous regions
* Garbage First strategy
* SATB (Snapshot-At-The-Beginning)
* Remembered Sets
* Write Barriers
* Evacuation
* Mixed GC
* Pause-time prediction
* Humongous objects
* JVM tuning
* Reading G1 GC logs
* Java 21 interview questions

---

# 1. Why Was G1 Created?

Previous collectors had trade-offs:

| Collector | Problem                                |
| --------- | -------------------------------------- |
| Serial    | Long pauses                            |
| Parallel  | High throughput but long pauses        |
| CMS       | Fragmentation, Concurrent Mode Failure |

Oracle wanted:

* Predictable pauses
* Good throughput
* Large heap support
* Automatic compaction
* Concurrent operation

Result:

## G1 (Garbage First)

---

# 2. The Biggest Difference

Previous collectors divide heap into:

```text
Young

Old
```

G1 divides heap into:

```text
‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°‚ñ°
```

called

## Regions

---

# 3. Region-Based Heap

Instead of one large Young area:

```text
+----------------------------------+
| Young                            |
+----------------------------------+
| Old                              |
+----------------------------------+
```

G1:

```text
+----+----+----+----+----+----+
| R1 | R2 | R3 | R4 | R5 | R6 |
+----+----+----+----+----+----+

+----+----+----+----+----+----+
| R7 | R8 | R9 |R10 |R11 |R12 |
+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

Each region is typically **1 MB to 32 MB**, depending on heap size.

---

# 4. Region Types

A region can dynamically become:

```text
Young

Old

Humongous

Free
```

Unlike older collectors, regions are **not permanently assigned**.

Today:

```text
Region 5

Young
```

Tomorrow:

```text
Region 5

Old
```

---

# 5. Garbage First Philosophy

Suppose:

```text
Region 1

95% Garbage

Region 2

20% Garbage

Region 3

80% Garbage
```

Question:

Which region should be collected first?

Answer:

```text
Region 1
```

Hence the name:

## Garbage First

Collect regions with the most reclaimable memory first.

---

# 6. Young GC

Young regions:

```text
R1

R2

R3
```

Objects:

```text
A

B

C

D
```

Survivors:

```text
A

D
```

G1 copies survivors to new regions.

Old Young regions become free.

This process is called:

## Evacuation

---

# 7. Evacuation

Instead of:

```text
Mark

Sweep
```

G1 performs:

```text
Copy Live Objects

‚Üì

Free Entire Region
```

Example:

Before:

```text
Region 1

A

B

C
```

After:

```text
Region 7

A

C
```

Region 1 becomes:

```text
Free
```

This automatically avoids fragmentation.

---

# 8. Remembered Sets

Question:

How does G1 know which regions reference each other?

Each region maintains a:

## Remembered Set (RSet)

Example:

```text
Region A

‚Üì

Region C
```

RSet for Region C records:

```text
Referenced by

Region A
```

During collection, G1 scans only the relevant remembered sets instead of the entire heap.

---

# 9. Write Barrier

When code executes:

```java
customer.order = order;
```

The JVM executes a **write barrier**.

Conceptually:

```text
Reference Update

‚Üì

Write Barrier

‚Üì

Update Remembered Set
```

This keeps cross-region reference information up to date.

---

# 10. SATB (Snapshot-At-The-Beginning)

This is one of the most frequently asked G1 interview topics.

During concurrent marking:

```text
Application

Running

+

GC

Running
```

The object graph changes.

To avoid inconsistency, G1 logically marks objects based on a **snapshot of reachability at the beginning of the marking cycle**.

This algorithm is called:

## SATB (Snapshot-At-The-Beginning)

Objects that were reachable in the initial snapshot are treated as live for that marking cycle, even if references change later.

---

# 11. Concurrent Mark Cycle

G1 performs:

```text
Initial Mark (STW)

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Remark (STW)

‚Üì

Cleanup
```

Notice the similarity to CMS.

However:

G1 later performs evacuation and compaction.

---

# 12. Mixed GC

One of G1's biggest innovations.

Instead of collecting:

```text
Only Young
```

or

```text
Entire Old Generation
```

G1 collects:

```text
Young Regions

+

Selected Old Regions
```

Only the regions with high reclaimable garbage are included.

This is called:

## Mixed GC

---

# 13. Example

Heap:

```text
R1 Young

R2 Young

R3 Old (90% Garbage)

R4 Old (10% Garbage)

R5 Old (80% Garbage)
```

Mixed GC collects:

```text
R1

R2

R3

R5
```

R4 is skipped because reclaiming it would yield little benefit.

---

# 14. Humongous Objects

Suppose:

Region size:

```text
2 MB
```

Object:

```text
3 MB
```

It doesn't fit into a single region.

G1 allocates it into one or more contiguous **Humongous Regions**.

Examples:

* Large byte arrays
* Large image buffers
* Huge JSON payloads

Humongous allocations are more expensive and can impact GC behavior.

---

# 15. Pause-Time Prediction

Unique feature.

Developer specifies:

```bash
-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200
```

G1 estimates:

```text
Region A

10 ms

Region B

30 ms

Region C

50 ms

Region D

120 ms
```

It selects a collection set expected to fit within the target pause budget.

This is a **goal**, not a guarantee.

---

# 16. JVM Options

Enable G1:

```bash
-XX:+UseG1GC
```

Target pause:

```bash
-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200
```

Heap:

```bash
-Xms8g
-Xmx8g
```

GC logs:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*
```

---

# 17. Example G1 Log

```text
[2.140s][info][gc]
Pause Young (Normal)
256M->80M(2G)
```

Mixed GC:

```text
Pause Young (Mixed)
```

Concurrent mark:

```text
Concurrent Mark Cycle
```

These entries are common when reading G1 logs.

---

# 18. Internal Flow

```text
Allocation

‚Üì

Young Regions Full

‚Üì

Young GC

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Mixed GC

‚Üì

Continue
```

---

# 19. Advantages

* Default in Java 21
* Predictable pause goals
* Automatic compaction
* No long-term fragmentation
* Excellent for medium and large heaps
* Suitable for microservices
* Region-based design

---

# 20. Disadvantages

* More complex than Parallel GC
* Slightly lower peak throughput in some batch workloads
* Humongous objects can affect performance
* Pause targets are goals, not guarantees

---

# 21. Parallel vs G1

| Feature          | Parallel   | G1                            |
| ---------------- | ---------- | ----------------------------- |
| Goal             | Throughput | Balanced latency + throughput |
| Heap Layout      | Young/Old  | Regions                       |
| Compaction       | Yes        | Yes (evacuation)              |
| Concurrent Mark  | No         | Yes                           |
| Pause Prediction | No         | Yes                           |
| Java 21 Default  | No         | Yes                           |

---

# 22. CMS vs G1

| Feature       | CMS     | G1      |
| ------------- | ------- | ------- |
| Fragmentation | Yes     | No      |
| Compaction    | No      | Yes     |
| Mixed GC      | No      | Yes     |
| Region-Based  | No      | Yes     |
| Java 21       | Removed | Default |

---

# 23. Production Example

Spring Boot microservice:

* Heap = 8 GB
* 2,000 requests/sec
* Kafka
* Redis
* PostgreSQL

Thousands of request-scoped objects are created every second.

G1:

```text
Young Regions

‚Üì

Evacuate Survivors

‚Üì

Free Regions

‚Üì

Occasional Mixed GC

‚Üì

Low Pause Times
```

This is why G1 is a strong default choice for modern backend services.

---

# 24. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå "G1 eliminates all Stop-The-World pauses."

No.

STW still occurs during:

* Initial Mark
* Remark
* Evacuation pauses

The goal is to keep these pauses relatively short.

---

### ‚ùå "G1 guarantees `MaxGCPauseMillis`."

No.

It is a **soft target**, not a hard guarantee.

---

### ‚ùå "G1 has Young and Old spaces."

Logically yes.

Physically, the heap is composed of **regions**, and regions are dynamically assigned roles.

---

### ‚ùå "Remembered Sets are Card Tables."

They are related but not identical.

* **Card Tables** track modified memory areas.
* **Remembered Sets** provide region-level cross-reference information used by G1.

---

# 25. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. Why was G1 created?
2. Why is it called Garbage First?
3. What is a region?

---

## Intermediate

4. What is Mixed GC?
5. What is evacuation?
6. What are Humongous Objects?
7. What is a Remembered Set?

---

## Advanced

8. Explain SATB.
9. How does G1 avoid fragmentation?
10. Why is G1 better than CMS?
11. How does G1 predict pause time?
12. Explain region selection during Mixed GC.
13. Why are Humongous Objects challenging?

---

# Architect Perspective

Imagine your telecom analytics platform:

* Java 21
* Spring Boot
* Kubernetes
* 16 GB heap
* 10,000 REST requests/minute
* Kafka consumers
* Prometheus metrics

Requirements:

* Low latency
* Stable response times
* Automatic heap management
* Good throughput

G1 achieves this by:

```text
Region-Based Heap

‚Üì

Concurrent Marking

‚Üì

Remembered Sets

‚Üì

Evacuation

‚Üì

Mixed GC

‚Üì

Predictable Pause Targets
```

This balance between throughput and latency is why G1 became the default collector for modern server-side Java.

---

# Summary Diagram

```text
                 Heap (Regions)

+----+----+----+----+----+----+
| Y  | Y  | O  | O  | F  | H  |
+----+----+----+----+----+----+
| O  | Y  | O  | F  | O  | Y  |
+----+----+----+----+----+----+

Y = Young
O = Old
F = Free
H = Humongous

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Concurrent Mark

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Garbage-First Region Selection

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Evacuation (Copy Live Objects)

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Free Regions

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Application Continues
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* **G1 GC** is the **default garbage collector in Java 21**.
* It divides the heap into **regions**, not fixed Young and Old memory spaces.
* **Garbage First** means collecting regions with the highest amount of reclaimable memory.
* **Evacuation** copies live objects and frees entire regions, preventing fragmentation.
* **Remembered Sets** and **Write Barriers** efficiently track cross-region references.
* **SATB** enables accurate concurrent marking while the application continues running.
* **Mixed GC** allows G1 to reclaim both Young regions and selected Old regions in the same cycle.
* `-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis` is a **pause-time goal**, helping G1 balance latency and throughput rather than guaranteeing a specific pause duration.

---

## Next Phase (Phase 11 ‚Äì ZGC)

Now we'll study the **most advanced low-latency collector in Java 21**.

Topics include:

* Colored pointers
* Load barriers
* Concurrent relocation
* Pointer remapping
* Multi-terabyte heap support
* Sub-millisecond pause goals
* NUMA awareness
* Generational ZGC (Java 21)
* ZGC tuning
* G1 vs ZGC comparison
* Production use cases for ultra-low-latency systems

This is one of the hottest JVM topics in modern Java architecture interviews.

pages.title: ZGC (Z Garbage Collector)
pages.text:
Excellent. Now we reach the **most advanced production garbage collector in Java 21**.

> **ZGC is one of the most frequently discussed Java 21 interview topics**, especially for Staff Engineer, Principal Engineer, Architect, and JVM-focused interviews.

Unlike G1, which tries to **minimize pauses**, ZGC was designed to **virtually eliminate long GC pauses**.

---

# Garbage Collectors Deep Dive

# Phase 11 ‚Äì ZGC (Z Garbage Collector)

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* Why ZGC was created
* ZGC architecture
* Concurrent GC model
* Colored Pointers
* Load Barriers
* Concurrent Mark
* Concurrent Relocation
* Pointer Remapping
* NUMA awareness
* Generational ZGC (Java 21)
* JVM tuning
* Production use cases
* Interview questions

---

# 1. Why Was ZGC Created?

Even G1 has pauses.

Example:

```text
Heap = 128 GB

G1 Pause

150‚Äì300 ms
```

For applications like:

* Stock Trading
* Telecom Control Plane
* Online Gaming
* Fraud Detection
* AI Inference
* Payment Systems

Even **100 ms** can be unacceptable.

Oracle's goal:

> **Keep pause times nearly constant, even with very large heaps.**

---

# 2. Design Goals

ZGC aims for:

* Pause times typically in the **sub-10 ms** range (often much lower)
* Heap sizes from a few GB to multiple TB
* Concurrent garbage collection
* Automatic compaction
* Excellent scalability

Unlike Parallel GC:

```text
Application

STOP

‚Üì

GC
```

ZGC performs almost all work while the application continues running.

---

# 3. High-Level Architecture

```text
Application Threads

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Load Barrier

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Concurrent GC Threads

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Mark

Relocate

Remap
```

Notice:

The application rarely waits for GC.

---

# 4. Heap Layout

Like G1:

ZGC also divides memory into regions (called pages internally).

```text
+------+------+------+------+
|Page1 |Page2 |Page3 |Page4 |
+------+------+------+------+
```

Pages can be:

* Small
* Medium
* Large

depending on allocation size.

---

# 5. Traditional GC Problem

Suppose object:

```java
Customer c = new Customer();
```

moves from:

```text
Address A

‚Üì

Address B
```

Application thread:

```text
Still Reading Address A
```

Older collectors often require stopping the application while references are updated.

---

# 6. ZGC Solution

Instead of stopping the world:

ZGC lets objects move **concurrently**.

Application threads automatically discover the new location.

This is the key innovation.

---

# 7. Colored Pointers

This is the most famous ZGC interview topic.

Traditional pointer:

```text
0xABC12345
```

ZGC pointer:

```text
Metadata + Address
```

Conceptually:

```text
+-----------------------------+
| Mark Bits | State | Address |
+-----------------------------+
```

The pointer itself carries metadata.

This is called a **Colored Pointer**.

> Internally, ZGC uses unused address bits on supported 64-bit architectures to encode metadata.

---

# 8. Why Colored Pointers?

Suppose:

Object moved.

Traditional GC:

```text
Pause

‚Üì

Update Every Reference
```

ZGC:

```text
Reference Used

‚Üì

Metadata Checked

‚Üì

Automatically Resolve New Address
```

No long global pause is required.

---

# 9. Load Barrier

This is the heart of ZGC.

Every object read conceptually passes through a **Load Barrier**.

Example:

```java
customer.getAddress();
```

Conceptually:

```text
Read Reference

‚Üì

Load Barrier

‚Üì

Correct Address

‚Üì

Return Object
```

If the object has moved:

The barrier transparently updates the reference.

---

# 10. Load Barrier Example

Without Load Barrier:

```text
Reference

‚Üì

Old Address

‚Üì

Crash / Invalid Access
```

With Load Barrier:

```text
Old Address

‚Üì

Barrier

‚Üì

New Address

‚Üì

Continue
```

The application never notices the relocation.

---

# 11. Concurrent Mark

Like G1:

```text
Initial Mark

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Remark
```

Most marking occurs while the application continues running.

---

# 12. Concurrent Relocation

Instead of:

```text
Pause

‚Üì

Move Objects
```

ZGC performs:

```text
Application Running

+

Move Objects

At Same Time
```

This is one of ZGC's defining capabilities.

---

# 13. Pointer Remapping

Suppose:

Object moved:

```text
Address A

‚Üì

Address B
```

Some references still point to:

```text
Address A
```

When accessed:

```text
Load Barrier

‚Üì

Reference Updated

‚Üì

Continue
```

References are gradually corrected during normal execution.

---

# 14. Stop-The-World Phases

ZGC is **not completely pause-free**.

Very short pauses still occur.

Typically:

* Pause Mark Start
* Pause Mark End
* Pause Relocate Start

These pauses are intentionally kept very short.

---

# 15. Generational ZGC (Java 21)

This is a major enhancement.

Earlier ZGC versions were:

```text
Non-Generational
```

Java 21 introduced:

```text
Generational ZGC
```

Benefits:

* Better handling of short-lived objects
* Lower CPU overhead
* Better throughput
* Faster collections

It combines:

* Generational GC principles
* ZGC's ultra-low latency

---

# 16. NUMA Awareness

Modern servers:

```text
CPU 1

Local Memory

CPU 2

Local Memory
```

Remote memory access is slower.

ZGC is NUMA-aware and tries to keep memory access local to improve performance.

This matters on large enterprise servers.

---

# 17. JVM Options

Enable ZGC:

```bash
-XX:+UseZGC
```

Enable Generational ZGC (Java 21):

```bash
-XX:+ZGenerational
```

Heap:

```bash
-Xms16g
-Xmx16g
```

GC Logs:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*
```

---

# 18. Example Log

```text
Pause Mark Start

0.45 ms

Concurrent Mark

Pause Mark End

0.60 ms

Concurrent Relocate
```

Notice:

Pauses are extremely short.

---

# 19. Advantages

* Extremely low pause times
* Heap sizes from GBs to TBs
* Automatic compaction
* Concurrent relocation
* Excellent scalability
* Suitable for latency-critical systems
* Generational mode improves efficiency

---

# 20. Disadvantages

* Higher CPU usage due to concurrent work
* More complex implementation
* Peak throughput may be slightly lower than Parallel GC
* Requires understanding of concurrent behavior when tuning

---

# 21. G1 vs ZGC

| Feature               | G1        | ZGC               |
| --------------------- | --------- | ----------------- |
| Default in Java 21    | ‚úÖ Yes     | ‚ùå No              |
| Target                | Balanced  | Ultra-low latency |
| Heap                  | Regions   | Pages/Regions     |
| Concurrent Mark       | Yes       | Yes               |
| Concurrent Relocation | No        | Yes               |
| Colored Pointers      | No        | Yes               |
| Load Barrier          | No        | Yes               |
| Pause Time            | Low       | Extremely Low     |
| Large Heap            | Excellent | Outstanding       |

---

# 22. Parallel vs G1 vs ZGC

| Feature    | Parallel | G1     | ZGC   |
| ---------- | -------- | ------ | ----- |
| Throughput | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   |
| Pause Time | ‚≠ê        | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê |
| Large Heap | ‚≠ê‚≠ê       | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê |
| Complexity | Low      | Medium | High  |

---

# 23. Production Example

Imagine your telecom analytics platform:

* 64 GB heap
* 500 Kafka partitions
* 50,000 messages/sec
* REST APIs
* Prometheus metrics
* SLA: Response < 20 ms

With Parallel GC:

```text
GC Pause

500 ms
```

With G1:

```text
GC Pause

40 ms
```

With ZGC:

```text
GC Pause

< 2 ms (typical)
```

The application remains responsive even during garbage collection.

---

# 24. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå "ZGC has no Stop-The-World."

Incorrect.

ZGC still has STW phases.

They are simply **very short**.

---

### ‚ùå "Colored Pointers store the object."

No.

They store metadata alongside the memory address.

---

### ‚ùå "Load Barriers slow every object access dramatically."

They add overhead, but they are highly optimized and are fundamental to ZGC's concurrent relocation design.

---

### ‚ùå "ZGC is always better than G1."

Not always.

For many microservices:

* 2‚Äì8 GB heap
* Typical REST APIs

G1 is often simpler and provides excellent performance.

ZGC shines when **latency requirements are extremely strict** or heap sizes become very large.

---

# 25. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. Why was ZGC created?
2. What problem does it solve?
3. What is a Colored Pointer?

---

## Intermediate

4. Explain Load Barriers.
5. What is Concurrent Relocation?
6. Why does ZGC have low pauses?
7. What is Pointer Remapping?

---

## Advanced

8. Explain Colored Pointer internals conceptually.
9. How does Generational ZGC improve performance?
10. Compare G1 and ZGC.
11. Why is ZGC NUMA-aware?
12. When would you choose ZGC over G1?

---

# Architect Perspective

Suppose your organization operates:

* High-frequency telecom signaling
* Real-time charging
* Fraud detection
* Payment authorization

Requirements:

* 99.99% availability
* Response time under 10 ms
* Heap size = 64 GB

ZGC's architecture:

```text
Application Threads

‚Üì

Load Barrier

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Concurrent Relocation

‚Üì

Pointer Remapping

‚Üì

Continue Processing
```

means garbage collection rarely causes noticeable latency spikes.

For a typical Spring Boot CRUD service with a 4 GB heap, however, G1 is usually the better default because it offers a strong balance between throughput, latency, and operational simplicity.

---

# Summary Diagram

```text
               Heap

+------+------+------+------+
| P1   | P2   | P3   | P4   |
+------+------+------+------+

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Concurrent Mark

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Concurrent Relocate

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Load Barrier

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Pointer Remapping

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Application Continues
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* **ZGC** is designed for **ultra-low latency** with pause times that remain very small even on large heaps.
* It uses **Colored Pointers** and **Load Barriers** to enable **concurrent object relocation**.
* **Pointer Remapping** allows references to be updated lazily as objects are accessed.
* **Generational ZGC**, introduced in Java 21, combines generational collection with ZGC's concurrent architecture for better throughput and lower CPU usage.
* ZGC is ideal for **large heaps and latency-sensitive systems**, while **G1 remains the default and best general-purpose collector** for most Java 21 server applications.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 12 ‚Äì Shenandoah Garbage Collector)

We'll cover another state-of-the-art low-latency collector:

* Why Shenandoah was created
* Brooks Pointers
* Read/Load barriers
* Concurrent evacuation
* Concurrent compaction
* Shenandoah vs ZGC
* Shenandoah vs G1
* Performance trade-offs
* JVM tuning
* Interview-focused comparisons

After completing Phase 12, we'll conclude with a comprehensive comparison of **all major Java garbage collectors**, including guidance on choosing the right collector for different production workloads and interview scenarios.

pages.title: Shenandoah Garbage Collector
pages.text:
Excellent. We have now reached the final production GC in Java 21.

Like **ZGC**, **Shenandoah** is an **ultra-low latency garbage collector**, but it achieves this goal using a **completely different design**.

This is a favorite topic in **JVM internals**, **Principal Engineer**, **Performance Engineer**, and **Architect** interviews because comparing **ZGC vs Shenandoah** demonstrates deep JVM knowledge.

---

# Garbage Collectors Deep Dive

# Phase 12 ‚Äì Shenandoah Garbage Collector

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* Why Shenandoah was created
* Shenandoah architecture
* Brooks Pointer
* Read/Load barriers
* Concurrent evacuation
* Concurrent compaction
* Region-based heap
* SATB
* JVM tuning
* Shenandoah vs G1
* Shenandoah vs ZGC
* Production use cases
* Interview questions

---

# 1. Why Was Shenandoah Created?

Even G1 still has evacuation pauses.

Example:

```text
Heap = 64 GB

Young GC

‚Üì

Pause

40 ms
```

For applications such as:

* Telecom Core Networks
* Trading
* Payment Systems
* Low-latency APIs

Even tens of milliseconds may be too much.

Red Hat introduced **Shenandoah** with the goal:

> **Move almost all garbage collection work off the Stop-The-World path, including object evacuation.**

---

# 2. Design Goal

Like ZGC:

* Extremely low pause times
* Large heaps
* Automatic compaction
* Concurrent relocation

Unlike G1:

```text
Pause

‚Üì

Copy Objects
```

Shenandoah performs:

```text
Application Running

+

Copy Objects
```

---

# 3. High-Level Architecture

```text
Application Threads

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Read Barrier

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Concurrent GC

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Mark

Evacuate

Update References
```

Most work occurs while the application continues running.

---

# 4. Region-Based Heap

Like G1:

```text
+------+------+------+------+
| R1   | R2   | R3   | R4   |
+------+------+------+------+
```

Regions may contain:

* Young objects (in generational mode)
* Old objects
* Free space

Shenandoah can reclaim regions independently.

---

# 5. Traditional GC Problem

Suppose:

```java
Customer customer = new Customer();
```

moves from:

```text
Address A

‚Üì

Address B
```

Application thread:

```text
Still Accessing

Address A
```

Traditional collectors usually require a pause to make references consistent.

---

# 6. Shenandoah Solution

Shenandoah introduces:

## Brooks Pointer

Instead of every reference pointing directly to the object:

```text
Reference

‚Üì

Object
```

Objects contain an extra forwarding pointer.

```text
Reference

‚Üì

Object Header

‚Üì

Brooks Pointer

‚Üì

Actual Object
```

This forwarding pointer allows the object to move while references remain valid.

---

# 7. Brooks Pointer Example

Before movement:

```text
Reference

‚Üì

Object A
```

Brooks Pointer:

```text
Object A

‚Üì

Object A
```

After relocation:

```text
Reference

‚Üì

Old Object Header

‚Üì

Brooks Pointer

‚Üì

New Object
```

The application transparently reaches the relocated object.

---

# 8. Read Barrier

Every object read conceptually passes through a barrier.

Example:

```java
customer.getAddress();
```

Conceptually:

```text
Read Reference

‚Üì

Read Barrier

‚Üì

Check Brooks Pointer

‚Üì

Return Current Object
```

If relocation has occurred, the barrier follows the forwarding pointer.

---

# 9. Concurrent Mark

Like G1 and ZGC:

```text
Initial Mark

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Final Mark
```

Most marking happens concurrently with application execution.

---

# 10. Concurrent Evacuation

One of Shenandoah's defining innovations.

Instead of:

```text
Pause

‚Üì

Move Objects
```

Shenandoah performs:

```text
Application Running

+

Move Objects
```

References remain valid through the Brooks Pointer.

---

# 11. Concurrent Reference Update

After relocation:

Some references still point to the old object header.

Shenandoah gradually updates them:

```text
Old Reference

‚Üì

Read Barrier

‚Üì

New Object

‚Üì

Reference Updated
```

This work also happens concurrently.

---

# 12. SATB

Like G1:

Shenandoah uses:

## SATB (Snapshot At The Beginning)

This ensures that concurrent marking observes a consistent logical view of the object graph.

---

# 13. Stop-The-World Phases

Shenandoah still pauses briefly for:

* Initial Mark
* Final Mark
* Initial Update References (depending on mode)

These pauses are designed to remain very short.

---

# 14. JVM Options

Enable Shenandoah:

```bash
-XX:+UseShenandoahGC
```

Generational Shenandoah (Java 21):

```bash
-XX:ShenandoahGCMode=generational
```

GC logs:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*
```

Heap:

```bash
-Xms16g
-Xmx16g
```

---

# 15. Example GC Log

```text
Pause Init Mark

0.4 ms

Concurrent Mark

Concurrent Evacuation

Concurrent Update Refs

Pause Final Mark

0.5 ms
```

Notice:

Most work is concurrent.

---

# 16. Advantages

* Extremely low pause times
* Concurrent compaction
* Concurrent evacuation
* Large heap support
* Predictable latency
* Suitable for real-time workloads

---

# 17. Disadvantages

* Additional overhead from Brooks Pointer
* Read barrier overhead
* More complex implementation
* Higher CPU usage than throughput-oriented collectors
* Less widely deployed than G1

---

# 18. G1 vs Shenandoah

| Feature               | G1                       | Shenandoah |
| --------------------- | ------------------------ | ---------- |
| Default Java 21       | ‚úÖ                        | ‚ùå          |
| Concurrent Mark       | Yes                      | Yes        |
| Concurrent Evacuation | No                       | Yes        |
| Compaction            | During evacuation pauses | Concurrent |
| Pause Time            | Low                      | Very Low   |

---

# 19. ZGC vs Shenandoah

This is one of the most common advanced interview questions.

| Feature               | ZGC              | Shenandoah                   |
| --------------------- | ---------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Main Technique        | Colored Pointers | Brooks Pointer               |
| Barrier               | Load Barrier     | Read Barrier                 |
| Pointer Metadata      | In pointer bits  | Forwarding pointer in object |
| Concurrent Relocation | Yes              | Yes                          |
| Compaction            | Concurrent       | Concurrent                   |
| Pause Time            | Extremely Low    | Extremely Low                |

---

# 20. Brooks Pointer vs Colored Pointer

### ZGC

```text
Reference

‚Üì

Metadata

‚Üì

Address
```

Metadata is embedded in the pointer.

---

### Shenandoah

```text
Reference

‚Üì

Object

‚Üì

Brooks Pointer

‚Üì

Current Object
```

Metadata is effectively managed through the forwarding pointer in the object.

---

# 21. Production Example

Suppose your telecom platform:

* Heap = 128 GB
* Millions of metrics
* Continuous Kafka processing
* SLA = 5 ms

Shenandoah:

```text
Application Running

+

Concurrent Mark

+

Concurrent Evacuation

+

Concurrent Update References
```

The application continues processing while GC performs most of its work.

---

# 22. Common Misconceptions

### ‚ùå "Shenandoah has zero pauses."

Incorrect.

It still has short STW phases.

---

### ‚ùå "Brooks Pointer is the same as Colored Pointer."

No.

* ZGC stores metadata in pointer bits.
* Shenandoah stores a forwarding pointer in the object.

---

### ‚ùå "Concurrent GC means no CPU cost."

Incorrect.

Concurrent GC uses CPU while the application is running.

---

### ‚ùå "Shenandoah is always better than G1."

Not necessarily.

For many Spring Boot applications with moderate heaps:

G1 is simpler and usually provides excellent performance.

---

# 23. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. Why was Shenandoah created?
2. What is a Brooks Pointer?
3. What is a Read Barrier?

---

## Intermediate

4. Explain Concurrent Evacuation.
5. Why does Shenandoah have low pause times?
6. Compare Shenandoah with G1.

---

## Advanced

7. Brooks Pointer vs Colored Pointer.
8. Compare ZGC and Shenandoah.
9. Why are Read Barriers needed?
10. Explain Concurrent Reference Update.
11. Explain SATB in Shenandoah.
12. When would you choose Shenandoah?

---

# Architect Perspective

Imagine your deployment:

* Java 21
* Kubernetes
* 64-core server
* Heap = 128 GB
* Telecom analytics platform
* Continuous Kafka ingestion
* Strict latency SLA

Shenandoah enables:

```text
Application Threads

‚Üì

Read Barrier

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Concurrent Evacuation

‚Üì

Concurrent Reference Update

‚Üì

Application Continues
```

The application remains highly responsive even during garbage collection.

However, for a typical microservice:

* Heap = 4‚Äì8 GB
* REST APIs
* PostgreSQL
* Redis
* Kafka

**G1 GC** remains the recommended default due to its maturity, simplicity, and balanced performance.

---

# Summary Diagram

```text
               Heap (Regions)

+------+------+------+------+
| R1   | R2   | R3   | R4   |
+------+------+------+------+

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Concurrent Mark

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Concurrent Evacuation

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Brooks Pointer

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Read Barrier

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Concurrent Update References

        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº

Application Continues
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* **Shenandoah** is a **low-latency, concurrent compacting collector** designed to minimize pause times.
* It uses a **Brooks Pointer** (forwarding pointer) rather than **Colored Pointers**.
* **Read Barriers** ensure that application threads always reach the current location of relocated objects.
* Most GC work‚Äîmarking, evacuation, and reference updates‚Äîruns concurrently with the application.
* **Generational Shenandoah** in Java 21 combines these techniques with generational collection for improved efficiency.
* Shenandoah is an excellent choice for **large heaps with strict latency requirements**, though **G1** remains the most common choice for general-purpose Java 21 applications.

---

# We Have Completed All Major Garbage Collectors 🎉

You now understand:

1. ‚úÖ Serial GC
2. ‚úÖ Parallel GC
3. ‚úÖ CMS
4. ‚úÖ G1 GC
5. ‚úÖ ZGC
6. ‚úÖ Shenandoah GC

---

# Recommended Remaining GC Phases

To become interview-ready at the **Staff Engineer / Architect** level, I recommend these final phases:

| Phase  | Topic                                                                                              | Importance |
| ------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------- |
| **13** | Complete GC Algorithms (Mark-Sweep, Mark-Compact, Copying, Generational, SATB, Incremental Update) | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| **14** | GC Tuning (`-Xms`, `-Xmx`, `MaxGCPauseMillis`, `InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent`, etc.)             | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| **15** | GC Logging & Analysis (`-Xlog:gc`, log interpretation, GCViewer, GCeasy)                           | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| **16** | Heap Dumps & Memory Leak Analysis (MAT, VisualVM, `jcmd`, `jmap`)                                  | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| **17** | Production GC Tuning for Spring Boot Microservices                                                 | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| **18** | Java 21 GC Interview Questions (50+ Q&A from basic to architect level)                             | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| **19** | GC Decision Matrix (Which collector to choose and why)                                             | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |
| **20** | End-to-End Case Study: Debugging a Real Production GC Problem                                      | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      |

These final phases focus on **practical production skills** and the kinds of troubleshooting and design discussions that distinguish senior Java engineers in interviews.

pages.title: Complete GC Algorithms
pages.text:
Excellent. This is one of the **most important JVM theory phases**.

Many engineers memorize GC names (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah) but fail interviews because they **don't understand the underlying algorithms**.

Every Java garbage collector is built from a small set of fundamental algorithms.

Once you master these algorithms, every GC implementation becomes much easier to understand.

---

# Java 21 Garbage Collection Deep Dive

# Phase 13 ‚Äì Complete GC Algorithms

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* Why GC algorithms exist
* Mark-Sweep
* Mark-Compact
* Copying
* Reference Counting
* Generational GC
* SATB
* Incremental Update
* Tri-Color Marking
* Write Barrier
* Read Barrier
* Algorithm comparison
* Which collector uses which algorithm

---

# 1. Why Do We Need GC Algorithms?

Imagine a heap:

```text
+--------------------------------------+
| A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I |
+--------------------------------------+
```

Some objects are still reachable.

Some are garbage.

The JVM needs to answer:

1. Which objects are alive?
2. Which objects are garbage?
3. How should memory be reclaimed?
4. Should objects be moved?

Different algorithms answer these questions differently.

---

# 2. Major GC Algorithms

There are seven important algorithms/concepts:

```text
Reference Counting

‚Üì

Mark-Sweep

‚Üì

Mark-Compact

‚Üì

Copying

‚Üì

Generational Collection

‚Üì

SATB

‚Üì

Incremental Update
```

Let's study them one by one.

---

# 3. Reference Counting

The simplest algorithm.

Every object stores a counter.

Example:

```java
Customer c = new Customer();
Order o = new Order();

c.order = o;
```

Memory:

```text
Customer (count = 1)

‚Üì

Order (count = 1)
```

When another reference is added:

```java
Order another = o;
```

```text
Order (count = 2)
```

When references disappear:

```text
count = 0

‚Üì

Delete Object
```

---

## Problem: Circular References

```java
class A {
    B b;
}

class B {
    A a;
}
```

```text
A --> B
^     |
|_____|
```

Both reference each other.

Reference count:

```text
A = 1

B = 1
```

Even if no external references exist, they are never collected.

This is why HotSpot does **not** use reference counting as its primary GC algorithm.

---

# 4. Mark-Sweep Algorithm

Used by:

* CMS (Old Generation)

---

## Phase 1 ‚Äì Mark

Start from **GC Roots**.

```text
GC Root

‚Üì

A

‚Üì

B

‚Üì

C
```

Mark:

```text
A ‚úì

B ‚úì

C ‚úì
```

---

## Phase 2 ‚Äì Sweep

Delete everything unmarked.

Before:

```text
A B C D E F
```

Marked:

```text
A‚úì B‚úì D‚úì
```

After sweep:

```text
A B ‚ñ° D ‚ñ° ‚ñ°
```

---

## Advantages

* Simple
* Doesn't move objects

---

## Disadvantages

Leaves holes.

```text
A ‚ñ° B ‚ñ° C ‚ñ° D
```

This causes **fragmentation**.

---

# 5. Mark-Compact Algorithm

Used by:

* Serial GC (Old)
* Parallel GC (Old)

---

## Phase 1

Mark.

```text
A‚úì

B‚úì

D‚úì
```

---

## Phase 2

Move objects together.

Before:

```text
A ‚ñ° B ‚ñ° D ‚ñ°
```

After:

```text
A B D ‚ñ° ‚ñ° ‚ñ°
```

No fragmentation.

---

## Advantages

* Eliminates fragmentation
* Fast future allocations

---

## Disadvantages

Moving objects requires updating references.

This increases pause time.

---

# 6. Copying Algorithm

Used for:

Young Generation.

---

Suppose:

```text
Eden

A B C D E
```

Alive:

```text
A

D
```

Copy:

```text
Survivor

A

D
```

Discard Eden.

---

Advantages:

* Extremely fast when most objects die young.
* No fragmentation.
* Allocation is simple (pointer bump).

---

Disadvantage:

Requires extra memory because one region (or Survivor space) must be available for copying.

---

# 7. Generational Collection

Based on:

## Weak Generational Hypothesis

Most objects:

```text
Die Young
```

Long-lived objects:

```text
Move

‚Üì

Old Generation
```

Young:

Copying.

Old:

Collector-specific algorithm.

---

# 8. Tri-Color Marking

Modern concurrent collectors use this concept.

Objects are grouped into:

```text
White

Gray

Black
```

---

### White

```text
Not Visited
```

Potential garbage.

---

### Gray

```text
Visited

Children Not Processed
```

Still being explored.

---

### Black

```text
Visited

Children Processed
```

Completely processed.

---

Example:

```text
Root

‚Üì

A

‚Üì

B

‚Üì

C
```

Process:

```text
White

‚Üì

Gray

‚Üì

Black
```

Eventually:

```text
All reachable

‚Üì

Black
```

Remaining White objects are garbage.

---

# 9. SATB (Snapshot At The Beginning)

Used by:

* G1
* Shenandoah

Problem:

Application keeps modifying references while GC runs.

Example:

```java
customer.address = null;
```

During marking.

Without protection:

GC could incorrectly reclaim a live object.

---

SATB solution:

Take a logical snapshot of reachability **at the beginning** of the marking cycle.

Objects reachable in that snapshot remain live for the current cycle.

---

# 10. Incremental Update

Used in some concurrent collectors (historically including CMS).

Instead of preserving the initial snapshot, it tracks **new references created during marking**.

Example:

```java
customer.address = new Address();
```

A write barrier records this update so the collector can revisit affected objects if necessary.

---

# 11. Write Barrier

Triggered on reference writes.

Example:

```java
customer.order = order;
```

Conceptually:

```text
Reference Update

‚Üì

Write Barrier

‚Üì

Record Metadata
```

Used for:

* Card Tables
* Remembered Sets
* SATB
* Incremental Update

---

# 12. Read (Load) Barrier

Triggered when a reference is read.

Example:

```java
customer.getOrder();
```

Conceptually:

```text
Read Reference

‚Üì

Barrier

‚Üì

Correct Address

‚Üì

Return Object
```

Used by:

* ZGC
* Shenandoah (read barrier/fowarding mechanism)

---

# 13. Card Table

Used to efficiently track references from the Old Generation into the Young Generation.

Instead of scanning the entire Old Generation:

```text
Old Generation

‚Üì

Card Table

‚Üì

Dirty Cards

‚Üì

Scan Only Those
```

---

# 14. Remembered Set

Used by G1.

Tracks:

```text
Region A

‚Üì

References

‚Üì

Region B
```

Each region knows which other regions reference it.

---

# 15. Region Evacuation

Used by:

* G1
* ZGC
* Shenandoah

Instead of:

```text
Delete Objects
```

They:

```text
Copy Live Objects

‚Üì

Free Entire Region
```

This automatically compacts memory.

---

# 16. Concurrent Relocation

Used by:

* ZGC
* Shenandoah

Objects move while:

```text
Application

Still Running
```

This is the major innovation enabling extremely low pause times.

---

# 17. Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm             | Moves Objects | Fragmentation | Concurrent      | Used By            |
| --------------------- | ------------- | ------------- | --------------- | ------------------ |
| Reference Counting    | No            | No            | Yes             | Not HotSpot GC     |
| Mark-Sweep            | No            | Yes           | Can be          | CMS                |
| Mark-Compact          | Yes           | No            | Usually STW     | Serial, Parallel   |
| Copying               | Yes           | No            | During Young GC | Young Generation   |
| SATB                  | N/A           | N/A           | Yes             | G1, Shenandoah     |
| Incremental Update    | N/A           | N/A           | Yes             | CMS (historically) |
| Concurrent Relocation | Yes           | No            | Yes             | ZGC, Shenandoah    |

---

# 18. Which Collector Uses Which Algorithm?

| Collector   | Main Algorithms                                         |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| Serial GC   | Copying + Mark-Compact                                  |
| Parallel GC | Parallel Copying + Parallel Mark-Compact                |
| CMS         | Mark-Sweep + Incremental Update                         |
| G1          | Copying (Evacuation) + SATB + Remembered Sets           |
| ZGC         | Concurrent Relocation + Colored Pointers + Load Barrier |
| Shenandoah  | Concurrent Relocation + Brooks Pointer + Read Barrier   |

---

# 19. Evolution of Java GC

```text
Reference Counting
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Mark-Sweep
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Mark-Compact
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Generational GC
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
CMS
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
G1
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
ZGC / Shenandoah
```

Each generation solved limitations of the previous one.

---

# 20. Common Interview Questions

## Basic

1. Why doesn't Java use Reference Counting?
2. What is the difference between Mark-Sweep and Mark-Compact?
3. Why is Copying ideal for the Young Generation?

---

## Intermediate

4. Explain Tri-Color Marking.
5. What is SATB?
6. What is Incremental Update?
7. What is a Write Barrier?

---

## Advanced

8. Why does CMS fragment memory?
9. How does G1 avoid fragmentation?
10. Why does ZGC need Load Barriers?
11. Brooks Pointer vs Colored Pointer.
12. Explain Concurrent Relocation.

---

# Architect Perspective

Imagine a Java 21 Spring Boot microservice processing **20,000 requests/sec**.

Every request creates:

* DTOs
* JSON objects
* Validation objects
* Response objects

These die quickly, so the **Copying Algorithm** efficiently reclaims them in the Young Generation.

Long-lived objects like:

* Spring Beans
* Kafka Producers
* Database Pools

remain in the Old Generation.

If you're using:

* **G1**, live objects are evacuated between regions.
* **ZGC**, objects can relocate concurrently using **Colored Pointers** and **Load Barriers**.
* **Shenandoah**, concurrent relocation is enabled through **Brooks Pointers** and **Read Barriers**.

Understanding these algorithms allows you to explain **why** one collector is chosen over another instead of simply memorizing collector names.

---

# Complete Algorithm Mind Map

```text
                     Garbage Collection
                            ‚îÇ
    ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
    ‚îÇ                       ‚îÇ                        ‚îÇ
Reference Counting     Mark-Sweep            Mark-Compact
                            ‚îÇ                        ‚îÇ
                            ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
                                         ‚îÇ
                                  Generational GC
                                         ‚îÇ
                        ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
                        ‚îÇ                                 ‚îÇ
                   Young Generation                 Old Generation
                    (Copying)                 (Collector Specific)
                        ‚îÇ
        ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
        ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ                 ‚îÇ                      ‚îÇ
     Parallel        CMS              G1 GC           ZGC / Shenandoah
                         ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ                     ‚îÇ
                Incremental Update      SATB       Concurrent Relocation
                                         ‚îÇ          ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
                                  Remembered Sets  Colored Ptr   Brooks Ptr
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* All modern Java garbage collectors are built on a **small set of core algorithms**.
* **Mark-Sweep** is simple but causes fragmentation.
* **Mark-Compact** removes fragmentation at the cost of moving objects.
* **Copying** is ideal for the Young Generation because most objects die young.
* **Generational GC** is based on the **Weak Generational Hypothesis**.
* **SATB** and **Incremental Update** solve correctness issues in concurrent marking.
* **Write Barriers**, **Read Barriers**, **Card Tables**, and **Remembered Sets** enable efficient concurrent and generational collection.
* Modern collectors such as **G1**, **ZGC**, and **Shenandoah** extend these foundational algorithms rather than replacing them.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 14 ‚Äì JVM GC Tuning)

This is where theory becomes practical.

We'll cover:

* Heap sizing (`-Xms`, `-Xmx`)
* Young generation sizing
* `MaxGCPauseMillis`
* `G1HeapRegionSize`
* `InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent`
* `ParallelGCThreads`
* `ConcGCThreads`
* String Deduplication
* Large page support
* Container-aware JVM tuning
* Spring Boot production tuning
* Java 21 best practices
* Real interview scenarios

This is one of the most valuable phases for real-world JVM performance engineering.

pages.title: JVM GC Tuning
pages.text:
Excellent. This phase is where **theory meets production**.

As a **Staff Engineer** or **Architect**, interviewers rarely ask:

> *"What is G1 GC?"*

Instead, they ask questions like:

* *Why is my Spring Boot service pausing for 2 seconds?*
* *How would you tune JVM for Kubernetes?*
* *Why is GC running every few seconds?*
* *How do you size the heap?*

This phase prepares you for exactly those discussions.

---

# Java 21 Garbage Collection Deep Dive

# Phase 14 ‚Äì JVM GC Tuning

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* Heap sizing
* Young vs Old generation tuning
* G1 tuning parameters
* ZGC tuning
* Thread tuning
* Container-aware JVM
* Spring Boot production tuning
* Memory sizing methodology
* GC tuning workflow
* Common mistakes
* Interview questions

---

# 1. The First Rule of GC Tuning

> **Never tune blindly.**

Always follow this order:

```text
Application Slow

‚Üì

Collect Metrics

‚Üì

Analyze GC Logs

‚Üì

Find Root Cause

‚Üì

Tune

‚Üì

Measure Again
```

Many GC problems are actually caused by:

* Memory leaks
* Excessive object creation
* Large caches
* Poor application design

---

# 2. JVM Heap Layout

```text
                Heap

+--------------------------------------+
|           Young Generation           |
| Eden | Survivor 0 | Survivor 1       |
+--------------------------------------+
|            Old Generation            |
+--------------------------------------+
```

Important JVM options:

```bash
-Xms
-Xmx
```

---

# 3. `-Xms` (Initial Heap)

Example:

```bash
-Xms2g
```

Meaning:

```text
JVM starts with

2 GB Heap
```

Advantages:

* Less heap resizing
* Faster warm-up
* Stable performance

---

# 4. `-Xmx` (Maximum Heap)

Example:

```bash
-Xmx8g
```

Meaning:

```text
Heap

Maximum

8 GB
```

---

# 5. Best Practice

For servers:

```bash
-Xms8g
-Xmx8g
```

Same value.

Why?

Avoid:

```text
2 GB

‚Üì

3 GB

‚Üì

5 GB

‚Üì

8 GB
```

Repeated heap expansion causes additional work and can affect latency.

---

# 6. How Much Heap?

There is no universal answer.

Example:

Spring Boot

* 500 requests/sec
* Kafka
* PostgreSQL
* Redis

Typical heap:

```text
4‚Äì8 GB
```

Analytics platform:

* Millions of metrics

```text
16‚Äì64 GB
```

Choose based on:

* Live data size
* Allocation rate
* Latency goals
* Available RAM

---

# 7. Young Generation

Purpose:

Store short-lived objects.

```text
HTTP Request

‚Üì

DTO

‚Üì

JSON

‚Üì

Response

‚Üì

Dead
```

Most allocations die here.

---

# 8. If Young Generation Is Too Small

Example:

```text
100 MB
```

Application allocates:

```text
500 MB/sec
```

Result:

```text
Young GC

‚Üì

Young GC

‚Üì

Young GC

‚Üì

Young GC
```

Very frequent collections.

---

# 9. If Young Generation Is Too Large

Example:

```text
6 GB Young
```

Young GC:

```text
Scan

6 GB
```

Longer pauses.

Balance is important.

---

# 10. G1 Important Parameters

## Pause Target

```bash
-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200
```

Meaning:

Desired pause:

```text
‚âà 200 ms
```

This is a **goal**, not a guarantee.

---

## Region Size

```bash
-XX:G1HeapRegionSize=8m
```

Region sizes are normally chosen automatically.

Manual tuning is rarely required.

---

## Initiating Heap Occupancy Percent (IHOP)

```bash
-XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=45
```

Meaning:

Start concurrent marking when approximately **45% of the old generation** is occupied.

If marking starts too late:

```text
Old Generation Full

‚Üì

Emergency Full GC
```

---

# 11. Parallel GC Threads

```bash
-XX:ParallelGCThreads=8
```

Controls:

```text
Parallel Workers
```

Too few:

```text
GC Slow
```

Too many:

```text
CPU Contention
```

Modern JVMs usually size this automatically.

---

# 12. Concurrent GC Threads

```bash
-XX:ConcGCThreads=4
```

Used by:

* G1
* ZGC
* Shenandoah

These threads perform concurrent marking and related work.

Again, defaults are generally appropriate.

---

# 13. String Deduplication

Enable:

```bash
-XX:+UseStringDeduplication
```

Works with G1.

Example:

```java
new String("ERROR")
new String("ERROR")
new String("ERROR")
```

Duplicate character arrays may be shared, reducing memory usage.

Useful when applications create many duplicate strings.

---

# 14. Large Pages

```bash
-XX:+UseLargePages
```

May reduce Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) misses on supported operating systems.

Usually considered only for large heaps and carefully managed environments.

---

# 15. Container Awareness

Java 21 is container-aware.

Example:

Container:

```text
Memory

4 GB
```

JVM automatically considers container limits when sizing memory.

Recommended:

```bash
-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75
```

Example:

Container:

```text
4 GB
```

Heap:

```text
‚âà 3 GB
```

Leaving memory for:

* Metaspace
* Thread stacks
* Direct buffers
* Native libraries

---

# 16. Spring Boot Production Example

Server:

```text
16 CPU

32 GB RAM
```

Container:

```text
8 GB
```

Typical configuration:

```bash
-Xms4g
-Xmx4g
-XX:+UseG1GC
-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200
-Xlog:gc*
```

This provides:

* Stable heap
* Predictable pauses
* GC logging

---

# 17. ZGC Tuning

Enable:

```bash
-XX:+UseZGC
```

Generational mode:

```bash
-XX:+ZGenerational
```

Usually:

Minimal tuning.

ZGC is designed to require fewer collector-specific knobs.

---

# 18. Shenandoah Tuning

Enable:

```bash
-XX:+UseShenandoahGC
```

Generational mode:

```bash
-XX:ShenandoahGCMode=generational
```

Like ZGC, Shenandoah generally relies on sensible defaults.

---

# 19. Common Production Problems

## Problem 1

GC every 2 seconds.

Reason:

```text
Heap Too Small
```

---

## Problem 2

2-second pauses.

Reason:

```text
Old Generation Full
```

---

## Problem 3

OutOfMemoryError.

Possible causes:

* Memory leak
* Heap too small
* Excessive caching
* Large object retention

---

## Problem 4

High CPU during GC.

Possible causes:

* Excessive allocation rate
* Too many concurrent GC threads
* High application load

---

# 20. GC Tuning Workflow

```text
Slow Application

‚Üì

Enable GC Logs

‚Üì

Analyze

‚Üì

Find Allocation Rate

‚Üì

Find Pause Time

‚Üì

Find Heap Usage

‚Üì

Tune

‚Üì

Measure Again
```

---

# 21. Common Mistakes

### ‚ùå Increasing `-Xmx` without analysis

A larger heap can increase the amount of work during collections.

---

### ‚ùå Ignoring allocation rate

Creating millions of temporary objects is often the real issue.

---

### ‚ùå Using Parallel GC for low-latency systems

Parallel GC prioritizes throughput over pause time.

---

### ‚ùå Tuning every JVM option

Modern JVM defaults are generally excellent.

Tune only when measurements justify it.

---

# 22. Production JVM Examples

## REST Microservice

```bash
-Xms2g
-Xmx2g
-XX:+UseG1GC
-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200
-Xlog:gc*
```

---

## Kafka Consumer

```bash
-Xms4g
-Xmx4g
-XX:+UseG1GC
-XX:+UseStringDeduplication
```

---

## Analytics Platform

```bash
-Xms16g
-Xmx16g
-XX:+UseZGC
-XX:+ZGenerational
-Xlog:gc*
```

---

# 23. Choosing the Right Collector

| Workload                                                                | Recommended GC |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------- |
| Small CLI application                                                   | Serial         |
| Batch processing                                                        | Parallel       |
| Typical Spring Boot microservice                                        | G1             |
| Large heap (>32‚Äì64 GB) with strict latency                              | ZGC            |
| Very low-latency enterprise workloads (where available and appropriate) | Shenandoah     |

---

# 24. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What do `-Xms` and `-Xmx` mean?
2. Why set them to the same value?
3. What is `MaxGCPauseMillis`?

---

## Intermediate

4. What is `InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent`?
5. Explain `UseStringDeduplication`.
6. Why does a small Young Generation increase GC frequency?

---

## Advanced

7. How would you tune a Spring Boot application?
8. How would you tune a Kubernetes deployment?
9. When would you choose ZGC over G1?
10. Explain GC tuning methodology.
11. What metrics would you collect before tuning?
12. Why can increasing the heap make pauses longer?

---

# Architect Perspective

Imagine your telecom analytics platform:

* Java 21
* Spring Boot
* Kubernetes
* Kafka
* PostgreSQL
* Prometheus
* 10,000 REST requests/minute
* 8 GB container limit

A reasonable starting point would be:

```bash
-Xms4g
-Xmx4g
-XX:+UseG1GC
-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200
-XX:+UseStringDeduplication
-Xlog:gc*:file=/var/log/app/gc.log:time,uptime,level,tags
```

Then monitor:

* GC pause time
* Allocation rate
* Old generation occupancy
* CPU utilization
* Response latency (p95/p99)
* Throughput

Tune **only** if production metrics indicate a problem.

---

# GC Tuning Decision Flow

```text
Application Slow
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Enable GC Logs
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Pause Too High?
        ‚îÇ
   ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
   ‚îÇ          ‚îÇ
  Yes         No
   ‚îÇ          ‚îÇ
Tune Heap   Look at
& GC        Application Code
   ‚îÇ
   ‚ñº
Measure Again
```

---

# Java 21 Production Tuning Checklist

| Area               | Recommendation                                                                   |
| ------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Heap Size          | Set `-Xms` = `-Xmx` for stable server workloads                                  |
| Default GC         | Use G1 unless latency requirements justify another collector                     |
| Pause Goal         | Start with `-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200` (adjust only if needed)                    |
| Logging            | Always enable GC logging in production                                           |
| Containers         | Use container-aware sizing (`MaxRAMPercentage`) rather than assuming host memory |
| String Duplication | Consider `UseStringDeduplication` for string-heavy applications                  |
| Monitoring         | Track GC pauses, allocation rate, heap usage, and p95/p99 latency together       |

---

# Key Takeaways

* GC tuning begins with **measurement**, not JVM flags.
* Use **G1** as the default collector for most Java 21 Spring Boot services.
* Set **`-Xms` and `-Xmx` to the same value** for long-running production servers to avoid heap resizing.
* Tune **heap size based on live data and allocation rate**, not arbitrary rules.
* For containers, size the heap relative to the container memory limit rather than the host.
* Enable **GC logging** from day one so historical data is available when performance issues arise.
* Most performance gains come from **reducing object allocation and fixing memory leaks**, not from changing GC flags.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 15 ‚Äì GC Logging & Analysis)

This is the phase where you'll learn to **diagnose real production issues** by reading GC logs.

We'll cover:

* Unified GC logging (`-Xlog:gc*`)
* Understanding every field in a GC log
* Young GC, Mixed GC, Full GC events
* G1 log interpretation
* ZGC log interpretation
* GCViewer and GCeasy
* Detecting memory leaks from logs
* Real production case studies
* Interview log-analysis exercises

This is one of the most practical skills for JVM performance troubleshooting.

pages.title: GC Logging & Analysis
pages.text:
Excellent. This is one of the **highest-value phases** for interviews and production support.

Many developers know GC theory, but very few can **read a GC log and immediately identify a JVM problem**.

At the **Staff Engineer/Architect** level, interviewers often hand you a GC log and ask:

> "What do you think is happening here?"

This phase teaches you exactly that.

---

# Java 21 Garbage Collection Deep Dive

# Phase 15 ‚Äì GC Logging & Analysis

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* Unified JVM logging
* Enabling GC logs
* Reading G1 GC logs
* Reading ZGC logs
* Understanding every field
* Young GC
* Mixed GC
* Full GC
* Concurrent Mark Cycle
* Humongous allocations
* Detecting memory leaks
* Production troubleshooting

---

# 1. Why GC Logs Matter

Imagine your Spring Boot service suddenly becomes slow.

Users report:

```
Response Time

300 ms

‚Üì

2 seconds
```

Without GC logs:

```
Guessing
```

With GC logs:

```
See GC Events

‚Üì

Find Problem

‚Üì

Fix Problem
```

GC logs are one of the first artifacts to examine during JVM performance investigations.

---

# 2. Enable GC Logging (Java 21)

The old Java 8 options:

```bash
-XX:+PrintGCDetails
```

are obsolete.

Java 21 uses **Unified Logging**.

Basic:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*
```

Production:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*:file=logs/gc.log:time,uptime,level,tags
```

Recommended rotation:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*:file=logs/gc.log:time,uptime:filecount=10,filesize=100M
```

---

# 3. Example G1 Log

```
[2.345s][info][gc]
GC(12) Pause Young (Normal)
512M->128M(2G)
12.45ms
```

Let's decode it.

---

# 4. Timestamp

```
[2.345s]
```

Meaning:

```
JVM started

‚Üì

2.345 seconds later

‚Üì

GC occurred
```

---

# 5. GC Number

```
GC(12)
```

Meaning:

```
13th GC Event
```

This helps correlate log entries belonging to the same GC cycle.

---

# 6. GC Type

Example:

```
Pause Young (Normal)
```

Means:

```
Young Generation

Collected
```

Possible values:

```
Pause Young

Pause Young (Mixed)

Pause Full

Concurrent Mark Cycle
```

---

# 7. Heap Usage

Example:

```
512M->128M(2G)
```

Meaning:

Before GC:

```
512 MB Used
```

After GC:

```
128 MB Used
```

Maximum Heap:

```
2 GB
```

GC reclaimed:

```
384 MB
```

---

# 8. Pause Time

```
12.45ms
```

Meaning:

Application paused for:

```
12.45 milliseconds
```

This is the value most people first look at.

---

# 9. Young GC Example

```
Pause Young (Normal)

256M

‚Üì

90M

8 ms
```

Interpretation:

* Eden filled up
* Young GC occurred
* Most objects died
* Excellent result

---

# 10. Mixed GC Example

```
Pause Young (Mixed)

2.4G

‚Üì

1.8G
```

Meaning:

G1 collected:

* Young Regions
* Some Old Regions

This is normal after a concurrent marking cycle.

---

# 11. Concurrent Mark Cycle

Example:

```
Concurrent Mark Cycle
```

Meaning:

```
Application Running

+

GC Marking
```

No major pause.

Expected sequence:

```
Initial Mark

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Remark

‚Üì

Cleanup
```

---

# 12. Full GC

Example:

```
Pause Full

6G

‚Üì

2G

4.8 sec
```

This is a warning sign.

Possible causes:

* Heap exhausted
* Allocation failure
* Large object pressure
* Memory leak
* Incorrect heap sizing

---

# 13. Reading G1 Sequence

Typical pattern:

```
Young GC

‚Üì

Young GC

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Mixed GC

‚Üì

Young GC

‚Üì

Mixed GC
```

This is healthy behavior.

---

# 14. Bad Pattern

```
Young GC

Young GC

Young GC

Young GC

Young GC

Every Second
```

Meaning:

Young Generation is probably too small or the application allocates objects very rapidly.

---

# 15. Another Bad Pattern

```
Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Full GC

‚Üì

Concurrent Mark

‚Üì

Full GC
```

Likely causes:

* Old Generation fills faster than concurrent marking can complete.
* Memory leak.
* Heap too small.

---

# 16. Memory Leak Pattern

Suppose logs show:

```
1.2G

‚Üì

900M

‚Üì

1.3G

‚Üì

1.0G

‚Üì

1.4G

‚Üì

1.2G
```

Notice:

After each GC:

Heap baseline increases.

This is classic memory leak behavior.

---

# 17. Healthy Pattern

```
500M

‚Üì

120M

‚Üì

550M

‚Üì

130M

‚Üì

600M

‚Üì

125M
```

Heap always returns close to the same baseline.

No evidence of a leak.

---

# 18. G1 Humongous Objects

Example:

```
Humongous Allocation
```

Meaning:

Object larger than roughly half a region.

Examples:

* Huge JSON payload
* Large byte[]
* Image buffer

Too many humongous allocations may increase GC pressure.

---

# 19. ZGC Logs

Example:

```
Pause Mark Start

0.30ms

Concurrent Mark

Pause Mark End

0.40ms

Concurrent Relocate
```

Interpretation:

* Extremely short pauses
* Most work concurrent

Exactly what ZGC is designed for.

---

# 20. Shenandoah Logs

Typical:

```
Pause Init Mark

0.4 ms

Concurrent Mark

Concurrent Evacuation

Concurrent Update References

Pause Final Mark
```

Again:

Most work concurrent.

---

# 21. Useful Commands

Enable logging:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*
```

More detail:

```bash
-Xlog:gc,gc+heap,gc+phases
```

Debug:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*=debug
```

Trace:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*=trace
```

Use `debug` or `trace` only when needed because they generate large logs.

---

# 22. Production Example

Suppose:

```
Requests/sec

500

‚Üì

Latency

2 seconds
```

GC logs show:

```
Pause Full

4.5 sec
```

Immediately suspect:

* Heap pressure
* Memory leak
* Allocation spikes

The GC log points you in the right direction before changing JVM flags.

---

# 23. GC Analysis Workflow

```
Enable Logs

‚Üì

Find Full GC

‚Üì

Find Young GC Frequency

‚Üì

Find Pause Time

‚Üì

Find Heap Growth

‚Üì

Look for Leak

‚Üì

Tune
```

---

# 24. External Analysis Tools

| Tool                       | Purpose                                      |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| GCViewer                   | Visualize GC logs                            |
| GCeasy                     | Cloud-based GC analysis with recommendations |
| Eclipse MAT                | Heap dump analysis (not GC logs)             |
| VisualVM                   | Live monitoring and heap inspection          |
| Java Mission Control (JMC) | JVM profiling using JFR                      |

---

# 25. Common Production Symptoms

## Frequent Young GC

Likely:

* Small Young Generation
* High allocation rate

---

## Long Full GC

Likely:

* Heap exhausted
* Memory leak
* Large old generation occupancy

---

## Growing Heap After GC

Likely:

* Memory leak
* Cache growth
* Long-lived object retention

---

## High GC CPU

Likely:

* Excessive allocation
* Too-small heap
* Heavy concurrent GC activity

---

# 26. Interview Exercise

Log:

```
GC(31)

Pause Young

900M->320M

15ms
```

Interpretation:

* Normal Young GC
* Good memory reclaimed
* Pause acceptable

---

Log:

```
GC(42)

Pause Full

7G->4G

5.2 sec
```

Interpretation:

* Serious performance issue
* Investigate why Full GC occurred
* Examine heap usage and allocation patterns

---

Log:

```
100M

‚Üì

80M

‚Üì

120M

‚Üì

90M

‚Üì

140M

‚Üì

100M
```

Interpretation:

Healthy baseline.

---

Log:

```
800M

‚Üì

700M

‚Üì

900M

‚Üì

820M

‚Üì

1.1G

‚Üì

1.0G
```

Interpretation:

Possible memory leak or growing retained set.

---

# 27. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. How do you enable GC logging in Java 21?
2. Explain `512M->128M(2G)`.
3. What is a Young GC?

---

## Intermediate

4. What is a Mixed GC?
5. Why is a Full GC bad?
6. How do you identify a memory leak from GC logs?
7. What is a Concurrent Mark Cycle?

---

## Advanced

8. Explain a healthy G1 log pattern.
9. What does increasing post-GC heap usage indicate?
10. What causes Humongous allocations?
11. How would you analyze a production GC log?
12. What metrics would you correlate with GC logs?

---

# Architect Perspective

Imagine your telecom analytics platform:

* Java 21
* Spring Boot
* Kafka
* PostgreSQL
* Prometheus
* 8 GB heap
* 15,000 REST requests/minute

A production-ready JVM should always have:

```bash
-Xlog:gc*:file=/var/log/app/gc.log:time,uptime,level,tags:filecount=10,filesize=100M
```

When users report latency spikes:

1. Check application metrics (CPU, memory, request latency).
2. Inspect GC logs for:

   * Young GC frequency
   * Pause duration
   * Full GC events
   * Heap occupancy after GC
3. Correlate with allocation spikes, deployments, or traffic increases.
4. Decide whether the issue is:

   * JVM tuning
   * Memory leak
   * High allocation rate
   * Application design

---

# GC Troubleshooting Flow

```text
Slow Application
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Read GC Logs
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Full GC?
   ‚îÇ
 ‚îå‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
 ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ
Yes             No
 ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ
Investigate   Check Young GC
Heap/Leaks    Frequency & Pauses
 ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ
 ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
        ‚ñº
Correlate with CPU,
Memory, Allocation Rate,
and Application Metrics
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* Always enable **Unified GC Logging** (`-Xlog:gc*`) in production.
* Learn to interpret:

  * **Young GC**
  * **Mixed GC**
  * **Concurrent Mark**
  * **Full GC**
* A **stable post-GC baseline** generally indicates healthy memory behavior.
* A **steadily increasing post-GC baseline** is a strong indicator of a memory leak or growing retained objects.
* Frequent **Full GC** events require immediate investigation.
* GC logs should be analyzed together with application metrics‚Äînot in isolation.
* Tools like **GCViewer**, **GCeasy**, **Java Mission Control**, and **Eclipse MAT** complement GC log analysis.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 16 ‚Äì Heap Dumps & Memory Leak Analysis)

This is one of the most practical JVM troubleshooting topics.

We'll cover:

* Heap dumps
* `jcmd`
* `jmap`
* Automatic heap dump on OOM
* Eclipse MAT
* Dominator Tree
* Leak Suspects Report
* Retained Heap vs Shallow Heap
* Finding memory leaks in Spring Boot
* Real production case studies
* End-to-end memory leak debugging

This phase is a must-have skill for senior Java engineers who support production systems.

pages.title: Heap Dumps & Memory Leak Analysis
pages.text:
Excellent. This is one of the **most valuable production support skills** for a **Staff Engineer, Architect, or JVM Performance Engineer**.

> **GC logs tell you *that* memory is a problem.**
>
> **Heap dumps tell you *what* is consuming the memory.**

Many production incidents are solved by analyzing a heap dump.

---

# Java 21 Garbage Collection Deep Dive

# Phase 16 ‚Äì Heap Dumps & Memory Leak Analysis

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you'll understand:

* What is a Heap Dump?
* When should you capture one?
* `jcmd`
* `jmap`
* Automatic Heap Dump on OOM
* Eclipse Memory Analyzer (MAT)
* Dominator Tree
* Histogram
* Shallow Heap vs Retained Heap
* Leak Suspects Report
* Common Spring Boot memory leaks
* Production debugging workflow

---

# 1. What is a Heap Dump?

A **Heap Dump** is a snapshot of all objects in the JVM heap at a specific moment.

It contains:

* Every Java object
* Object references
* Class information
* Arrays
* Strings
* Collections
* Thread-local objects
* Object sizes

Think of it as a **photograph of the heap**.

```text
               JVM Heap

+----------------------------------------+
| Customer                              |
| Order                                 |
| HashMap                               |
| ArrayList                             |
| byte[]                                |
| Spring Beans                          |
| Kafka Objects                         |
| Cached Objects                        |
+----------------------------------------+

            ‚Üì

       Heap Dump (.hprof)
```

---

# 2. When Should You Take a Heap Dump?

Typical situations:

* OutOfMemoryError
* Memory leak
* High heap usage
* Frequent Full GC
* Heap never drops after GC
* Production investigation

Example:

```text
Heap Usage

1 GB

‚Üì

3 GB

‚Üì

6 GB

‚Üì

7 GB

‚Üì

Still 7 GB after GC
```

This strongly suggests a leak.

---

# 3. Automatically Create Heap Dump on OOM

Production JVM option:

```bash
-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError
```

Specify location:

```bash
-XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/log/app/
```

Example:

```bash
java \
-Xms4g \
-Xmx4g \
-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError \
-XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/log/app/
```

When an OOM occurs:

```text
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError

‚Üì

heapdump.hprof
```

No manual intervention required.

---

# 4. Capture Heap Dump Using `jcmd` (Recommended)

Find PID:

```bash
jps -l
```

Example:

```text
12345 my-app.jar
```

Capture heap dump:

```bash
jcmd 12345 GC.heap_dump heapdump.hprof
```

Example output:

```text
Heap dump file created
```

**`jcmd` is the preferred tool on modern JDKs.**

---

# 5. Capture Heap Dump Using `jmap`

Older method:

```bash
jmap -dump:live,format=b,file=heap.hprof 12345
```

Meaning:

* dump only live objects
* binary format
* save as `heap.hprof`

`jcmd` is generally preferred because it is more actively maintained.

---

# 6. Heap Dump Analysis Tools

| Tool                         | Purpose                         |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Eclipse MAT                  | Most popular heap analysis tool |
| VisualVM                     | Heap browsing                   |
| Java Mission Control         | JFR + memory analysis           |
| IntelliJ Profiler (Ultimate) | Memory profiling                |
| YourKit / JProfiler          | Commercial profilers            |

For interviews, **MAT** is the most commonly discussed tool.

---

# 7. Opening Heap Dump in MAT

```text
heapdump.hprof

‚Üì

Open in MAT

‚Üì

Parse Heap

‚Üì

Reports
```

MAT automatically indexes millions of objects.

---

# 8. Histogram

One of the first views to inspect.

Example:

```text
Class                  Objects

String                 2,400,000

byte[]                 700,000

HashMap                80,000

ArrayList              60,000
```

Immediately identify:

* Which classes dominate the heap?
* Unexpected object counts?

---

# 9. Shallow Heap

Definition:

Memory occupied by the object itself.

Example:

```java
Customer customer = new Customer();
```

Customer object:

```text
Customer

‚Üì

64 Bytes
```

That is the **Shallow Heap**.

It does **not** include referenced objects.

---

# 10. Retained Heap

Much more important.

Example:

```java
HashMap<Long, Customer> cache;
```

Suppose:

```text
HashMap

‚Üì

1,000,000 Customers
```

Shallow Heap:

```text
200 Bytes
```

Retained Heap:

```text
2 GB
```

If the HashMap disappears:

Entire 2 GB becomes reclaimable.

This is why **Retained Heap** is crucial.

---

# 11. Dominator Tree

The most useful MAT view.

Example:

```text
Application

‚Üì

CacheManager

‚Üì

HashMap

‚Üì

Customer

‚Üì

Orders

‚Üì

Items
```

If:

```text
CacheManager
```

is removed,

everything below it becomes eligible for GC.

MAT helps identify these dominating object graphs.

---

# 12. Leak Suspects Report

MAT can automatically generate:

```text
Leak Suspects Report
```

Example:

```text
One HashMap

Retains

4.2 GB
```

Excellent starting point.

Still verify the findings manually.

---

# 13. Common Memory Leak #1 ‚Äì Static Collections

```java
public class Cache {

    static List<Customer> customers =
            new ArrayList<>();
}
```

Every request:

```java
customers.add(customer);
```

Never removed.

Heap:

```text
Customer

‚Üì

Growing Forever
```

---

# 14. Common Memory Leak #2 ‚Äì Unbounded Cache

```java
Map<String,Object> cache =
        new HashMap<>();
```

Every request:

```java
cache.put(id, response);
```

No eviction.

Eventually:

```text
Heap Full
```

Prefer bounded caches such as **Caffeine**.

---

# 15. Common Memory Leak #3 ‚Äì ThreadLocal

```java
ThreadLocal<UserContext> context =
        new ThreadLocal<>();
```

Forgot:

```java
context.remove();
```

Long-lived threads (especially thread pools) may retain objects longer than intended.
