> **"Why introduce Sequenced Collections when `ArrayList` already had `get(0)`?"**

A strong answer is:

> The goal was **API consistency across all ordered collections**, not new functionality. Before Java 21, different ordered collections exposed different methods for accessing the first and last elements. Sequenced Collections define a common contract, making algorithms easier to write against interfaces rather than concrete implementations. It also improves discoverability, readability, and reduces collection-specific code.

---

## Next Phase (Phase 10)

We'll cover **String Improvements in Modern Java**, including:

* Text Blocks
* `formatted()`
* `strip()`, `stripIndent()`, `translateEscapes()`
* `repeat()`, `indent()`, `lines()`
* String Templates (Preview in Java 21)
* Internal implementation and performance
* Production use cases
* Senior interview questions

generalNotes.title: Switch Expression
generalNotes.text:
Excellent. **Phase 8** is **Switch Expressions**, one of the foundational language improvements that makes Java more expressive and works seamlessly with **Records**, **Pattern Matching**, and **Sealed Classes**.

Although it looks like a small feature, interviewers often combine it with Pattern Matching and Sealed Classes in coding and design questions.

---

# Phase 8 ‚Äì Switch Expressions (Java 21)

> **Status:** Switch Expressions became a final feature in **Java 14** (JEP 361) and are fully supported in Java 21.

---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you should understand:

* Why Switch Expressions were introduced
* Switch Statement vs Switch Expression
* Arrow (`->`) syntax
* `yield`
* Multiple labels
* Exhaustiveness
* Switch with Enums
* Switch with Strings
* Switch with Pattern Matching
* Performance
* JVM implementation
* Production use cases

---

# 1. The Problem with Traditional Switch

Before Java 14, switch was only a **statement**.

Example:

```java
String paymentType;

switch(method){

    case CARD:
        paymentType = "Card";
        break;

    case UPI:
        paymentType = "UPI";
        break;

    case WALLET:
        paymentType = "Wallet";
        break;

    default:
        paymentType = "Unknown";
}
```

Problems:

* Boilerplate
* `break` required
* Fall-through bugs
* Variable declared outside switch
* Verbose

---

# 2. Fall-through Problem

Consider:

```java
switch(day){

    case MONDAY:
        System.out.println("Monday");

    case TUESDAY:
        System.out.println("Tuesday");

    case WEDNESDAY:
        System.out.println("Wednesday");
}
```

Input:

```text
MONDAY
```

Output:

```text
Monday
Tuesday
Wednesday
```

Why?

Because Java continues executing until it encounters a `break`.

This has caused countless production bugs.

---

# 3. Switch Expression

Java 21 allows:

```java
String paymentType =
        switch(method){

            case CARD -> "Card";

            case UPI -> "UPI";

            case WALLET -> "Wallet";

            default -> "Unknown";
        };
```

Notice:

* No variable declaration beforehand
* No `break`
* Cleaner
* Returns a value directly

---

# 4. Statement vs Expression

### Statement

Produces **side effects**

```java
switch(day){
    ...
}
```

No return value.

---

### Expression

Produces **a value**

```java
String result =
        switch(day){
            ...
        };
```

This is similar to expressions in many modern languages.

---

# 5. Arrow Syntax (`->`)

Old:

```java
case CARD:
    return process();
```

New:

```java
case CARD -> process();
```

Advantages:

* No fall-through
* Concise
* Easier to read

---

# 6. Multiple Labels

Instead of:

```java
case SATURDAY:
case SUNDAY:
```

Use:

```java
case SATURDAY, SUNDAY ->
        "Weekend";
```

Much cleaner.

---

# 7. Multi-line Logic

Need multiple statements?

Use a block.

```java
String result =
switch(method){

    case CARD -> {

        log();

        yield "Card";
    }

    default -> "Unknown";
};
```

Notice:

```java
yield
```

returns the value of that case.

---

# 8. Why `yield`?

Inside a block:

```java
{
   ...
}
```

Java needs a way to indicate:

> "This is the value returned by this switch expression."

Example:

```java
yield "Success";
```

Think of `yield` as the switch equivalent of `return`.

---

# 9. Switch on Enum

```java
enum PaymentMethod {

    CARD,
    UPI,
    WALLET
}
```

Expression:

```java
String type =
switch(method){

    case CARD -> "Credit Card";

    case UPI -> "UPI";

    case WALLET -> "Wallet";
};
```

Notice:

No `default`.

Why?

Compiler knows every enum constant.

---

# 10. Exhaustiveness

Compiler checks:

```text
Did you handle every possible case?
```

If not:

Compilation error (for exhaustive switches such as enums or sealed hierarchies).

This greatly reduces bugs.

---

# 11. Switch on String

```java
String currency =

switch(code){

    case "USD" -> "Dollar";

    case "EUR" -> "Euro";

    case "INR" -> "Rupee";

    default -> "Unknown";
};
```

---

# 12. Switch with Pattern Matching

One of Java 21's strongest combinations.

```java
switch(payment){

    case Card c ->
            processCard(c);

    case Wallet w ->
            processWallet(w);

    case UPI u ->
            processUPI(u);

    default ->
            throw new IllegalArgumentException();
}
```

No casting.

---

# 13. Switch with Records

```java
record Employee(int id,
                String name){}
```

Switch:

```java
switch(obj){

    case Employee(int id,
                  String name) ->

            System.out.println(name);

    default -> {}
}
```

Automatic decomposition.

---

# 14. Switch with Sealed Classes

```java
sealed interface Payment

permits

Card,

Wallet,

UPI
```

Switch:

```java
switch(payment){

    case Card c -> ...

    case Wallet w -> ...

    case UPI u -> ...
}
```

No `default`.

Compiler guarantees completeness.

---

# 15. Null Handling

Old switch:

```java
switch(obj)
```

Null caused

```text
NullPointerException
```

Java 21:

```java
switch(obj){

    case null ->
            handleNull();

    case Employee e ->
            process(e);
}
```

Explicit and safer.

---

# 16. Internal Working

Compiler roughly transforms:

```java
switch(color)
```

into optimized branching logic.

For enums:

```text
Enum

‚Üì

Ordinal

‚Üì

Jump Table
```

For Strings:

```text
HashCode

‚Üì

Equals()

‚Üì

Jump
```

The exact strategy depends on the compiler and JVM, but the goal is efficient dispatch.

---

# 17. Performance

### Integer Switch

Usually compiled into:

```text
Jump Table
```

O(1) dispatch for dense ranges.

---

### String Switch

Typically:

```text
hashCode()

‚Üì

equals()

‚Üì

Jump
```

Much faster than long `if-else` chains.

---

### Pattern Matching Switch

Conceptually:

```text
instanceof

‚Üì

Type Check

‚Üì

Cast

‚Üì

Branch
```

Optimized by the compiler and JVM.

---

# 18. Production Example

Payment Gateway

```java
sealed interface Payment
```

Subtypes:

```text
Card

UPI

Wallet

NetBanking
```

Processing:

```java
return switch(payment){

    case Card c ->
            authorizeCard(c);

    case UPI u ->
            authorizeUPI(u);

    case Wallet w ->
            authorizeWallet(w);

    case NetBanking n ->
            authorizeBank(n);
};
```

No casting.

No Visitor pattern.

---

# 19. Telecom Example

Suppose alarms arrive from different technologies.

```java
sealed interface Alarm
```

Implementations:

```text
DWDMAlarm

OTNAlarm

SDHAlarm

EthernetAlarm
```

Routing:

```java
return switch(alarm){

    case DWDMAlarm a ->
            processDWDM(a);

    case OTNAlarm a ->
            processOTN(a);

    case SDHAlarm a ->
            processSDH(a);

    case EthernetAlarm a ->
            processEthernet(a);
};
```

Very common in telecom mediation systems.

---

# 20. Old vs New

### Traditional

```java
switch

‚Üì

case

‚Üì

break

‚Üì

mutable variable
```

---

### Java 21

```java
Expression

‚Üì

Arrow

‚Üì

Yield

‚Üì

Immutable result
```

---

# 21. Switch Statement vs Switch Expression

| Switch Statement                 | Switch Expression          |
| -------------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Doesn't return a value           | Returns a value            |
| Needs `break`                    | No `break`                 |
| Fall-through possible            | No fall-through with `->`  |
| Mutable variables often required | Encourages immutable style |
| More boilerplate                 | More concise               |

---

# 22. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What is a Switch Expression?
2. Difference between Statement and Expression?
3. Why was `yield` introduced?
4. Why is arrow syntax safer?

---

## Intermediate

5. Multiple labels?
6. Exhaustiveness?
7. Switch on Enum?
8. Switch on String?
9. Switch with Pattern Matching?

---

## Advanced

10. Internal implementation?
11. Compiler optimizations?
12. Switch vs if-else performance?
13. Why does Switch Expression fit Functional Programming?
14. Switch with Sealed Classes?

---

# Architecture Perspective

Imagine you're designing a **Notification Service** that supports multiple channels.

```java
sealed interface Notification
        permits Email, SMS, Push {}
```

Processing becomes straightforward:

```java
String status = switch (notification) {
    case Email e -> sendEmail(e);
    case SMS s -> sendSms(s);
    case Push p -> sendPush(p);
};
```

### Why this is architecturally valuable

* **Compile-time safety:** Adding a new notification type forces you to handle it.
* **No hidden fall-through bugs:** Each case is isolated.
* **Easy maintenance:** The routing logic is centralized and readable.
* **Works naturally with Records and Pattern Matching:** Notification types can be immutable records that are automatically deconstructed.

---

# Summary Diagram

```text
               Switch Expression
                      ‚îÇ
      ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
      ‚îÇ               ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº               ‚ñº                ‚ñº
  Arrow Syntax     yield        Multiple Labels
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
 Pattern Matching
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
 Record Patterns
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
 Sealed Classes
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
 Exhaustiveness
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* A **Switch Expression** returns a value and encourages an immutable coding style.
* The **arrow (`->`) syntax** eliminates accidental fall-through.
* Use **`yield`** when a case block contains multiple statements and must return a value.
* Combined with **Pattern Matching** and **Sealed Classes**, Switch Expressions provide powerful compile-time guarantees.
* While performance is generally excellent, the primary reasons to adopt Switch Expressions are **clarity, safety, and maintainability**.

---

## Next Phase (Phase 9)

We'll cover **Sequenced Collections (Java 21)**, including:

* Why they were introduced
* `SequencedCollection`, `SequencedSet`, and `SequencedMap`
* New APIs such as `reversed()`, `addFirst()`, `addLast()`, `getFirst()`, and `getLast()`
* Internal implementation
* Compatibility with existing collections
* Performance considerations
* Production use cases
* Interview questions for senior Java roles

generalNotes.title: Pattern Matching
generalNotes.text:
Excellent. **Pattern Matching** is one of the biggest language improvements after Records. It is frequently asked in Java 21 interviews because it demonstrates modern Java coding style and works closely with **Records** and **Sealed Classes**.

For an **Architect/Staff Engineer**, the interviewer expects you to understand:

* Why Pattern Matching was introduced.
* How it improves type safety and readability.
* How it works with Records and Sealed Classes.
* How the compiler ensures exhaustiveness.
* Performance considerations.

---

# Phase 6 ‚Äì Pattern Matching (Java 21)

## Learning Objectives

After this phase, you should understand:

* Why Pattern Matching was introduced
* Pattern Matching for `instanceof`
* Pattern Matching for `switch`
* Record Patterns
* Nested Record Patterns
* Guarded Patterns (`when`)
* Null handling
* Exhaustiveness checking
* Internal implementation
* Performance
* Production use cases

---

# 1. The Problem Before Pattern Matching

Suppose we have:

```java
Object obj = getEmployee();
```

Traditional Java:

```java
if (obj instanceof Employee) {
    Employee emp = (Employee) obj;
    System.out.println(emp.name());
}
```

Notice the duplication:

* Check type
* Cast type
* Use object

This becomes repetitive across large codebases.

---

# 2. Pattern Matching Solution

Java 21:

```java
if (obj instanceof Employee emp) {
    System.out.println(emp.name());
}
```

The compiler:

* Checks the type
* Performs the cast
* Creates the variable

All in one step.

---

# 3. What Is a Pattern?

A pattern means:

> Test + Extract

Instead of

```text
Check

‚Üì

Cast

‚Üì

Extract
```

Java performs all three operations together.

---

# 4. Pattern Matching for `instanceof`

Old:

```java
if (obj instanceof Customer) {

    Customer customer = (Customer) obj;

    process(customer);
}
```

New:

```java
if (obj instanceof Customer customer) {

    process(customer);
}
```

Less code.

No explicit casting.

---

# 5. Scope of Pattern Variables

Valid:

```java
if (obj instanceof Employee emp) {
    System.out.println(emp.name());
}
```

Invalid:

```java
System.out.println(emp.name());
```

outside the `if`.

The variable exists **only where the compiler knows the pattern has matched**.

---

# 6. Pattern Matching with Logical Operators

Example:

```java
if (obj instanceof Employee emp &&
    emp.salary() > 100000) {

    process(emp);
}
```

Perfectly valid.

The compiler knows:

If the second condition executes,

`emp` already exists.

---

# 7. Pattern Matching for `switch`

This is where Java 21 becomes very powerful.

Suppose

```java
Object payment = ...
```

Traditional:

```java
if (payment instanceof Card) {

}

else if (payment instanceof UPI) {

}

else if (payment instanceof Wallet) {

}
```

Java 21:

```java
switch (payment) {

    case Card c ->
            processCard(c);

    case UPI u ->
            processUPI(u);

    case Wallet w ->
            processWallet(w);

    default ->
            throw new IllegalArgumentException();
}
```

Much cleaner.

---

# 8. Why Is This Better?

Traditional:

```text
instanceof

‚Üì

Cast

‚Üì

Method
```

Repeated many times.

Pattern Matching:

```text
switch

‚Üì

Compiler

‚Üì

Correct Type
```

No duplication.

---

# 9. Record Patterns

Suppose we have

```java
public record Employee(
        int id,
        String name) {}
```

Instead of

```java
Employee e = ...

int id = e.id();

String name = e.name();
```

Java allows:

```java
if (obj instanceof Employee(int id,
                            String name)) {

    System.out.println(id);

    System.out.println(name);
}
```

The compiler extracts fields automatically.

---

# 10. Nested Record Patterns

Suppose

```java
record Address(
        String city,
        String country)
```

```java
record Employee(
        String name,
        Address address)
```

Now:

```java
if (obj instanceof Employee(

        String name,

        Address(

            String city,

            String country))) {

    System.out.println(city);
}
```

No getter chains.

The structure is decomposed directly.

---

# 11. Real Production Example

Order Event

```java
record Order(

    Customer customer,

    Payment payment)
```

Payment

```java
record Card(...)
```

Pattern:

```java
switch(order) {

    case Order(

        Customer customer,

        Card card) ->

            process(customer, card);

    default -> ...
}
```

Ideal for event-driven systems.

---

# 12. Guarded Patterns

Suppose

```java
Employee employee
```

Only process high earners.

```java
switch(employee) {

    case Employee e
        when e.salary() > 100000 ->

            premium(e);

    default ->

            regular(employee);
}
```

The `when` clause adds an additional condition.

---

# 13. Null Handling

Traditional switch:

```java
switch(value)
```

Null caused

```text
NullPointerException
```

Java 21:

```java
switch(value) {

    case null ->

        handleNull();

    case Employee e ->

        process(e);
}
```

Cleaner and explicit.

---

# 14. Sealed Class Example

Suppose

```java
sealed interface Payment
```

Permits

```text
Card

UPI

Wallet
```

Switch

```java
switch(payment) {

    case Card c ->

    case UPI u ->

    case Wallet w ->
}
```

No default required.

Why?

Compiler knows

These are all possible subclasses.

---

# 15. Exhaustiveness

One of Java 21's biggest improvements.

Compiler checks:

```text
Did you handle every possible type?
```

If not,

Compilation fails (when exhaustive checking applies, such as with sealed hierarchies).

Huge improvement for maintainability.

---

# 16. Internal Working

Compiler transforms

```java
case Card c
```

into something conceptually similar to

```java
if(obj instanceof Card){

    Card c = (Card)obj;
}
```

Pattern Matching is largely syntactic sugar with compiler assistance, while also enabling stronger static analysis.

---

# 17. Performance

Traditional

```text
instanceof

‚Üì

Cast
```

Pattern Matching

```text
instanceof

‚Üì

Compiler Optimized
```

Performance is generally comparable.

The benefit is:

* readability
* maintainability
* fewer bugs

---

# 18. Record Pattern Example

```java
record Point(int x,
             int y)
```

Pattern

```java
if(obj instanceof Point(

        int x,

        int y)) {

    System.out.println(x+y);
}
```

Automatic decomposition.

---

# 19. Production Uses

Very useful for

* Kafka Event Processing
* REST Request Routing
* Payment Gateway
* Telecom Alarm Processing
* Notification Systems
* AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) processing
* Rule Engines

---

# 20. Pattern Matching vs Visitor Pattern

Old Java

```text
Visitor Pattern

‚Üì

Double Dispatch

‚Üì

Many Classes
```

Java 21

```text
Pattern Matching

‚Üì

Switch

‚Üì

Simple
```

For many hierarchies, Pattern Matching can reduce the need for the classic Visitor pattern, although Visitor still has value when behavior changes frequently while the object hierarchy remains stable.

---

# 21. Interview Questions

## Basic

1. What is Pattern Matching?
2. Why was it introduced?
3. What is Pattern Matching for `instanceof`?

---

## Intermediate

4. Pattern Matching for `switch`
5. Record Patterns
6. Nested Record Patterns
7. Guarded Patterns
8. Null handling

---

## Advanced

9. What is Exhaustiveness?
10. Why is Pattern Matching safer?
11. Pattern Matching vs Visitor Pattern?
12. Pattern Matching with Sealed Classes?
13. Compiler optimizations?
14. Would you use Pattern Matching in a large enterprise application?

---

# Architecture Example

Imagine a **Telecom Network Management System**, which aligns well with your background.

Incoming events:

```java
sealed interface NetworkEvent
        permits AlarmEvent,
                PerformanceEvent,
                ConfigurationEvent {}
```

Records:

```java
record AlarmEvent(String nodeId, String severity)
        implements NetworkEvent {}

record PerformanceEvent(String nodeId, double cpu)
        implements NetworkEvent {}

record ConfigurationEvent(String nodeId, String operation)
        implements NetworkEvent {}
```

Processing becomes straightforward:

```java
switch (event) {
    case AlarmEvent(String nodeId, String severity)
            when severity.equals("CRITICAL") ->
        escalate(nodeId);

    case AlarmEvent(String nodeId, String severity) ->
        logAlarm(nodeId);

    case PerformanceEvent(String nodeId, double cpu)
            when cpu > 90 ->
        triggerScaleOut(nodeId);

    case PerformanceEvent p ->
        storeMetrics(p);

    case ConfigurationEvent c ->
        auditConfiguration(c);
}
```

Benefits:

* No explicit casts.
* Compile-time exhaustiveness checking.
* Easy to add new event types.
* Clear, declarative routing logic.

---

# Summary Diagram

```text
Pattern Matching

        ‚îÇ

        ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê

        ‚ñº              ‚ñº

instanceof         switch

                        ‚îÇ

                        ‚ñº

                Record Pattern

                        ‚îÇ

                        ‚ñº

               Nested Pattern

                        ‚îÇ

                        ‚ñº

                Guarded Pattern

                        ‚îÇ

                        ‚ñº

               Exhaustiveness
```

---

# Key Takeaways

* Pattern Matching combines **type checking, casting, and variable binding** into a single construct.
* Pattern Matching for `switch` enables concise, type-safe branching.
* Record Patterns allow direct decomposition of immutable data.
* Guarded Patterns (`when`) let you add conditions after a successful match.
* Combined with **Records** and **Sealed Classes**, Pattern Matching provides powerful compile-time guarantees and cleaner domain modeling.
* The primary benefits are **readability, maintainability, and type safety**, not dramatic runtime performance improvements.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 7)

We'll cover **Sealed Classes**, one of the most architecturally significant language features. We'll dive into:

* `sealed`, `permits`, `final`, and `non-sealed`
* JVM implementation
* Exhaustiveness with Pattern Matching
* Domain modeling
* Replacing complex inheritance hierarchies
* Production examples (Payments, Telecom, Banking)
* Architect-level interview questions

generalNotes.title: Sealed Class
Record/Record class/Record type
generalNotes.text:
Excellent. **Records** are one of the most frequently asked Java 17/21 interview topics because they eliminate boilerplate while encouraging immutable data models.

For an **Architect/Staff Engineer**, simply saying "Records reduce boilerplate" is not enough. You should understand **why they were introduced, how they work internally, their limitations, and when to use or avoid them.**

---

# Phase 5 ‚Äì Records (Java 21)

> **Status:** Records became a **final language feature in Java 16** (JEP 395) and are fully available in Java 21.

---

# Learning Objectives

After this phase, you should be able to answer:

* Why were Records introduced?
* What problem do they solve?
* How are Records implemented by the compiler?
* Canonical vs Compact Constructor
* Records vs Classes
* Records vs Lombok
* Records vs Immutable Classes
* Records and Serialization
* Performance implications
* Production use cases

---

# 1. The Problem Before Records

Suppose we have a simple DTO.

```java
public class Employee {

    private final int id;
    private final String name;
    private final String department;

    public Employee(int id,
                    String name,
                    String department) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
        this.department = department;
    }

    public int getId() {
        return id;
    }

    public String getName() {
        return name;
    }

    public String getDepartment() {
        return department;
    }

    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) { ... }

    @Override
    public int hashCode() { ... }

    @Override
    public String toString() { ... }
}
```

Over 50 lines for three fields.

Most of this code is boilerplate.

---

# 2. Record Solution

The same class becomes:

```java
public record Employee(
        int id,
        String name,
        String department) {
}
```

That's it.

The compiler generates the rest.

---

# 3. What Does the Compiler Generate?

The compiler automatically creates:

```text
private final fields

‚Üì

Constructor

‚Üì

Accessor methods

‚Üì

equals()

‚Üì

hashCode()

‚Üì

toString()
```

You don't write them yourself.

---

# 4. Generated Methods

For

```java
public record Employee(
        int id,
        String name) {
}
```

The compiler generates approximately:

```java
private final int id;
private final String name;

public Employee(int id,
                String name) {
    this.id = id;
    this.name = name;
}

public int id() { return id; }

public String name() { return name; }

public boolean equals(...)

public int hashCode()

public String toString()
```

Notice:

Accessors are

```java
employee.id()
```

NOT

```java
employee.getId()
```

---

# 5. Record Components

```java
public record Employee(
        int id,
        String name,
        String department)
```

The parameters are called

**Record Components**

Each component becomes:

* private final field
* constructor parameter
* accessor

---

# 6. Canonical Constructor

Every Record has one primary constructor.

```java
public record Employee(
        int id,
        String name) {

    public Employee(int id,
                    String name) {

        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
    }
}
```

This is called the **Canonical Constructor**.

It must initialize every component.

---

# 7. Compact Constructor

Instead of repeating assignments:

```java
public record Employee(
        int id,
        String name) {

    public Employee {

        if(id <= 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException();

        name = name.trim();
    }
}
```

The compiler automatically inserts:

```java
this.id = id;
this.name = name;
```

after your validation/normalization.

This is the preferred way to enforce invariants.

---

# 8. Validation Example

```java
public record Customer(
        String email) {

    public Customer {

        if(email == null)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException();

        email = email.toLowerCase();
    }
}
```

Every instance is guaranteed to be valid.

---

# 9. Immutability

All fields are

```java
final
```

Meaning

```java
customer.email = "abc";
```

Compilation error.

Records are immutable **by design**.

---

# 10. Important Clarification

The fields are immutable.

The objects referenced by those fields may not be.

Example

```java
public record Student(
        List<String> subjects) {
}
```

Problem

```java
subjects.add("Math");
```

The list changes.

The Record is only **shallowly immutable**.

Better

```java
public record Student(
        List<String> subjects) {

    public Student {
        subjects = List.copyOf(subjects);
    }
}
```

Now the list cannot be modified through the Record.

---

# 11. Records and Inheritance

A Record

Cannot extend another class.

```java
public record Employee(...)
        extends Person
```

Compilation error.

Reason

Every Record already extends

```java
java.lang.Record
```

---

# 12. Can Records Implement Interfaces?

Yes.

```java
public record Employee(
        int id,
        String name)
        implements Serializable {
}
```

Records can implement any number of interfaces.

---

# 13. Custom Methods

Records are not limited to accessors.

```java
public record Employee(
        String first,
        String last) {

    public String fullName() {
        return first + " " + last;
    }
}
```

Behavior is allowed.

The key idea is that the behavior should naturally belong to the data.

---

# 14. Static Members

Records can contain:

```java
static fields

static methods

nested classes
```

Just like normal classes.

---

# 15. Serialization

Records integrate well with Java serialization.

The canonical constructor is respected during deserialization, which helps preserve validation logic.

Many serialization frameworks (such as Jackson 2.12+) also support Records directly.

---

# 16. Reflection

Reflection can identify a Record.

```java
Employee.class.isRecord()
```

Returns

```text
true
```

Components

```java
Employee.class.getRecordComponents()
```

Useful for frameworks and code generation.

---

# 17. Record vs POJO

| POJO                   | Record               |
| ---------------------- | -------------------- |
| Lots of boilerplate    | Minimal code         |
| Manual equals/hashCode | Generated            |
| Mutable by default     | Immutable by default |
| Getters                | Accessors            |
| Error-prone            | Safer                |

---

# 18. Record vs Lombok

| Lombok `@Data`                 | Record                              |
| ------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- |
| Annotation Processor           | Language Feature                    |
| Generates setters (by default) | No setters                          |
| Mutable by default             | Immutable                           |
| External dependency            | Built into Java                     |
| Can hide generated code        | Compiler-generated and standardized |

---

# 19. Record vs Immutable Class

Traditional immutable class

```java
final class Employee
```

Requires

* constructor
* getters
* equals
* hashCode
* toString

Record

Everything is generated automatically.

---

# 20. Performance

Memory usage is similar to an equivalent immutable class.

Performance benefits come primarily from:

* Less boilerplate
* Easier optimization opportunities
* Simpler code maintenance

Don't claim that Records are inherently much faster than regular classes‚Äîthey generally are not.

---

# 21. Production Use Cases

Records are excellent for:

* REST request DTOs
* REST response DTOs
* Kafka events
* RabbitMQ messages
* Database projections
* Configuration objects
* Value Objects in Domain-Driven Design

Example

```java
public record OrderCreatedEvent(
        String orderId,
        double amount,
        String customerId) {
}
```

---

# 22. When Not to Use Records

Avoid Records when:

* The object has mutable state.
* You need setters.
* The class has a complex lifecycle.
* The class is part of an inheritance hierarchy.
* Framework requirements demand a no-argument constructor (unless the framework explicitly supports Records).

---

# 23. Common Mistakes

### Mistake 1

```java
record Student(List<String> subjects)
```

Returning a mutable list without making a defensive copy.

---

### Mistake 2

Trying to add setters.

Records are immutable.

---

### Mistake 3

Using Records for entities managed by ORM frameworks.

Many ORM frameworks expect mutable entities and proxying capabilities.

---

# 24. Interview Questions

### Basic

1. What is a Record?
2. Why were Records introduced?
3. What methods are generated automatically?

### Intermediate

4. What is a Canonical Constructor?
5. What is a Compact Constructor?
6. Can Records have methods?
7. Can Records implement interfaces?
8. Why are Records immutable?

### Advanced

9. Can a Record extend another class?
10. Are Records deeply immutable?
11. Record vs Lombok?
12. Record vs Immutable Class?
13. Would you use Records for JPA entities? Why or why not?
14. How do Records improve API design in microservices?

---

# Architecture Perspective

Imagine an **Order Service**.

Incoming request:

```java
public record CreateOrderRequest(
        String customerId,
        List<String> items,
        String currency) {

    public CreateOrderRequest {
        items = List.copyOf(items);

        if (customerId == null || customerId.isBlank()) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Customer ID is required");
        }
    }
}
```

Advantages:

* Immutable request object.
* Validation at construction time.
* Defensive copy prevents accidental modification.
* Thread-safe for concurrent processing.
* Clear, concise API model.

This makes Records an excellent choice for DTOs and event messages in distributed systems.

---

# Key Takeaways

* Records are **transparent, immutable data carriers**.
* The compiler automatically generates constructors, accessors, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, and `toString()`.
* Records are **shallowly immutable**, so mutable components require defensive copies.
* They are ideal for DTOs, events, and value objects, but generally not for mutable domain entities or complex inheritance hierarchies.
* In architect-level interviews, emphasize that Records improve **correctness, readability, and maintainability**, rather than claiming significant runtime performance gains.

---

# Next Phase (Phase 6)

We'll cover **Pattern Matching** in depth:

* Pattern Matching for `instanceof`
* Pattern Matching for `switch`
* Record Patterns
* Nested Pattern Matching
* Exhaustiveness
* Guarded Patterns
* JVM implementation
* Performance considerations
* Production examples and architect-level interview questions
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
This is one of the most confusing topics because Java uses the word **record** in three different contexts.

For Java 21 interviews, understand the distinction clearly.

| Term             | Meaning                                                | Exists in Java?             | Example                                  |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| **Record**       | Language feature introduced in Java 16 (preview in 14) | ‚úÖ Yes                       | `record Person(String name, int age) {}` |
| **Record Class** | A class declared using the `record` keyword            | ‚úÖ Yes                       | `record Employee(...) {}`                |
| **Record Type**  | The type represented by a record class                 | ‚úÖ Yes (conceptual/JLS term) | `Person`, `Employee`                     |

---

# 1. Record

A **record** is a new kind of class whose primary purpose is to hold immutable data.

Instead of writing:

```java
public final class Person {

    private final String name;
    private final int age;

    public Person(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
    }

    public String name() {
        return name;
    }

    public int age() {
        return age;
    }

    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object o) { }

    @Override
    public int hashCode() { }

    @Override
    public String toString() { }
}
```

You simply write

```java
public record Person(String name, int age) {
}
```

The compiler generates

* private final fields
* constructor
* getters (called accessors)
* equals()
* hashCode()
* toString()

---

# 2. Record Class

When you write

```java
record Employee(String id, String department) {}
```

the compiler creates a **class**.

Internally it is approximately equivalent to

```java
final class Employee extends Record {

    private final String id;
    private final String department;

    ...
}
```

Notice:

* It is still a **class**
* It extends `java.lang.Record`
* It is implicitly final
* Cannot extend another class

Hence people often say:

> "A record is a special kind of class."

That class is called a **record class**.

---

# 3. Record Type

Every class defines a **type**.

Example:

```java
class Car {}
```

`Car` is a type.

Likewise

```java
record Person(String name, int age) {}
```

defines a type called

```
Person
```

This type can be used anywhere a normal type is used.

```java
Person p = new Person("John", 30);

List<Person> people = new ArrayList<>();
```

Here

```
Person
```

is the **record type**.

---

# Visual Representation

```
                record keyword
                     ‚îÇ
                     ‚ñº
      record Person(String name, int age)
                     ‚îÇ
          Compiler generates
                     ‚îÇ
                     ‚ñº
           Record Class (Person)
                     ‚îÇ
             defines the type
                     ‚îÇ
                     ‚ñº
               Record Type
```

---

# Example

```java
record Student(String name, int marks) {}
```

### Record declaration

```java
record Student(...)
```

This is the **record**.

---

### Record class

```
Student
```

is a class generated by the compiler.

Equivalent to

```java
final class Student extends Record
```

---

### Record type

You can declare variables

```java
Student s1;
Student s2;
```

Here `Student` is the **record type**.

---

# Reflection Example

```java
record Employee(String id, int salary) {}

public class Main {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        Class<?> clazz = Employee.class;

        System.out.println(clazz.isRecord());
    }
}
```

Output

```
true
```

Java recognizes that Employee is a **record class**.

---

You can inspect its components:

```java
for (var component : Employee.class.getRecordComponents()) {
    System.out.println(component.getName());
}
```

Output

```
id
salary
```

---

# Comparison with Normal Classes

| Feature                         | Class    | Record Class        |
| ------------------------------- | -------- | ------------------- |
| Mutable                         | Yes      | Typically immutable |
| Boilerplate                     | High     | Very low            |
| Can extend another class        | ‚úÖ Yes    | ‚ùå No                |
| Final                           | Optional | Implicitly final    |
| Extends `java.lang.Record`      | ‚ùå        | ‚úÖ                   |
| Compiler generates constructor  | ‚ùå        | ‚úÖ                   |
| Compiler generates `equals()`   | ‚ùå        | ‚úÖ                   |
| Compiler generates `hashCode()` | ‚ùå        | ‚úÖ                   |
| Compiler generates `toString()` | ‚ùå        | ‚úÖ                   |

---

# Interview Question

**Interviewer:** *Is a record a class?*

**Answer:**

Yes. A record is a special kind of class introduced to model immutable data. A declaration using the `record` keyword creates a **record class**, which implicitly extends `java.lang.Record`, is final, and automatically provides implementations of methods such as the canonical constructor, accessors, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, and `toString()`.

---

## Easy way to remember

* **Record** ‚Üí the Java language feature (`record Person(...) {}`)
* **Record class** ‚Üí the actual class generated from that declaration
* **Record type** ‚Üí the type (`Person`) that you use in variables, method parameters, return types, generics, and elsewhere in your code

For Java 21 interviews, using these three terms correctly demonstrates a solid understanding of how records are defined and represented in the language.

generalNotes.title: Scoped Values
generalNotes.text:
Excellent. This is one of the **most misunderstood Java 21 features**. Many developers think **Scoped Values = ThreadLocal replacement**, but that's only partially true.

For **Staff Engineer/Architect interviews**, the interviewer expects you to explain:

> **Why ThreadLocal becomes problematic with Virtual Threads and why Scoped Values were introduced as an immutable context propagation mechanism.**

> **Status:** Scoped Values are a **Preview Feature in Java 21** (JEP 446). They require `--enable-preview`.

---

# Phase 4 ‚Äì Scoped Values (Java 21)

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you should understand:

* Why ThreadLocal exists
* Problems with ThreadLocal
* Why Virtual Threads expose ThreadLocal limitations
* What Scoped Values are
* How they work internally
* Immutable context propagation
* Performance implications
* Production use cases
* Interview questions

---

# 1. The Problem

Imagine a request enters your Order Service.

```
Client

   ‚îÇ

   ‚ñº

Order Service

   ‚îÇ

   ‚ñº

OrderController
```

Every method needs:

* User ID
* Request ID
* Tenant ID
* Trace ID
* Authentication

Passing these through every method becomes cumbersome.

Example:

```java
public void createOrder(
        String requestId,
        String tenantId,
        String userId,
        String traceId,
        OrderRequest request)
```

Now every downstream method needs the same parameters.

This leads to "parameter pollution."

---

# 2. ThreadLocal Solution

Java introduced

```java
ThreadLocal<T>
```

Example

```java
ThreadLocal<String> USER =
        new ThreadLocal<>();
```

Set

```java
USER.set("Saurabh");
```

Read

```java
String user = USER.get();
```

No need to pass it as a method parameter.

---

# 3. How ThreadLocal Works

```
Platform Thread

      ‚îÇ

      ‚ñº

ThreadLocalMap

      ‚îÇ

 ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê

 USER  ‚Üí Saurabh

 TRACE ‚Üí xyz123

 LANG  ‚Üí EN

 ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
```

Each **platform thread** owns its own `ThreadLocalMap`.

---

# 4. Why ThreadLocal Was Good

Perfect for request-scoped information.

Examples:

* Security Context
* User Session
* Request ID
* Trace ID
* Locale
* Logging (MDC)

Frameworks like Spring Security and logging frameworks rely heavily on it.

---

# 5. The Problems with ThreadLocal

## Problem 1 ‚Äì Mutable State

```java
USER.set("John");

...

USER.set("Alice");
```

Any code can overwrite the value.

Who changed it?

Difficult to determine.

---

## Problem 2 ‚Äì Memory Leaks

Suppose a thread from a thread pool is reused.

```
Thread-1

‚Üì

Request A

‚Üì

USER = Alice

‚Üì

Request Ends

‚Üì

Forgot USER.remove()

‚Üì

Request B

‚Üì

Still Alice
```

This can leak request-specific data across users.

---

## Problem 3 ‚Äì Thread Pools

```
100 Threads

‚Üì

Millions of Requests

‚Üì

Thread Reuse
```

Each reused thread retains its `ThreadLocalMap` unless it is explicitly cleaned up.

---

# 6. Virtual Threads Make It Worse

Virtual Threads are **created by the millions**.

```
1 Million Virtual Threads
```

If every Virtual Thread maintains its own mutable ThreadLocal state:

* More memory overhead
* More object allocation
* More cleanup
* Harder reasoning

The Loom team wanted a better model.

---

# 7. What Are Scoped Values?

A Scoped Value is:

> An **immutable value** that is bound to a specific execution scope.

Instead of

```
Thread owns data
```

We get

```
Execution Scope owns data
```

This aligns naturally with Structured Concurrency.

---

# 8. API

Declare

```java
static final ScopedValue<String> USER =
        ScopedValue.newInstance();
```

Bind

```java
ScopedValue.where(USER, "Saurabh")
           .run(() -> {

               processOrder();

           });
```

Read

```java
String user = USER.get();
```

No `set()`.

No `remove()`.

---

# 9. Why No set()?

ThreadLocal

```java
USER.set("John");

USER.set("Alice");

USER.set("Bob");
```

Anyone can change the value.

Scoped Value

```java
ScopedValue.where(USER, "John")
```

After binding, it **cannot be modified**.

This immutability makes concurrent code safer.

---

# 10. Scope

```
ScopedValue.where(USER, "Saurabh")

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

 processOrder()

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

 saveOrder()

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

 publishKafka()
```

All methods within the scope can read the value.

Once the scope exits, the binding disappears automatically.

---

# 11. Automatic Cleanup

```
Enter Scope

‚Üì

Bind USER

‚Üì

Run Code

‚Üì

Exit Scope

‚Üì

Automatically Removed
```

No explicit `remove()` is needed.

---

# 12. Nested Scopes

```java
ScopedValue.where(USER, "John")

‚Üì

process()

‚Üì

ScopedValue.where(USER, "Alice")

‚Üì

payment()

‚Üì

Return

‚Üì

USER = John
```

The inner binding temporarily shadows the outer one.

When the inner scope ends, the outer value is restored.

---

# 13. Structured Concurrency Integration

Imagine:

```
Dashboard Scope

‚îÇ

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Profile

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Orders

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Wallet
```

All child tasks inherit the same immutable context.

Example:

```
Request ID

Trace ID

Tenant

User
```

No copying or manual propagation is required.

---

# 14. Virtual Threads Integration

Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Reads

‚Üì

Scoped Value

No mutable thread-specific state.

This is a natural fit for the lightweight execution model of Virtual Threads.

---

# 15. Performance

ThreadLocal

```
Thread

‚Üì

ThreadLocalMap

‚Üì

Hash Lookup
```

Scoped Value

```
Execution Scope

‚Üì

Direct Binding
```

The JVM can optimize this model because values are immutable and lexically scoped.

---

# 16. Comparison

| ThreadLocal                    | Scoped Value               |
| ------------------------------ | -------------------------- |
| Mutable                        | Immutable                  |
| Manual cleanup                 | Automatic cleanup          |
| Thread lifetime                | Scope lifetime             |
| Easy to leak                   | Much safer                 |
| Better for legacy thread pools | Better for Virtual Threads |
| `set()` / `remove()`           | `where()`                  |

---

# 17. Production Example

Microservice Request

```
Gateway

‚Üì

Order Service

‚Üì

Payment

‚Üì

Inventory

‚Üì

Notification
```

Every component needs:

* Trace ID
* User ID
* Tenant
* Correlation ID

Using a Scoped Value:

```java
ScopedValue.where(TRACE_ID, uuid)

‚Üì

Everything below

‚Üì

Can access TRACE_ID
```

No need to pass it through every method.

---

# 18. Internal View

```
Scope

‚Üì

Bindings

‚Üì

Scoped Value

‚Üì

Current Execution

‚Üì

Read Only
```

Notice that the binding belongs to the execution scope rather than a thread object.

---

# 19. When to Use

Ideal for:

* Request ID
* Authentication context
* Tenant ID
* Correlation ID
* Locale
* Distributed tracing
* Logging context

---

# 20. When Not to Use

Don't use Scoped Values for:

* Mutable counters
* Caches
* Shared state
* Configuration that changes over time
* Global application state

They are intended for immutable contextual information.

---

# 21. ThreadLocal vs Scoped Value

```java
ThreadLocal<String>
```

```
Mutable

‚Üì

Manual remove()

‚Üì

Leak possible
```

```java
ScopedValue<String>
```

```
Immutable

‚Üì

Auto cleanup

‚Üì

Safer
```

---

# 22. Interview Questions

### Basic

1. What is a Scoped Value?
2. Why was it introduced?
3. How is it different from ThreadLocal?

### Intermediate

4. Why is immutability important?
5. How does automatic cleanup work?
6. How do nested scopes behave?

### Advanced

7. Why does ThreadLocal become problematic with Virtual Threads?
8. Why are Scoped Values a better fit for Structured Concurrency?
9. How are Scoped Values propagated to child tasks?
10. Would you replace every ThreadLocal in an existing application with Scoped Values? Why or why not?

A strong answer to the last question is **no**. Existing frameworks and libraries still rely heavily on `ThreadLocal`, and Scoped Values are currently a preview feature in Java 21. Migrations should be selective and driven by use cases, especially in new code using Virtual Threads and Structured Concurrency.

---

# Architecture Perspective

Imagine a **Payment Microservice**.

Every request needs:

```
Trace ID

‚Üì

User ID

‚Üì

Tenant ID

‚Üì

Authentication
```

Instead of passing four parameters through dozens of methods, bind them once at the request entry point using Scoped Values. Every downstream component participating in that request can access the immutable context. Combined with **Structured Concurrency**, all child tasks automatically inherit the same request context, reducing boilerplate and making concurrent request handling easier to reason about.

---

# Key Takeaways

* **ThreadLocal** attaches mutable state to a thread and requires careful lifecycle management.
* **Scoped Values** attach immutable context to an execution scope, with automatic cleanup.
* They complement **Virtual Threads** and **Structured Concurrency** by providing safe, efficient context propagation.
* Scoped Values are best suited for immutable request context such as trace IDs, tenant IDs, and security context‚Äînot for shared mutable state.

---
----------------------------------------------------------------
Since you're preparing for **Java 21 interviews**, this is one of the most important modern concurrency topics. Interviewers frequently ask:

> **Why did Java introduce Scoped Values when ThreadLocal already exists?**

Let's understand it with a real working example.

---

# What is ThreadLocal?

`ThreadLocal` stores data that belongs to the current thread.

```java
ThreadLocal<String> USER = new ThreadLocal<>();
```

Each thread has its own copy.

```
Thread A
   USER -> "Saurabh"

Thread B
   USER -> "John"
```

---

# Example using ThreadLocal

Suppose every request has a User ID.

```java
class RequestContext {

    static ThreadLocal<String> USER =
            new ThreadLocal<>();

}
```

Business code

```java
class Service {

    static void process() {
        System.out.println(
                "Current User : " +
                        RequestContext.USER.get());
    }
}
```

Main

```java
public class ThreadLocalExample {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        RequestContext.USER.set("Saurabh");

        Service.process();

        RequestContext.USER.remove();
    }
}
```

Output

```
Current User : Saurabh
```

Works perfectly.

---

# But ThreadLocal has problems

Imagine

```
HTTP Request

‚Üì

ThreadPool Thread-1

‚Üì

ThreadLocal = "Saurabh"

‚Üì

Request Ends

‚Üì

Thread returned to pool

‚Üì

Next Request

‚Üì

Same Thread

‚Üì

Oops...
Still contains "Saurabh"
```

If developer forgets

```java
remove();
```

the next request may see previous user's data.

This causes

* Memory leaks
* Security issues
* Wrong data

---

# Example

Request 1

```java
USER.set("Alice");
```

Forgot

```java
remove();
```

Now Thread-1 reused

Request 2

```java
System.out.println(USER.get());
```

Output

```
Alice
```

Although Request 2 never set it.

---

# Another problem

ThreadLocal is mutable.

```java
USER.set("Alice");

...

USER.set("Bob");

...

USER.set("Charlie");
```

Any code can overwrite it.

Finding who changed it becomes difficult.

---

# Virtual Threads Problem

Java 21 introduces millions of Virtual Threads.

```
Carrier Thread

‚Üì

Virtual Thread 1

‚Üì

Virtual Thread 2

‚Üì

Virtual Thread 3
```

ThreadLocal still works.

But

* unnecessary mutable state
* harder cleanup
* inheritance complexity

Hence Java introduced Scoped Values.

---

# What is Scoped Value?

Scoped Value is

> Immutable data shared across a well-defined execution scope.

Instead of

```
Thread owns value
```

it's

```
Execution Scope owns value
```

---

# Creating Scoped Value

```java
static final ScopedValue<String> USER =
        ScopedValue.newInstance();
```

No

```
set()

remove()

initialValue()
```

Only

```
where(...)
```

---

# Working Example

## Step 1

```java
import java.lang.ScopedValue;

public class RequestContext {

    static final ScopedValue<String> USER =
            ScopedValue.newInstance();

}
```

---

## Step 2

Service

```java
class Service {

    static void process() {

        System.out.println(
                "Current User : " +
                        RequestContext.USER.get());

    }

}
```

---

## Step 3

Main

```java
public class ScopedValueExample {

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        ScopedValue.where(
                RequestContext.USER,
                "Saurabh"
        ).run(() -> {

            Service.process();

        });

    }

}
```

Output

```
Current User : Saurabh
```

---

# Notice something

There is

No

```java
set()
```

No

```java
remove()
```

No

```java
finally
```

The value exists only inside

```java
where(...).run(...)
```

After run completes

```
Value disappears automatically.
```

---

# Nested Calls

```
main()

‚Üì

Controller()

‚Üì

Service()

‚Üì

DAO()
```

Controller

```java
ScopedValue.where(USER, "Saurabh")
        .run(Service::process);
```

Service

```java
static void process() {
    DAO.fetch();
}
```

DAO

```java
static void fetch() {
    System.out.println(USER.get());
}
```

Output

```
Saurabh
```

No parameter passing required.

---

# Nested Scope Example

```java
ScopedValue.where(USER, "Alice")
        .run(() -> {

            System.out.println(USER.get());

            ScopedValue.where(USER, "Bob")
                    .run(() -> {

                        System.out.println(USER.get());

                    });

            System.out.println(USER.get());

        });
```

Output

```
Alice
Bob
Alice
```

Notice

The old value is automatically restored.

---

# ThreadLocal equivalent

With ThreadLocal

```java
USER.set("Alice");

try {

    USER.set("Bob");

    ...

}
finally {

    USER.set("Alice");

}
```

Easy to make mistakes.

Scoped Value manages this automatically.

---

# Example with Structured Concurrency

This is where Scoped Values shine.

```java
import java.lang.ScopedValue;
import java.util.concurrent.StructuredTaskScope;

public class StructuredExample {

    static final ScopedValue<String> USER =
            ScopedValue.newInstance();

    public static void main(String[] args)
            throws Exception {

        ScopedValue.where(USER, "Saurabh")
                .run(() -> {

                    try (var scope =
                                 new StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure()) {

                        scope.fork(() -> {

                            System.out.println(
                                    "Profile : " +
                                            USER.get());

                            return null;
                        });

                        scope.fork(() -> {

                            System.out.println(
                                    "Orders : " +
                                            USER.get());

                            return null;
                        });

                        scope.join();

                    } catch (Exception e) {

                    }

                });

    }

}
```

Output

```
Profile : Saurabh
Orders : Saurabh
```

The Scoped Value is automatically available to child tasks created within the structured scope.

---

# Why Scoped Values are better

| Feature                          | ThreadLocal | Scoped Value                   |
| -------------------------------- | ----------- | ------------------------------ |
| Mutable                          | ‚úÖ Yes       | ‚ùå No                           |
| Automatic cleanup                | ‚ùå No        | ‚úÖ Yes                          |
| Memory leak risk                 | High        | None (scope-bound)             |
| Works with Virtual Threads       | Yes         | Yes (designed for them)        |
| Nested scopes                    | Manual      | Automatic                      |
| Restore previous value           | Manual      | Automatic                      |
| Thread pool problems             | Possible    | Avoided due to lexical scoping |
| Good for request context         | Good        | Excellent                      |
| Best with Structured Concurrency | No          | Yes                            |

---

# Interview Question

**Why not replace ThreadLocal completely?**

Because ThreadLocal is still useful when you need **mutable per-thread state**, such as caches or state that genuinely belongs to a long-lived thread.

Scoped Values are designed for **immutable context propagation**, such as:

* Authentication/User ID
* Correlation ID
* Request ID
* Tenant ID
* Locale
* Trace ID

---

# Interview Summary (30-second answer)

> **ThreadLocal** attaches mutable data to a thread. Developers must explicitly manage its lifecycle with `set()` and `remove()`, making it prone to leaks and stale data, especially with thread pools.
>
> **Scoped Values** (introduced in Java 21) attach immutable context to a lexical execution scope rather than to a thread. Values are bound using `ScopedValue.where(...).run(...)`, automatically cleaned up when the scope exits, support nested scopes naturally, and integrate well with virtual threads and structured concurrency. They are the preferred choice for propagating read-only request context in modern Java applications.

-----------------------------

## Next Phase

**Phase 5 ‚Äì Records** shifts from concurrency to language features. We'll cover:

* Records vs Classes vs Lombok
* Canonical and compact constructors
* JVM implementation
* Serialization behavior
* Performance characteristics
* Real-world DTO and event design
* Common architect-level interview questions
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

generalNotes.title: Structured Concurrency
generalNotes.text:
Excellent. This is one of the most important topics after Virtual Threads.

> **Structured Concurrency** is not about making your code faster. It's about making **concurrent code easier to reason about, safer, and more maintainable**.

Many senior interviewers ask this because it demonstrates whether you understand **modern concurrency design**, not just APIs.

> **Status:** Structured Concurrency is a **Preview Feature in Java 21** (JEP 453). It requires `--enable-preview` to use.

---

# Phase 3 ‚Äì Structured Concurrency (Java 21)

# Learning Objectives

After this phase you should be able to answer:

* Why was Structured Concurrency introduced?
* What problem does it solve?
* Why is `ExecutorService` not sufficient?
* What is a StructuredTaskScope?
* How does cancellation work?
* What are ShutdownOnFailure and ShutdownOnSuccess?
* How is it different from CompletableFuture?
* Production use cases
* Architecture implications

---

# 1. The Problem

Let's build a **Customer Dashboard**.

The UI requires:

```
Customer Profile

Customer Orders

Customer Wallet

Customer Recommendations

Customer Coupons
```

These are independent services.

Traditional implementation:

```java
CustomerProfile profile = profileService.get();

Orders orders = orderService.get();

Wallet wallet = walletService.get();

Recommendations recommendations = recommendationService.get();
```

Everything executes sequentially.

Total time:

```
Profile          200 ms

Orders           300 ms

Wallet           250 ms

Recommendation   400 ms

------------------------

Total

‚âà1150 ms
```

Very slow.

---

# 2. First Improvement

Run everything concurrently.

```
Profile ------------200ms

Orders ----------------300ms

Wallet -------------250ms

Recommendation ----------------400ms

Total

‚âà400 ms
```

Huge improvement.

---

# 3. Traditional ExecutorService

```java
ExecutorService executor =
        Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);

Future<Profile> profile =
        executor.submit(profileService::get);

Future<Order> orders =
        executor.submit(orderService::get);

Future<Wallet> wallet =
        executor.submit(walletService::get);
```

Looks good.

But there are several problems.

---

# 4. Problem 1 ‚Äì Failure

Suppose

```
Profile

SUCCESS

Orders

FAILED

Wallet

Still Running

Recommendation

Still Running
```

Question:

Should Wallet continue?

Usually

No.

Because the dashboard cannot be built correctly.

ExecutorService does not automatically cancel related tasks.

---

# 5. Problem 2 ‚Äì Resource Leak

Suppose

```
Profile

Done

Orders

Done

Wallet

Still Running

User Closed Browser
```

Wallet continues consuming:

* CPU
* Database connections
* Network
* Memory

Nobody needs the result anymore.

---

# 6. Problem 3 ‚Äì Cancellation

Suppose

```
Parent Request

‚Üì

Create 10 Threads

‚Üì

Parent Fails
```

What happens?

The child threads continue running unless you manually cancel them.

This violates a key principle:

> Child tasks should not outlive their parent.

---

# 7. Structured Concurrency Idea

Instead of

```
Parent

‚Üì

Random Threads

‚Üì

Random Lifetime
```

We get

```
Parent Scope

‚îÇ

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Task A

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Task B

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Task C

‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Task D

```

Every child belongs to exactly one parent scope.

When the parent finishes:

All children finish.

No exceptions.

---

# 8. Family Analogy

Imagine a family trip.

Parent

‚Üì

Children

If the parent goes home,

Children also go home.

Children don't continue sightseeing for three hours.

ExecutorService behaves like:

Parent leaves.

Children keep wandering.

Structured Concurrency behaves like:

Everyone stays together.

---

# 9. StructuredTaskScope

Java introduces

```java
StructuredTaskScope
```

It manages

* task lifecycle
* cancellation
* waiting
* failure propagation

---

# 10. Example

```java
try (var scope =
        new StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure()) {

    Supplier<Profile> profile =
            scope.fork(profileService::get);

    Supplier<Orders> orders =
            scope.fork(orderService::get);

    Supplier<Wallet> wallet =
            scope.fork(walletService::get);

    scope.join();

    scope.throwIfFailed();

    return new Dashboard(
            profile.get(),
            orders.get(),
            wallet.get());
}
```

Notice:

No manual Future handling.

No manual cancellation.

No executor shutdown.

Everything belongs to one scope.

---

# 11. Scope Lifecycle

```
Create Scope

‚Üì

Fork Tasks

‚Üì

Run Concurrently

‚Üì

Join

‚Üì

Success?

‚Üì

Yes ‚Üí Return Result

‚Üì

No ‚Üí Cancel Remaining Tasks

‚Üì

Scope Ends

‚Üì

All Threads End
```

This predictable lifecycle is the essence of structured concurrency.

---

# 12. ShutdownOnFailure

Suppose

```
Profile

SUCCESS

Orders

FAILED

Wallet

Running

Coupons

Running
```

Behavior

```
Orders Failed

‚Üì

Wallet Cancelled

‚Üì

Coupons Cancelled

‚Üì

Exception Returned
```

No wasted work.

---

# 13. ShutdownOnSuccess

Another scenario.

Searching inventory.

```
Warehouse A

Warehouse B

Warehouse C

Warehouse D
```

You only need the first successful result.

```
Warehouse A

SUCCESS

‚Üì

Cancel B

‚Üì

Cancel C

‚Üì

Cancel D
```

Perfect use case.

---

# 14. Comparison

ExecutorService

```
Task A

Task B

Task C

Independent
```

Structured Concurrency

```
Parent Scope

‚îÇ

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Task A

‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Task B

‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Task C

```

Children are tied to the parent.

---

# 15. Cancellation Flow

Traditional

```
Parent Failed

‚Üì

Children Continue
```

Structured

```
Parent Failed

‚Üì

Cancel Children

‚Üì

Release Resources
```

---

# 16. Production Example

Order Summary API

One request needs

```
Customer

‚Üì

Order

‚Üì

Payment

‚Üì

Shipment

‚Üì

Coupon

‚Üì

Recommendation
```

All services are independent.

Without Structured Concurrency

Lots of

* Future
* try/catch
* timeout
* cancellation
* executor shutdown

With Structured Concurrency

One scope.

Cleaner code.

---

# 17. Another Example

Travel Booking

```
Flight

Hotel

Taxi

Insurance
```

If Flight fails,

Should Hotel continue?

Usually no.

Structured Concurrency

Automatically cancels.

---

# 18. Relationship with Virtual Threads

Perfect combination.

```
Structured Scope

‚Üì

Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Carrier Threads
```

Every task executes as a Virtual Thread.

Much easier than

```
Executor

‚Üì

Thread Pool

‚Üì

Future

‚Üì

Callbacks

‚Üì

Cancellation
```

---

# 19. CompletableFuture Comparison

CompletableFuture

```java
CompletableFuture
        .allOf(...)
```

Problems

* Complex chaining
* Exception handling
* Manual cancellation
* Hard debugging

Structured Concurrency

```
Fork

‚Üì

Join

‚Üì

Done
```

Linear code.

Much easier.

---

# 20. Exception Handling

Traditional

```
Task A

Task B

Task C

‚Üì

Several Exceptions
```

Hard to understand.

Structured

```
Task Failed

‚Üì

Scope Failed

‚Üì

Everything Cancelled
```

Much cleaner.

---

# 21. Timeout Handling

Example

```
Profile

100 ms

Orders

5 sec

Wallet

80 ms
```

Timeout

```
1 second
```

Behavior

```
Orders Cancelled

‚Üì

Scope Ends

‚Üì

Return Timeout
```

No lingering tasks.

---

# 22. Internal Architecture

```
StructuredTaskScope

        ‚îÇ

Fork()

        ‚îÇ

Virtual Thread

        ‚îÇ

Virtual Thread

        ‚îÇ

Virtual Thread

        ‚îÇ

Join()

        ‚îÇ

Wait

        ‚îÇ

Success?

       / \

     Yes  No

      ‚îÇ    ‚îÇ

 Return  Cancel Others
```

---

# 23. Benefits

* Easier reasoning
* Automatic cancellation
* Better error propagation
* Resource cleanup
* Better readability
* Simpler debugging
* Parent-child lifecycle
* Safer concurrency

---

# 24. Limitations

It is **not** suitable for:

* Fire-and-forget tasks
* Long-running background jobs
* Kafka consumers that run indefinitely
* Scheduler services
* Daemons

Those tasks intentionally outlive the initiating request.

---

# 25. Interview Questions

### Basic

1. What is Structured Concurrency?
2. Why was it introduced?
3. What problem does it solve?

### Intermediate

4. What is StructuredTaskScope?
5. Explain ShutdownOnFailure.
6. Explain ShutdownOnSuccess.
7. How does cancellation work?

### Advanced

8. Why is ExecutorService considered unstructured?
9. Why is CompletableFuture harder to debug?
10. Why does Structured Concurrency improve reliability?
11. Would you use Structured Concurrency in Spring Boot?
12. Which production scenarios benefit most from it?

---

# Architecture Interview Scenario

**Question:**

Design a high-performance **Customer Dashboard API**.

Requirements:

* Fetch Profile
* Fetch Orders
* Fetch Wallet
* Fetch Recommendations
* Fetch Coupons

All services are independent.

**Strong Answer:**

* Use **Virtual Threads** so each downstream call gets its own lightweight thread.
* Wrap them in a **StructuredTaskScope** to ensure they share a common lifecycle.
* Use **ShutdownOnFailure** so that if any critical dependency fails, remaining tasks are cancelled immediately.
* Apply request-level deadlines so slow tasks are cancelled when the overall SLA is exceeded.
* This approach provides simple imperative code, automatic cleanup, and better resource utilization than manually coordinating `ExecutorService` or deeply nested `CompletableFuture` chains.

---

# Key Takeaways

* **Virtual Threads** solve the scalability problem of blocking I/O.
* **Structured Concurrency** solves the lifecycle management problem of concurrent tasks.
* Virtual Threads answer **"How do we run many concurrent tasks efficiently?"**
* Structured Concurrency answers **"How do we manage those concurrent tasks safely as one logical unit?"**

These two features are designed to complement each other and represent the biggest shift in Java's concurrency model since the introduction of the `java.util.concurrent` package.

---

## Next Phase (Phase 4)

We'll cover **Scoped Values**, another Project Loom feature that complements Virtual Threads and Structured Concurrency by providing a safer, immutable alternative to `ThreadLocal` for passing request context across concurrent operations. This topic is frequently discussed alongside the previous two in senior Java 21 interviews.

generalNotes.title: Virtual Threads
generalNotes.text:
Excellent choice. **Virtual Threads** are the single most important Java 21 topic for Staff Engineer and Architect interviews. Interviewers increasingly expect more than just API knowledge‚Äîthey want to understand the runtime internals, performance characteristics, and architectural implications.

# Phase 2 ‚Äì Virtual Threads (Project Loom)

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this phase, you should be able to answer:

* Why were Virtual Threads introduced?
* What problem do they solve?
* How are they implemented inside the JVM?
* What is a Continuation?
* What are Carrier Threads?
* What are Mount/Unmount operations?
* What is Pinning?
* When should and shouldn't Virtual Threads be used?
* How do they compare with Platform Threads, CompletableFuture, and Reactive programming?

---

# 1. Why Did We Need Virtual Threads?

Imagine a typical request in a microservice:

```text
Client
   ‚îÇ
   ‚ñº
Order Service
   ‚îÇ
   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Inventory API
   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Payment API
   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Database
   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Kafka
```

Each request performs multiple blocking operations:

* Database queries
* HTTP calls
* Kafka communication
* Redis access

### Traditional Model

```
1 Request
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
1 Platform Thread
```

If the database takes **500 ms**, the platform thread waits for the entire duration.

During that wait, the CPU is often idle while the thread still consumes OS resources.

---

# 2. The Problem with Platform Threads

Each Java thread traditionally maps to one operating system thread.

```
Java Thread
      ‚îÇ
      ‚ñº
Operating System Thread
```

This **1:1 mapping** means:

* Every thread has an OS stack.
* Context switching is handled by the operating system.
* Creating many threads consumes significant memory.
* Excessive context switching hurts performance.

Example:

10,000 requests ‚Üí 10,000 OS threads

This is impractical for most systems.

---

# 3. Platform Thread Architecture

```text
                JVM

Platform Thread

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Operating System Thread

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

CPU
```

The JVM delegates scheduling to the operating system.

---

# 4. Memory Cost

Typical platform thread stack:

```
1 MB
```

For 10,000 threads:

```
10,000 √ó 1 MB

‚âà 10 GB memory
```

Even if each thread is idle, the memory is reserved.

---

# 5. The Real Problem

Suppose a thread executes:

```java
orderRepository.findById(id);
```

Execution timeline:

```
CPU executes query
‚Üì

Database processing

‚Üì

Waiting...

‚Üì

Waiting...

‚Üì

Waiting...

‚Üì

Database returns

‚Üì

CPU resumes
```

The CPU spends very little time doing useful work, yet the thread remains allocated.

This inefficiency is the motivation behind Project Loom.

---

# 6. What Is a Virtual Thread?

A Virtual Thread is:

> A lightweight thread managed primarily by the JVM rather than directly by the operating system.

Instead of:

```
1 Java Thread

‚Üì

1 OS Thread
```

We get:

```
10,000 Virtual Threads

‚Üì

50 Carrier Threads

‚Üì

Operating System Threads
```

This dramatically reduces resource usage.

---

# 7. Virtual Thread Architecture

```text
                JVM

  Virtual Thread

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Continuation

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Carrier Thread

        ‚îÇ

        ‚ñº

Operating System Thread
```

The key innovation is that the JVM schedules Virtual Threads onto a smaller pool of Carrier Threads.

---

# 8. Carrier Threads

Carrier Threads are ordinary platform threads.

Example:

```
100,000 Virtual Threads

‚Üì

32 Carrier Threads
```

If a Virtual Thread blocks on I/O, the Carrier Thread is released to execute another Virtual Thread.

This keeps CPUs busy.

---

# 9. Continuation

A Continuation represents the execution state of a Virtual Thread.

It contains:

* Current stack frames
* Local variables
* Program counter
* Execution state

When a Virtual Thread blocks:

```
Save Continuation

‚Üì

Remove from Carrier Thread

‚Üì

Carrier Thread becomes free
```

When the blocking operation completes:

```
Continuation restored

‚Üì

Virtual Thread resumes

‚Üì

Carrier Thread assigned
```

---

# 10. Mount and Unmount

### Mount

```
Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Carrier Thread

‚Üì

Running
```

The Virtual Thread is attached to a Carrier Thread.

### Unmount

```
Blocking call

‚Üì

Save Continuation

‚Üì

Detach Carrier Thread
```

The Carrier Thread is immediately available for other Virtual Threads.

---

# 11. Example

```java
try (var executor = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()) {

    for (int i = 0; i < 100000; i++) {
        executor.submit(() -> {
            Thread.sleep(1000);
            return null;
        });
    }
}
```

Although 100,000 tasks are created, only a relatively small number of Carrier Threads are needed because sleeping Virtual Threads unmount and do not occupy OS threads.

---

# 12. API

Creating a Virtual Thread:

```java
Thread.startVirtualThread(() -> {
    System.out.println("Hello");
});
```

Executor-based approach:

```java
try (ExecutorService executor =
         Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()) {

    executor.submit(() -> processOrder());
}
```

This executor is the preferred approach for request-per-task workloads.

---

# 13. Scheduling

Platform Threads:

```
OS Scheduler
```

Virtual Threads:

```
JVM Scheduler

‚Üì

ForkJoinPool (Carrier Threads)
```

The JVM decides which Virtual Thread runs on which Carrier Thread.

---

# 14. Lifecycle

```
Created

‚Üì

Mounted

‚Üì

Running

‚Üì

Blocking

‚Üì

Continuation Saved

‚Üì

Unmounted

‚Üì

Waiting

‚Üì

Mounted Again

‚Üì

Running

‚Üì

Completed
```

---

# 15. Performance Comparison

| Metric            | Platform Thread    | Virtual Thread                                     |
| ----------------- | ------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Creation Cost     | High               | Very Low                                           |
| Memory            | ~1 MB stack        | Much smaller, grows as needed                      |
| Scheduling        | Operating System   | JVM                                                |
| Context Switching | OS                 | Mostly JVM                                         |
| Scalability       | Thousands          | Hundreds of thousands or more (workload dependent) |
| Blocking I/O      | Occupies OS thread | Releases Carrier Thread                            |

---

# 16. Pinning (Very Important)

Pinning occurs when a Virtual Thread **cannot unmount** from its Carrier Thread.

Example:

```java
synchronized(lock) {
    Thread.sleep(5000);
}
```

The `sleep()` call occurs while holding a monitor.

Result:

```
Carrier Thread

‚Üì

Blocked

‚Üì

Cannot execute other Virtual Threads
```

This reduces scalability.

### Better Alternative

```java
ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();

lock.lock();

try {
    Thread.sleep(5000);
} finally {
    lock.unlock();
}
```

`ReentrantLock` is generally more Virtual Thread‚Äìfriendly because it cooperates better with Loom's scheduling model.

---

# 17. When Virtual Threads Shine

Ideal for I/O-bound workloads:

* REST APIs
* Database access
* Kafka consumers/producers
* Redis calls
* File I/O
* HTTP clients
* gRPC services

---

# 18. When Not to Use Them

Less beneficial for:

* CPU-intensive algorithms
* Video encoding
* Image processing
* Machine learning inference
* Cryptographic computations

If threads spend nearly all their time using the CPU rather than waiting on I/O, Virtual Threads provide little advantage.

---

# 19. Virtual Threads vs Platform Threads

| Feature     | Platform Thread  | Virtual Thread                            |
| ----------- | ---------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Managed By  | Operating System | JVM                                       |
| Memory      | Higher           | Lower                                     |
| Scheduling  | OS               | JVM                                       |
| Blocking    | Blocks OS thread | Unmounts on supported blocking operations |
| Scalability | Limited          | Much Higher                               |

---

# 20. Virtual Threads vs CompletableFuture

| Virtual Threads              | CompletableFuture                     |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| Sequential programming style | Callback/composition style            |
| Easier to read               | Can become complex                    |
| Blocking code is acceptable  | Designed for non-blocking composition |
| Simpler debugging            | Stack traces can be fragmented        |

They solve different problems and can even be combined.

---

# 21. Virtual Threads vs Reactive Programming

| Virtual Threads                     | Reactive                          |
| ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Thread-per-request model            | Event-loop model                  |
| Imperative code                     | Functional/reactive style         |
| Easier migration from existing code | Requires reactive APIs end-to-end |
| Uses blocking APIs efficiently      | Avoids blocking entirely          |

---

# 22. Common Interview Questions

### Basic

1. What are Virtual Threads?
2. Why were they introduced?
3. What problem do they solve?

### Intermediate

4. What is a Carrier Thread?
5. What is a Continuation?
6. What is Mount/Unmount?
7. How does scheduling work?

### Advanced

8. Explain Pinning.
9. Why is `synchronized` problematic with Virtual Threads?
10. Why can `ReentrantLock` be a better choice?
11. Why don't Virtual Threads improve CPU-bound workloads?
12. How would you migrate a Spring Boot application from Platform Threads to Virtual Threads?

---

# Architect's Perspective

As an architect, don't present Virtual Threads as a replacement for all concurrency models. Instead, position them appropriately:

* **Use Virtual Threads** for request-per-task, I/O-heavy services where simplicity and scalability are important.
* **Continue using asynchronous/reactive approaches** when integrating with fully reactive stacks or when you need advanced stream processing and backpressure.
* **Optimize CPU-bound work** with appropriate thread pools and parallelism rather than expecting Virtual Threads to improve raw computation.

This balanced view demonstrates an understanding of trade-offs, which is what senior interviewers look for.

---

This is one of the **most important Java 21 interview topics**. The reason lies in **how Virtual Threads are scheduled**.

---
# Q. 9. Why is `synchronized` problematic with Virtual Threads?
# Q. 10. Why can `ReentrantLock` be a better choice?

# First Understand How Virtual Threads Work

Virtual Threads are **not OS threads**.

```
Thousands of Virtual Threads
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
+------------------------+
| ForkJoinPool Scheduler |
+------------------------+
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Few Platform Threads
(OS Threads)
```

Example

```
100,000 Virtual Threads

        ‚Üì

Only 8 Platform Threads
```

A Virtual Thread only uses a Platform Thread **while it is actually running**.

When it blocks on I/O

```
Virtual Thread

‚Üì

HTTP Call

‚Üì

Scheduler detaches it

‚Üì

Platform Thread becomes free

‚Üì

Runs another Virtual Thread
```

This is called

> **Unmounting**

This is why Virtual Threads scale so well.

---

# The Problem with synchronized

Now imagine

```java
synchronized(lock) {
    Thread.sleep(Duration.ofSeconds(5));
}
```

Looks harmless.

But internally

```
Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Enter synchronized

‚Üì

Owns Monitor

‚Üì

Sleep
```

During sleep

**Can JVM unmount it?**

No.

The Virtual Thread becomes

```
Pinned
```

---

# What is Pinning?

Pinning means

```
Virtual Thread
        ‚îÇ
        ‚ñº
Platform Thread

cannot separate
```

Normally

```
Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Waiting

‚Üì

Unmount

‚Üì

Platform Thread free
```

Pinned

```
Virtual Thread

‚Üì

Waiting

‚Üì

Platform Thread blocked too
```

The carrier thread is stuck.

---

# Example

Suppose

```
8 Platform Threads
```

Now

```
8 Virtual Threads

‚Üì

Enter synchronized

‚Üì

Sleep(30 sec)
```

Result

```
Platform Thread 1 blocked
Platform Thread 2 blocked
Platform Thread 3 blocked
...
Platform Thread 8 blocked
```

Now

```
100,000 Virtual Threads

‚Üì

No Platform Thread available
```

Everything slows dramatically.

---

# Why does synchronized pin?

Because synchronized uses an **Object Monitor**.

```
Monitor

‚Üì

OS level lock
```

The JVM currently cannot safely detach a Virtual Thread while it owns a monitor.

Therefore

```
Virtual Thread
must stay
on
Platform Thread
```

---

# Visual

Normal Virtual Thread

```
VT1
 |
 | HTTP
 |
Unmount

Carrier Thread free

‚Üì

Runs VT2
```

With synchronized

```
VT1

‚Üì

Owns Monitor

‚Üì

Sleep

‚Üì

Carrier Thread cannot leave

‚Üì

Pinned
```

---

# Demonstration

```java
Object lock = new Object();

Thread.startVirtualThread(() -> {

    synchronized (lock) {

        try {
            Thread.sleep(Duration.ofSeconds(10));
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
        }

    }

});
```

Even though

```
sleep()
```

is Virtual Thread friendly,

the synchronized monitor prevents unmounting.

---

# Enter ReentrantLock

Instead use

