Architect Interview Questions

Here are questions frequently asked in senior interviews:

1. Why is prepare() required?

It guarantees that every participant has:

Executed its local work.
Written a durable PREPARED record.
Locked the necessary resources.
Promised it can either commit or roll back later.
2. Why does the Transaction Manager write its own log before sending commit()?

If the TM crashes after telling one database to commit but before telling another, the durable decision log allows it to recover and resend the commit to the remaining participants. Without that log, different databases could permanently disagree on the transaction outcome.

3. Why are both participant logs and coordinator logs needed?
Participant log: remembers that the participant voted YES (PREPARED) and must await the coordinator's decision.
Coordinator log: remembers the global decision (COMMIT or ROLLBACK) so it can complete the transaction after a crash.
4. Why is XA considered slow?

Because it incurs:

Two network phases (prepare and commit).
Durable log writes on both coordinator and participants.
Long-lived locks held between phases.
Recovery bookkeeping and coordination.

These costs are why modern cloud-native microservices typically favor Saga patterns over XA, unless strong ACID guarantees across multiple resources are an absolute requirement.

------------------------------------------------------------------
These are excellent questions. You're thinking like an Architect now‚Äîyou're trying to understand **where the transaction boundary actually is**.

Let's answer each point carefully.

---

# 1. If transaction happens across 2 databases, can Atomikos handle it automatically?

**Answer: Yes, but with some important conditions.**

Suppose your application looks like this:

```text
                 Spring Boot Application
                         |
                  Atomikos (TM)
                   /          \
                  /            \
         XA MySQL              XA PostgreSQL
         Order DB              Payment DB
```

Application code:

```java
@Transactional
public void placeOrder() {

    orderRepository.save(order);

    paymentRepository.save(payment);
}
```

If all of the following are true:

* ‚úÖ Both databases are XA-capable.
* ‚úÖ Both are configured as `XADataSource`.
* ‚úÖ Spring uses `JtaTransactionManager`.
* ‚úÖ Atomikos (or Narayana) is configured as the transaction manager.

Then Atomikos automatically performs:

```text
begin()

‚Üì

create XID

‚Üì

MySQL.start()

‚Üì

Postgres.start()

‚Üì

SQL

‚Üì

prepare(MySQL)

‚Üì

prepare(Postgres)

‚Üì

commit(MySQL)

‚Üì

commit(Postgres)
```

You don't write any `prepare()` or `commit()` calls yourself.

### Does it matter if one is MySQL and the other PostgreSQL?

**No.**

As long as both JDBC drivers support XA.

For example:

| Database   | XA Support                  |
| ---------- | --------------------------- |
| MySQL      | ‚úÖ Yes (`MysqlXADataSource`) |
| PostgreSQL | ‚úÖ Yes (`PGXADataSource`)    |
| Oracle     | ‚úÖ Yes                       |
| SQL Server | ‚úÖ Yes                       |
| DB2        | ‚úÖ Yes                       |

The Transaction Manager only talks to the `XAResource` interface.

It doesn't care which database is behind it.

Think of it like this:

```text
          Atomikos

              |

---------------------------------

XAResource

‚Üì

MySQL

---------------------------------

XAResource

‚Üì

Postgres
```

The interface is the same.

---

# 2. Does this fit only in a monolith?

Mostly **yes**, and this is where many developers get confused.

## Monolith

Imagine:

```text
Spring Boot

‚Üì

OrderRepository

‚Üì

MySQL

‚Üì

PaymentRepository

‚Üì

Postgres
```

Everything is inside **one JVM**.

One Transaction Manager.

One `@Transactional`.

Perfect use case.

---

## Microservice

Now imagine:

```text
Order Service

‚Üì

HTTP

‚Üì

Payment Service

‚Üì

Payment DB
```

Now there are **two JVMs**.

Order Service:

```text
JVM #1
```

Payment Service:

```text
JVM #2
```

Each service has its own Spring container.

Each service has its own Transaction Manager.

Each service has its own DataSource.

Question:

Can Order Service directly call

```java
paymentRepository.save();
```

No.

Instead it calls

```java
restTemplate.postForObject(...)
```

or

```java
WebClient
```

or

```java
FeignClient
```

Now the transaction has crossed a network boundary.

---

This is why `@Transactional` **does not automatically cover the remote service**.

---

# Can XA work across microservices?

Technically:

**Yes.**

Practically:

**Almost never.**

People often say:

> "XA doesn't work with microservices."

That's not completely accurate.

It **can** work.

But every microservice must participate in the same global transaction.

For example

```text
Order Service
     |
Atomikos
     |
HTTP
     |
Payment Service
     |
Atomikos
     |
Payment DB
```

Now you have problems.

---

### Problem 1

Payment Service cannot commit until Order Service finishes.

So Payment DB keeps rows locked.

---

### Problem 2

If Payment Service crashes

Order waits.

---

### Problem 3

If network is slow

Everything waits.

---

### Problem 4

Scaling becomes difficult.

Imagine

```text
Order

‚Üì

Payment

‚Üì

Inventory

‚Üì

Shipping

‚Üì

Notification

‚Üì

Invoice
```

One slow service blocks everyone.

That's why almost every cloud architecture today uses **Saga** instead of XA.

---

# 3. How can Spring Boot + Atomikos be implemented in microservices?

This is the interesting part.

## Option A (Theoretical)

Suppose

```text
Order Service

‚Üì

Atomikos

‚Üì

Payment Service

‚Üì

Atomikos
```

People think this will work.

It won't.

Why?

Because:

```text
Order Service

Atomikos-A

Payment Service

Atomikos-B
```

These are **two different Transaction Managers**.

Each manages only its own process.

They don't automatically know about each other.

---

## Option B (What would actually be required)

You need a **distributed transaction coordinator**.

Something like

```text
                    Coordinator
                        |
        ----------------------------------
        |                                |
 Order Service                    Payment Service
        |                                |
    XAResource                      XAResource
```

Now the flow becomes

```text
Coordinator

‚Üì

start global XID

‚Üì

Order joins

‚Üì

Payment joins

‚Üì

prepare Order

‚Üì

prepare Payment

‚Üì

commit Order

‚Üì

commit Payment
```

This is possible.

But now:

* Every service must expose XA endpoints.
* Every service must propagate the XID.
* Every service must keep DB locks.
* Every service must support recovery.

Almost nobody does this today.

---

# What is actually used?

Modern architecture looks like this:

```text
Client

‚Üì

Order Service

‚Üì

Order DB (COMMIT)

‚Üì

Publish Event

‚Üì

Kafka

‚Üì

Payment Service

‚Üì

Payment DB (COMMIT)

‚Üì

Publish Event

‚Üì

Inventory Service
```

Every service has its own local transaction.

No distributed locks.

If Payment fails

```text
Order

‚Üì

Cancel Order
```

This is the Saga pattern.

---

# If someone insists on XA across microservices

You would need something like:

```text
                Narayana Coordinator

                     |

--------------------------------------------

Order Service

‚Üì

XAResource

‚Üì

MySQL

--------------------------------------------

Payment Service

‚Üì

XAResource

‚Üì

Oracle
```

Each service would:

* Receive an XID.
* Join the global transaction.
* Delay commit.
* Wait for the coordinator.
* Recover using `recover()`.

This requires protocols for transaction propagation (such as CORBA OTS in the past, proprietary mechanisms, or specialized middleware). It is complex, tightly coupled, and not aligned with modern HTTP/REST microservice designs.

---

# Architect's Summary

| Scenario                                            | Use XA/Atomikos?                                                                         | Recommended?                             |
| --------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| One Spring Boot application + 2 databases           | ‚úÖ Yes                                                                                    | ‚úÖ Yes, if strong consistency is required |
| One Spring Boot application + MySQL + PostgreSQL    | ‚úÖ Yes                                                                                    | ‚úÖ Supported if both use XA drivers       |
| Multiple microservices calling each other over REST | ‚öÝÔ∏è Technically possible only with significant infrastructure and transaction propagation | ‚ùå Generally not recommended              |
| Modern microservices with Kafka/events              | ‚ùå Use Saga instead                                                                       | ‚úÖ Industry standard                      |

---

## I can also show you a **complete working Spring Boot 3 + Atomikos project** that uses:

* **2 databases (MySQL + PostgreSQL)**
* **Spring Data JPA**
* **`@Transactional`**
* **Atomikos**
* Real configuration (`XADataSource`, `JtaTransactionManager`)
* Failure scenarios to demonstrate automatic rollback

This is a realistic, production-style example and closely reflects what you'd see in enterprise applications.
---------------------------------------------------------------
Absolutely. Since you're preparing for **Architect interviews**, let's build a **real production-style Spring Boot 3 + Atomikos + MySQL + PostgreSQL** application.

> **Goal:** One Spring Boot application updates two different databases in a single XA (2PC) transaction.

I'll also explain **what Spring Boot is doing internally** at every step.

---

# Project Structure

```text
springboot-xa-demo
‚îÇ
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ config
‚îÇ      OrderDbConfig.java
‚îÇ      PaymentDbConfig.java
‚îÇ
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ order
‚îÇ      Order.java
‚îÇ      OrderRepository.java
‚îÇ
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ payment
‚îÇ      Payment.java
‚îÇ      PaymentRepository.java
‚îÇ
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ service
‚îÇ      CheckoutService.java
‚îÇ
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ SpringbootXaApplication.java
‚îÇ
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ application.yml
```

---

# Step 1: Dependencies

```xml
<dependencies>

    <!-- Spring Boot -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-jpa</artifactId>
    </dependency>

    <!-- Atomikos -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>com.atomikos</groupId>
        <artifactId>transactions-spring-boot3-starter</artifactId>
        <version>6.0.0</version>
    </dependency>

    <!-- MySQL -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>com.mysql</groupId>
        <artifactId>mysql-connector-j</artifactId>
    </dependency>

    <!-- PostgreSQL -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.postgresql</groupId>
        <artifactId>postgresql</artifactId>
    </dependency>

</dependencies>
```

---

# Step 2: application.yml

```yaml
spring:

  jta:
    enabled: true

atomikos:

  properties:
    log-base-dir: ./atomikos-log
    max-timeout: 300000
    default-jta-timeout: 300000
```

Notice:

```
atomikos-log
```

This directory contains the **Transaction Recovery Log**.

Example:

```
atomikos-log

|

+-- tmlog.0

+-- tmlog.1

+-- checkpoint
```

If the application crashes,

Atomikos reads these files during restart.

---

# Step 3: Configure MySQL XA

```java
@Configuration
@EnableJpaRepositories(
        basePackages = "com.demo.order",
        entityManagerFactoryRef = "orderEntityManager",
        transactionManagerRef = "transactionManager")
public class OrderDbConfig {

    @Bean
    public DataSource orderDataSource() {

        MysqlXADataSource xa = new MysqlXADataSource();

        xa.setUrl(
          "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/orderdb");

        xa.setUser("root");
        xa.setPassword("root");

        AtomikosDataSourceBean ds =
            new AtomikosDataSourceBean();

        ds.setUniqueResourceName("orderDB");
        ds.setXaDataSource(xa);

        return ds;
    }

}
```

Notice

```
MysqlXADataSource
```

NOT

```
HikariDataSource
```

---

# Step 4: Configure PostgreSQL XA

```java
@Configuration
@EnableJpaRepositories(
        basePackages = "com.demo.payment",
        entityManagerFactoryRef = "paymentEntityManager",
        transactionManagerRef = "transactionManager")
public class PaymentDbConfig {

    @Bean
    public DataSource paymentDataSource() {

        PGXADataSource xa =
             new PGXADataSource();

        xa.setUrl(
          "jdbc:postgresql://localhost/paymentdb");

        xa.setUser("postgres");

        xa.setPassword("postgres");

        AtomikosDataSourceBean ds =
              new AtomikosDataSourceBean();

        ds.setUniqueResourceName("paymentDB");

        ds.setXaDataSource(xa);

        return ds;
    }
}
```

Notice

```
Unique Resource Name
```

Each participant must have a unique name.

---

# Step 5: Entity Managers

You need two `EntityManagerFactory` beans‚Äîone for each persistence unit.

Example (MySQL):

```java
@Bean
public LocalContainerEntityManagerFactoryBean orderEntityManager(
        EntityManagerFactoryBuilder builder,
        @Qualifier("orderDataSource") DataSource dataSource) {

    return builder
            .dataSource(dataSource)
            .packages(Order.class)
            .persistenceUnit("orderPU")
            .build();
}
```

Similarly, create another for the payment database using `Payment.class` and a different persistence unit name.

---

# Step 6: Transaction Manager

```java
@Bean
public PlatformTransactionManager transactionManager() {

    return new JtaTransactionManager();
}
```

Notice

NO

```java
DataSourceTransactionManager
```

Only

```
JtaTransactionManager
```

Internally

```
Spring

‚Üì

JtaTransactionManager

‚Üì

Atomikos

‚Üì

2PC
```

---

# Step 7: Repository

Nothing special.

```java
public interface OrderRepository
extends JpaRepository<Order,Long>{
}
```

Payment

```java
public interface PaymentRepository
extends JpaRepository<Payment,Long>{
}
```

---

# Step 8: Business Code

```java
@Service
public class CheckoutService {

    @Autowired
    OrderRepository orderRepository;

    @Autowired
    PaymentRepository paymentRepository;

    @Transactional
    public void checkout() {

        orderRepository.save(
            new Order(1L,"Laptop"));

        paymentRepository.save(
            new Payment(1L,85000));

    }
}
```

Notice

This code is identical to a normal Spring Boot application.

---

# What happens internally?

Application executes

```java
checkout();
```

Spring intercepts.

```
@Transactional

‚Üì

JtaTransactionManager.begin()
```

Atomikos creates

```
Global XID
```

Example

```
Global ID

A23456789

‚Üì

MySQL Branch

‚Üì

Postgres Branch
```

---

## MySQL receives

```
XA START
```

Internally

```
XA START

‚Üì

BEGIN TRANSACTION
```

---

## PostgreSQL

```
XA START

‚Üì

BEGIN
```

---

## Hibernate executes

```
INSERT ORDER
```

No commit.

---

Then

```
INSERT PAYMENT
```

No commit.

Current state

```
MySQL

Transaction Open

----------------

Postgres

Transaction Open
```

---

# Method completes

Spring executes

```
commit()
```

Atomikos now performs Phase 1.

```
prepare(MySQL)

‚Üì

prepare(Postgres)
```

---

## MySQL

Internally executes

```
Flush redo log

‚Üì

Write PREPARED

‚Üì

Lock rows

‚Üì

XA_OK
```

---

Postgres

```
PREPARED

‚Üì

XA_OK
```

---

# Atomikos writes

```
Transaction Log

TX100

Decision

COMMIT
```

This is written to disk before any resource is told to commit.

---

# Phase 2

```
commit(MySQL)

‚Üì

commit(Postgres)
```

Locks released.

Transaction complete.

---

# Failure Example

Suppose

```java
@Transactional
public void checkout(){

    orderRepository.save(order);

    paymentRepository.save(payment);

    throw new RuntimeException();
}
```

Flow

```
MySQL

INSERT

‚Üì

OPEN

---------------

Postgres

INSERT

‚Üì

OPEN

---------------

Exception

‚Üì

rollback(MySQL)

‚Üì

rollback(Postgres)
```

No data committed anywhere.

---

# Simulating a Crash

Suppose

```
prepare(MySQL)

‚Üì

YES

prepare(Postgres)

‚Üì

YES

‚Üì

Atomikos writes COMMIT

‚Üì

MySQL committed

‚Üì

Application crashes
```

What happens?

Restart.

Atomikos scans

```
atomikos-log
```

Finds

```
TX100

Decision

COMMIT
```

It then asks PostgreSQL:

```
recover()
```

PostgreSQL replies

```
TX100

PREPARED
```

Atomikos executes

```
commit(TX100)
```

Recovery completes automatically.

---

# Real Database Commands

When XA is enabled, the JDBC driver translates the protocol into database-specific XA commands.

For MySQL, conceptually it is similar to:

```sql
XA START 'TX100';

INSERT INTO orders VALUES (...);

XA END 'TX100';

XA PREPARE 'TX100';

XA COMMIT 'TX100';
```

For PostgreSQL, the driver maps the XA calls to PostgreSQL's prepared transaction mechanism (`PREPARE TRANSACTION`, `COMMIT PREPARED`, etc.). The application never executes these SQL statements directly.

---

# Log Output You May See

With DEBUG logging enabled for Atomikos and transaction packages, you'll see logs similar to:

```text
Creating global transaction

XID = 313233343536

Enlisting resource orderDB

Enlisting resource paymentDB

XAResource.start()

XAResource.end()

XAResource.prepare(orderDB)

XAResource.prepare(paymentDB)

Writing COMMIT decision

XAResource.commit(orderDB)

XAResource.commit(paymentDB)

Transaction completed
```

---

# Production Notes

This example demonstrates the mechanics of XA and 2PC, but there are a few things you'd typically add in a production application:

* Connection pooling and proper XA-aware datasource configuration.
* Health checks for all XA resources.
* Transaction timeout settings.
* Recovery configuration and monitoring of the Atomikos transaction logs.
* Careful sizing of connection pools because prepared transactions hold connections and locks longer than local transactions.

---

## My Recommendation for Your Architect Preparation

The next step I'd suggest is **not just another code example**, but tracing **one transaction from beginning to end**.

We can follow a single `@Transactional` call and inspect:

1. The exact Spring AOP interceptor call stack.
2. `JtaTransactionManager` internals.
3. How Hibernate enlists an `XAResource`.
4. The `XAResource.start()`, `end()`, `prepare()`, `commit()` calls.
5. The actual SQL sent to MySQL and PostgreSQL.
6. The contents of the Atomikos recovery log.
7. Crash recovery after restarting the application.

This "packet-level" walkthrough is the level of understanding expected in senior architect interviews and makes the entire XA lifecycle much easier to visualize.

# Decomposition Patterns in Microservices ‚Äì Deep Dive

Microservice decomposition is the process of breaking a large monolithic application into smaller, independently deployable services.

The biggest challenge in microservices is **deciding service boundaries**. Poor decomposition creates distributed monoliths, excessive network calls, and operational complexity.

---

# Why Decomposition Matters

A bad decomposition can lead to:

* Tight coupling between services
* Too many synchronous API calls
* Data consistency issues
* Difficult deployments
* Performance bottlenecks

A good decomposition provides:

* Independent deployment
* Team autonomy
* Better scalability
* Fault isolation
* Easier maintenance

---

# 1. Decompose by Business Capability (Recommended)

This is the most common and preferred approach.

A business capability represents something the business does.

### E-commerce Example

Instead of:

```
Monolith
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Product Module
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ User Module
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Order Module
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Payment Module
```

Create services:

```
Product Service
Customer Service
Order Service
Payment Service
Inventory Service
Shipping Service
```

### Responsibilities

| Service   | Responsibility     |
| --------- | ------------------ |
| Product   | Product catalog    |
| Customer  | Customer profile   |
| Order     | Order lifecycle    |
| Payment   | Payment processing |
| Inventory | Stock management   |
| Shipping  | Delivery           |

### Benefits

* Matches business structure
* Easier ownership
* Clear boundaries
* Scales well

### Real Companies

Many domains at [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com) and [Netflix](https://www.netflix.com?utm_source=chatgpt.com) are organized around business capabilities.

---

# 2. Decompose by Domain (DDD - Domain Driven Design)

DDD introduces:

### Domain

Entire business area.

Example:

```
Telecom OSS
```

### Subdomains

```
Inventory
Provisioning
Monitoring
Billing
Fault Management
```

### Bounded Context

Each subdomain becomes a bounded context.

```
Inventory Context
Provisioning Context
Billing Context
```

Then create microservices around these contexts.

---

## Telecom Example

Since you work in telecom/network management systems:

```
Network Inventory Service
Alarm Service
Performance Service
Topology Service
Provisioning Service
Configuration Service
```

Each service owns:

* Data
* APIs
* Business rules

No direct database sharing.

---

# 3. Decompose by Subdomain

DDD classifies domains into:

### Core Domain

Competitive advantage.

Example:

```
Network Analytics
AI Fault Prediction
```

### Supporting Domain

Needed but not differentiating.

Example:

```
User Management
Reporting
```

### Generic Domain

Commodity functionality.

Example:

```
Authentication
Notification
Email
```

---

Example:

```
Core:
  Analytics Service
  Correlation Service

Supporting:
  Reporting Service

Generic:
  Notification Service
  Auth Service
```

Invest most effort in Core Domain services.

---

# 4. Strangler Fig Pattern

Used when migrating a monolith.

Instead of rewriting everything:

```
Monolith
```

Gradually becomes:

```
API Gateway
    |
    +--> New Order Service
    +--> New Customer Service
    |
    +--> Remaining Monolith
```

Over time:

```
Monolith -> Shrinks -> Removed
```

### Advantages

* Low risk
* Incremental migration
* Production-safe

Very popular for legacy modernization.

---

# 5. Decompose by Transaction Boundary

Identify transactions that must remain consistent.

Example:

### Order Placement

```
Create Order
Reserve Inventory
Process Payment
```

Question:

Should these be one service?

Not necessarily.

Instead:

```
Order Service
Inventory Service
Payment Service
```

Coordinate using:

* Events
* Saga Pattern

---

Rule:

Services should own their own transaction boundary.

---

# 6. Decompose by Data Ownership

Each service owns its database.

Bad:

```
Order Service
Customer Service
Inventory Service

     |
 Shared Database
```

Good:

```
Order Service
   |
 Order DB

Customer Service
   |
 Customer DB

Inventory Service
   |
 Inventory DB
```

### Principle

"Database per Service"

Benefits:

* Loose coupling
* Independent scaling
* Independent schema evolution

---

# 7. Decompose by Team Structure (Conway's Law)

Conway's Law:

> Systems tend to mirror communication structures of organizations.

Example:

```
Team A -> Order Service
Team B -> Customer Service
Team C -> Inventory Service
```

If teams are independent, services should be too.

---

# 8. Event-Driven Decomposition

Instead of request-response:

```
Order Service
      |
 OrderCreated Event
      |
-------------------------
|          |           |
Inventory  Payment  Shipping
```

Advantages:

* Loose coupling
* Better scalability
* Asynchronous processing

Typical technologies:

* Apache Kafka
* RabbitMQ
* Apache Pulsar

---

# 9. Decompose by Volatility

Ask:

"What changes together?"

Example:

```
Pricing Rules
Discount Rules
Promotion Rules
```

Frequently change together.

Keep them in:

```
Pricing Service
```

Do NOT create:

```
Discount Service
Promotion Service
Pricing Service
```

That causes excessive coupling.

---

# 10. Decompose by Read/Write Patterns (CQRS)

Example:

Monitoring Platform

Write:

```
Collect Metrics
Store Metrics
```

Read:

```
Dashboards
Reports
Analytics
```

Separate:

```
Metric Ingestion Service
Query Service
Analytics Service
```

This scales much better for high-volume telemetry systems.

---

# Practical Example: Telecom NMS/EMS System

Suppose your current monolith contains:

```
Device Discovery
Inventory
Alarm Handling
Performance Metrics
Configuration
User Management
Reports
```

A reasonable decomposition:

```
Discovery Service
Inventory Service
Alarm Service
Performance Service
Configuration Service
User Service
Reporting Service
Notification Service
```

### Event Flow

```
Device Down

Alarm Service
    |
AlarmRaised Event
    |
--------------------------
|            |           |
Notification Reporting Analytics
```

This minimizes direct service-to-service dependencies.

---

# Common Decomposition Mistakes

### 1. CRUD Services

Bad:

```
Customer CRUD Service
Address CRUD Service
Phone CRUD Service
```

Too granular.

---

### 2. Shared Database

Bad:

```
10 Services
     |
 One DB
```

Creates hidden coupling.

---

### 3. Nano Services

Bad:

```
Order Validation Service
Order Tax Service
Order Discount Service
Order Status Service
```

Too many services.

---

### 4. Chatty Services

Bad:

```
Order Service
   |
10 API Calls
   |
Customer Service
```

Network latency accumulates.

---

### 5. Distributed Monolith

Looks like microservices but:

* Synchronous dependencies everywhere
* Shared DB
* Coordinated deployments

This is worse than a monolith.

---

# Interview Perspective (Architect/Staff Engineer)

When asked **"How would you decompose a monolith into microservices?"**, a strong answer is:

1. Understand business capabilities.
2. Identify DDD bounded contexts.
3. Define service ownership and data ownership.
4. Create database-per-service.
5. Define APIs/events between services.
6. Use Strangler Pattern for migration.
7. Use Saga/Event-Driven patterns for cross-service workflows.
8. Monitor coupling and cohesion continuously.

A good rule of thumb:

> **Start with business capabilities and bounded contexts. Refine using data ownership, transaction boundaries, and team ownership.** This produces service boundaries that remain stable as the system grows.
generalNotes.title: Decomposition Patterns in Microservices ‚Äì Deep Dive

quickNotes.title: Isolation Levels
quickNotes.text:
Isolation Levels

Know these well.

Isolation	Prevents
Read Uncommitted	Almost nothing
Read Committed	Dirty reads
Repeatable Read	Non-repeatable reads
Serializable	Phantom reads + full isolation

Most Spring Boot applications use Read Committed.

For inventory allocation, explicit row locking is usually preferred over switching the entire transaction to Serializable.

Considering **your profile**:

* **14+ years experience**
* **Staff Engineer ‚Üí Architect target**
* Strong Java/Spring Boot background
* Already following a structured Java 21 and System Design roadmap
* Interviewing for **Product companies** (not fresher campus placements)

I would **not** choose the same resource that I would recommend to a 2‚Äì5 year engineer.

Let's analyze all three deeply.

---

# Option 1

Example

Instead of just saying

Reverse Linked List

it explains

## Advantages

# Option 2

## Striver SDE Sheet

[Striver SDE Sheet](https://takeuforward.org/interviews/strivers-sde-sheet-top-coding-interview-problems/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

This is one of the most popular interview-focused sheets in India. It contains **191 curated problems** across arrays, trees, graphs, DP, heaps, recursion, and more, with a strong emphasis on common interview patterns. ([takeuforward][1])

---

## Structure

Starts from

Arrays

‚Üì

Hashing

* Multiple solutions

If you don't already know DSA,

# Question Quality Comparison

| Criteria          | Karumanchi | Striver | LC 150 |
| ----------------- | ---------- | ------- | ------ |
| Theory            | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê     | ‚≠ê      |
| Java              | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   |
| Pattern Learning  | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   |
| Interview Focus   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê        | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê  |
| Modern Questions  | ‚≠ê          | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê  |
| FAANG             | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê  |
| Product Companies | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê        | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê  |
| Revision          | ‚≠ê‚≠ê         | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   | ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê   |

---

# Question Analysis

## Striver

Approximately

* Easy ‚Üí 25
* Medium ‚Üí 93
* Hard ‚Üí 73 ([takeuforward][1])

Many Hard questions are there to expose advanced patterns rather than because they're commonly asked verbatim.

---

Patterns repeated intentionally

Example

Sliding Window

* Longest Substring
* Minimum Window
* Fruits Basket

This repetition builds recognition.

---

## LC Top Interview 150

Roughly

* Easy ‚Üí 35%
* Medium ‚Üí 50%
* Hard ‚Üí 15%

Much less intimidating.

More interview realistic.

Questions are

"Original classics."

---

# Which one is better for YOU?

This is where experience matters.

You **do not** have six months to become a competitive programmer.

You need to become **interview efficient**.

## If you choose ONLY Karumanchi

You'll know DSA.

But your interview speed won't improve enough.

‚ùå Not recommended alone.

---

## If you choose ONLY Striver

You'll become very good at interview patterns.

But you'll spend time on some questions that have diminishing returns for a senior candidate.

---

## If you choose ONLY LC Top 150

You'll solve interview questions.

But you'll sometimes miss the underlying pattern unless you already have strong theoretical foundations.

---

# My Recommendation (Best ROI)

I would combine them strategically rather than follow one resource end-to-end.

### Phase 1 (2‚Äì3 weeks)

Use **Karumanchi** only for theory:

* Arrays
* Linked Lists
* Stack
* Queue
* Trees
* Graphs
* Heap
* Hashing

Read each chapter to understand the data structure, complexity, and implementations. Do **not** solve every exercise.

### Phase 2 (8‚Äì10 weeks)

Make **LeetCode Top Interview 150** your primary practice list.

Reasons:

* Very little redundant practice.
* Excellent editorials.
* Questions map closely to senior product-company interviews.
* Easier to maintain consistent progress.

### Phase 3 (3‚Äì4 weeks)

Fill gaps using **Striver SDE Sheet**:

* Advanced Graphs
* Tries
* Union-Find (Disjoint Set)
* Monotonic Stack
* Binary Search on Answer
* Advanced Dynamic Programming
* Any topic where you felt weak after LC 150.

---

# Final Recommendation for Your Architect Interview Journey

If I were designing a one-year preparation plan specifically for **your** goal (Senior Staff/Architect in a product company), I would allocate:

* **10%** ‚Äî Karumanchi (build conceptual depth).
* **70%** ‚Äî LeetCode Top Interview 150 (core interview practice).
* **20%** ‚Äî Selected Striver problems to cover missing patterns.

This approach minimizes duplicate effort while maximizing interview readiness. It also complements the Java 21 and System Design roadmaps we've already been building.

In short, **don't try to finish all three resources**. Use each for what it does best:

* **Karumanchi** for understanding,
* **LeetCode Top Interview 150** for mastering interview questions,
* **Striver** as a targeted supplement for advanced patterns and any weak areas.

[1]: https://takeuforward.org/interviews/strivers-sde-sheet-top-coding-interview-problems/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Strivers SDE Sheet - Top Coding Interview Problems | takeUforward"
[2]: https://leetcode.com/discuss/post/2084075/strivers-sde-sheet-list-leetcode-link-by-8slz/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Striver's SDE Sheet List Leetcode Link - Discuss - LeetCode"

skills.name: Java 21 Features
skills.notes:
Since you're targeting **Architect/Staff Engineer** roles with **14+ years of experience**, I would not prepare Java 21 as "what's new in Java 21." Interviewers at companies like Amazon, Microsoft, Oracle, Uber, Atlassian, Cisco, Walmart, Goldman Sachs, SAP, Adobe, and similar product companies expect you to know:

* Why a feature was introduced.
* What problem it solves.
* Internal implementation.
* Performance implications.
* Production use cases.
* Trade-offs.
* Comparisons with older approaches.

I recommend covering Java 21 in the following roadmap.

---

# Java 21 Interview Roadmap

## Phase 1 ‚Äî Java Evolution

Before Java 21

* Java 8
* Java 9 Module System
* Java 10 var
* Java 11 LTS
* Java 14 Switch Expressions
* Java 15 Text Blocks
* Java 16 Records
* Java 17 LTS
* Java 19 Virtual Threads Preview
* Java 20 Updates
* Java 21 LTS

Interview Questions

* Why did Java evolve?
* Why is Java 21 considered a major release?
* Which features became permanent?
* Which preview features are worth knowing?

---

# Phase 2 ‚Äî Virtual Threads (Most Important)

This is probably the #1 Java 21 interview topic.

Topics

* Platform Threads
* OS Thread Mapping
* Thread Scheduling
* Virtual Threads
* Continuations
* Carrier Threads
* Mount/Unmount
* Parking
* Pinning
* Structured Concurrency relation
* Thread Local behavior
* ExecutorService changes

Deep Dive

* JVM implementation
* Stack storage
* Heap stack
* Scheduler
* ForkJoinPool
* Blocking behavior
* Synchronization impact
* Native calls

Production Example

Order Service

‚Üì

Inventory

‚Üì

Payment

‚Üì

Shipping

‚Üì

Notification

100,000 concurrent requests using Virtual Threads.

Interview Questions

* Why virtual threads?
* Difference from platform threads?
* When NOT to use virtual threads?
* What is pinning?
* Why synchronized can reduce scalability?

---

# Phase 3 ‚Äî Structured Concurrency (Preview)

Topics

* Parent-child tasks
* Cancellation
* Failure propagation
* Scope lifecycle
* Shutdown on failure
* Shutdown on success

Production Example

Customer Dashboard

‚Üì

Profile

Orders

Wallet

Recommendations

All executed together.

---

# Phase 4 ‚Äî Scoped Values (Preview)

Topics

* Alternative to ThreadLocal
* Immutable Context
* Performance
* Context Propagation

Compare

ThreadLocal

vs

ScopedValue

Interview Questions

* Why ThreadLocal fails with Virtual Threads?
* Why ScopedValue is immutable?

---

# Phase 5 ‚Äî Record Classes

Deep Dive

* Record Components
* Canonical Constructor
* Compact Constructor
* Serialization
* Reflection
* equals()
* hashCode()
* toString()

Compare

POJO

Lombok

Record

Immutable Class

Production Example

Kafka Event

REST DTO

API Response

Interview Questions

* Can Record extend class?
* Can Record be mutable?
* Why final?
* Record vs Lombok?

---

# Phase 6 ‚Äî Pattern Matching

Topics

instanceof

Switch Pattern Matching

Record Pattern

Guarded Pattern

Nested Pattern

Null Matching

Production Example

Message Processing

Interview Questions

* Pattern matching advantages?
* Compiler optimization?
* Exhaustiveness?

---

# Phase 7 ‚Äî Sealed Classes

Topics

permits

final

sealed

non-sealed

Inheritance restrictions

Production Example

Payment

Card

UPI

Wallet

Net Banking

Interview Questions

* Difference from abstract class?
* Why sealed?

---

# Phase 8 ‚Äî Switch Expressions

Topics

yield

Arrow syntax

Exhaustiveness

Pattern matching integration

Null handling

Production Example

Protocol Decoder

---

# Phase 9 ‚Äî Collections Improvements

Topics

List.of()

Set.of()

Map.of()

copyOf()

Collectors.toUnmodifiableList()

Sequenced Collections (Java 21)

LinkedHashMap changes

LinkedHashSet changes

Interview Questions

* Immutable collection vs Collections.unmodifiableList()

---

# Phase 10 ‚Äî String Improvements

Topics

Text Blocks

String Templates (Preview)

formatted()

strip()

repeat()

lines()

indent()

translateEscapes()

---

# Phase 11 ‚Äî Foreign Function & Memory API (Preview)

Topics

JNI problems

Native memory

MemorySegment

Arena

Linker

FunctionDescriptor

Production Example

Calling C library

---

# Phase 12 ‚Äî Garbage Collectors

Topics

G1

ZGC

Shenandoah

Serial

Parallel

When to use each

Java 21 improvements

Interview Questions

* Which GC for microservices?
* Which for low latency?

---

# Phase 13 ‚Äî JVM Improvements

Topics

Class Data Sharing

CDS

AppCDS

Startup improvements

Memory optimizations

JIT

C1/C2

Tiered Compilation

---

# Phase 14 ‚Äî Performance

Topics

Benchmarking

JMH

Virtual Thread performance

Record performance

Pattern Matching optimization

Escape Analysis

Inlining

---

# Phase 15 ‚Äî Java 21 APIs

New APIs

Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()

Thread.startVirtualThread()

SequencedCollection

SequencedMap

SequencedSet

StructuredTaskScope

ScopedValue

---

# Phase 16 ‚Äî Migration to Java 21

Topics

Migration strategy

Java 8 ‚Üí 21

Compatibility

Deprecated APIs

Removed APIs

Framework support

Spring Boot compatibility

Hibernate

Kafka

---

# Phase 17 ‚Äî Production Architecture

Design systems using Java 21

REST APIs

Kafka Consumers

Kafka Producers

Thread-per-request

Reactive vs Virtual Threads

Database

Redis

Microservices

---

# Phase 18 ‚Äî Coding Questions

Implement

Producer Consumer

Rate Limiter

Parallel API Calls

Virtual Thread Executor

Pattern Matching examples

Record DTOs

Structured Concurrency

---

# Phase 19 ‚Äî Architecture Discussion

When to use

Reactive

Virtual Threads

Async

CompletableFuture

Project Reactor

Netty

Spring MVC

Spring WebFlux

---

# Phase 20 ‚Äî Frequently Asked Interview Questions

Around 100+ questions such as:

* Why Java 21 LTS?
* Why Virtual Threads instead of Reactor?
* Does Virtual Thread replace CompletableFuture?
* Why ThreadLocal is problematic?
* What is Carrier Thread?
* Explain Pinning.
* What is Continuation?
* Record vs Class?
* Record vs Lombok?
* Pattern Matching internals?
* Switch optimization?
* Scoped Values?
* Structured Concurrency?
* Foreign Memory API?
* Sequenced Collections?
* G1 vs ZGC?
* Migration strategy from Java 8 to Java 21?
* Java 21 production best practices?

---

# Recommended Learning Order (Interview-Optimized)

Given your goal of moving into an Architect role, I'd prioritize the topics like this:

| Priority | Topic                         | Interview Importance |
| -------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------- |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | Virtual Threads               | Critical             |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | Structured Concurrency        | Critical             |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | Records                       | Critical             |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | Pattern Matching              | Critical             |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê    | Sealed Classes                | Critical             |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê     | Scoped Values                 | High                 |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê     | Switch Expressions            | High                 |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê     | Sequenced Collections         | High                 |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê     | GC and JVM Improvements       | High                 |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê     | Performance Tuning            | High                 |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      | Foreign Function & Memory API | Medium               |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      | String Templates (Preview)    | Medium               |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      | Migration to Java 21          | Medium               |
| ‚≠ê‚≠ê‚≠ê      | API Enhancements              | Medium               |

### Suggested Deep-Dive Approach

Given the structured way we've already covered topics like distributed systems, design patterns, and Saga orchestration, I suggest using the same format for Java 21. For each phase, we can cover:

1. Why the feature was introduced.
2. The problem it solves.
3. Internal JVM implementation.
4. API syntax and examples.
5. Production use cases (especially in microservices and high-throughput systems).
6. Performance characteristics and trade-offs.
7. Common interview questions (basic to advanced).
8. Hands-on coding examples.
9. Architecture scenarios where the feature is a good (or poor) fit.

This approach will prepare you for both senior coding interviews and system design discussions where Java 21 features influence architectural decisions.

tasks.title: Complete 3 more Tutorials Dojo practice tests ‚Äî target 80%+
tasks.detail: Review every wrong answer. Focus on scenario-based architecture trade-offs

tasks.title: Reach out to 2 senior engineers at Flipkart / Razorpay / Atlassian
tasks.detail: LinkedIn DM. Ask about Staff architect interview process ‚Äî not job openings

weeks.title: AWS SAA exam + blog post #3
weeks.goal: Pass the exam. Publish lessons-from-migration post.

tasks.title: Take and pass AWS SAA-C03 exam
tasks.detail: You're ready if scoring 80%+ on practice tests. Trust the prep.

tasks.title: Write blog post #3: 'Lessons from migrating a monolith to microservices'
tasks.detail: Be honest about failures. Engineers trust posts that share what went wrong.

sixMonthPlan.title: Leadership footprint
sixMonthPlan.focus: Stop being the best engineer in the room. Start being the one who changes how the room works.
sixMonthPlan.milestone: Cross-team design guild running. RFC adopted outside your team. Open-source contribution merged.

weeks.title: Launch design guild
weeks.goal: Propose and run first cross-team design session

tasks.title: Draft 1-page proposal for a bi-weekly architecture design guild
tasks.detail: Name, purpose, format (60 min), how to submit designs, who it's open to

tasks.title: Send Slack outreach to 5‚Äì8 engineers across 2+ teams
tasks.detail: Voluntary, peer-to-peer. Goal: 4+ confirmations for session 1

tasks.title: Run session 1: present TJ5600 design, invite challenge
tasks.detail: Prepare a Miro board or 15 slides. Ask: 'What failure modes am I missing?'

weeks.title: Write cross-team RFC
weeks.goal: Draft and share RFC that spans 2+ teams

tasks.title: Pick RFC topic that affects 2+ teams
tasks.detail: Good candidates: Kafka topic naming policy, circuit breaker config standards, schema registry adoption

tasks.title: Write full RFC using Google design doc format (5 sections)
tasks.detail: Context ‚Üí Goals/Non-goals ‚Üí Proposal ‚Üí Alternatives ‚Üí Risks. Target 2‚Äì4 pages

tasks.title: Share with 3 reviewers from different teams ‚Äî 10-day review window
tasks.detail: Ask specific questions to drive comments. Update doc with decisions made.

weeks.title: Open-source contribution
weeks.goal: Submit first contribution to Debezium or Spring

tasks.title: Browse Debezium / Spring Boot issues for 'good first issue' labels
tasks.detail: Spend 1 hour browsing. Pick one documentation or small bug issue to claim.

tasks.title: Submit PR or detailed bug report with minimal repro case
tasks.detail: Fork, fix, test locally, open PR with clear description + issue reference

weeks.title: Blog post #4 + resume rewrite
weeks.goal: Publish back-of-envelope post, rewrite resume

tasks.title: Write blog post #4: 'Back-of-envelope estimation ‚Äî how I think about scale'
tasks.detail: Use TJ5600 as worked example. Traffic ‚Üí storage ‚Üí bandwidth estimates with real numbers

tasks.title: Rewrite every resume bullet in architect framing
tasks.detail: Decision made + team/org scope + business outcome. Kill all 'implemented X' bullets.

sixMonthPlan.title: Interview readiness
sixMonthPlan.focus: You're not preparing anymore ‚Äî you're ready. This month is about polish, portfolio, and converting pipeline to offers.
sixMonthPlan.milestone: Architecture portfolio live. 3 mock system design interviews done. 5 target companies applied to.

weeks.title: Architecture portfolio
weeks.goal: Build public portfolio of technical work

tasks.title: Build architecture portfolio on GitHub Pages or Notion
tasks.detail: Include: TJ5600 case study, CDC write-up, ADRs, all 5 blog posts, RFC summary

tasks.title: Record a 10-minute Loom walkthrough of your TJ5600 design
tasks.detail: Explain: the problem, your decisions, the trade-offs, what you'd change.

weeks.title: Mock interviews √ó3
weeks.goal: Complete system design + DSA + behavioural mocks

tasks.title: Complete 3 mock system design interviews on Pramp or with peers
tasks.detail: Record them. Review and identify vocabulary gaps after each one

tasks.title: Complete 3 mock DSA interviews on LeetCode Mock / Pramp
tasks.detail: Timed ‚Äî 45 min each. Narrate your approach as you code

tasks.title: Complete 2 mock behavioural interviews with a peer
tasks.detail: Prepare: influence without authority, technical disagreement, failure story, cross-team collaboration

weeks.title: STAR stories + applications
weeks.goal: Prepare 8 STAR stories, apply to 5 companies

tasks.title: Prepare 8 STAR stories: influence, failure, decision, trade-off, conflict, hiring, strategy, mentoring
tasks.detail: Each story should demonstrate architect-level scope. Write them down ‚Äî don't wing it

tasks.title: Apply to 5 target companies in Bengaluru via referral
tasks.detail: Atlassian, Razorpay, PhonePe, Adobe, Flipkart. Referrals get 3√ó more interviews than cold apps

weeks.title: Blog post #5 + final polish
weeks.goal: Capstone post live, all gaps addressed

tasks.title: Write blog post #5: 'How I think about distributed systems trade-offs'
tasks.detail: Your capstone. Synthesise everything from the past 6 months.

tasks.title: Fix any remaining interview weak spots identified from mocks
tasks.detail: Re-do practice problems in 2 weakest areas. Book 1 more mock if needed.
